6. **Episodic Knowledge:** Past event records, e.g., "In 2019, this drug caused allergy in Patient X."
7. **Common Sense Knowledge:** Everyday world knowledge, e.g., "Ice is cold."
8. **Volatile Knowledge:** Time-dependent facts, e.g., "Today's share price of Infosys."

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table

| Parameter | Production Rules | Semantic Networks | Frames |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|--------|
| **Structure** | IF-THEN rules | Graph of nodes & links | Slots and slot values |
| **Best For** | Heuristic, procedural knowledge | Conceptual relationships | Stereotyped objects/situations |
| **Inheritance** | No native inheritance | Yes, via `is-a` edges | Yes, via class-instance hierarchy |
| **Inference** | Forward / Backward chaining | Inheritance + marker passing | Inheritance + slot value matching |
| **Readability** | Very high (natural language-like) | High (graphical) | Moderate (structured records) |
| **Example System** | MYCIN, CLIPS | WordNet, Mind maps | KRL, KL-ONE |
| **Limitation** | Rule explosion in large systems | No standard semantics, ambiguous | Hard to update deeply nested frames |

**Inferences Supported:**
- Production Rules: **Forward Chaining** (data-driven) and **Backward Chaining** (goal-driven).
- Semantic Networks: **Inheritance Reasoning**, **Marker Passing**.
- Frames: **Default Inheritance**, **Slot-based reasoning**, **Exception handling via `if-needed` and `if-added` daemons**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Represent the following statements in First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL). Then use Modus Ponens and Universal Instantiation to derive the goal:*
- (1) John is a student.
- (2) All students are hardworking.
- (3) All hardworking people pass exams.
- (4) **Goal:** Does John pass exams?

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Construct a semantic network for the following: "A sparrow is a bird. A bird is an animal. A bird can fly. Tweety is a sparrow. Tweety is yellow." Use the network to derive two new facts via inheritance.*

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — FOPL Representation & Derivation

**Step 1 — Define Predicates and Constants:**

- Constant: $\text{John}$ → $j$
- Predicates: $\text{Student}(x)$, $\text{Hardworking}(x)$, $\text{Passes}(x)$

**Step 2 — Encode each premise:**

- (1) $\text{Student}(j)$
- (2) $\forall x \, \big( \text{Student}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Hardworking}(x) \big)$
- (3) $\forall x \, \big( \text{Hardworking}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Passes}(x) \big)$

**Step 3 — Negate the goal for refutation (or apply direct deduction):**

Goal: $\text{Passes}(j)$

**Step 4 — Universal Instantiation on (2) with $x = j$:**

$$
\text{Student}(j) \Rightarrow \text{Hardworking}(j)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Modus Ponens using fact (1):**

$$
\text{Hardworking}(j) \text{ derived.}
$$

**Step 6 — Universal Instantiation on (3) with $x = j$:**

$$
\text{Hardworking}(j) \Rightarrow \text{Passes}(j)
$$

**Step 7 — Apply Modus Ponens:**

$$
\therefore \; \text{Passes}(j) \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Conclusion:** Yes, John passes the exams.

#### Part (b) — Semantic Network Construction

**Nodes:** `Sparrow`, `Bird`, `Animal`, `Tweety`, `Fly`, `Yellow`

**Edges:**

- $\text{Sparrow} \xrightarrow{\text{is-a}} \text{Bird}$
- $\text{Bird} \xrightarrow{\text{is-a}} \text{Animal}$
- $\text{Bird} \xrightarrow{\text{can}} \text{Fly}$
- $\text{Tweety} \xrightarrow{\text{instance-of}} \text{Sparrow}$
- $\text{Tweety} \xrightarrow{\text{color}} \text{Yellow}$

**Inheritance-Based Inferences (Tweety inherits via Sparrow → Bird → Animal):**

1. **Tweety is an Animal** — Inherited via the chain `Tweety is-a Sparrow, Sparrow is-a Bird, Bird is-a Animal`. Therefore: $\text{Tweety} \xrightarrow{\text{is-a (inherited)}} \text{Animal}$.

2. **Tweety can Fly** — Inherited from the property of Bird via the `can` edge. Therefore: $\text{Tweety} \xrightarrow{\text{can (inherited)}} \text{Fly}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting Universal Instantiation before applying Modus Ponens** — Modus Ponens operates on ground instances only. (−2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing `instance-of` and `is-a` in semantic networks** — `is-a` connects classes; `instance-of` connects an object to its class. Mixing them up is a classic error. (−2 marks)
> 3. **Omitting the chain of inheritance reasoning** — Examiners demand *transitive* inference shown step-by-step, not just the final derived fact. (−1.5 marks)
> 4. **Negating the goal incorrectly during Resolution** — The goal is negated *first* and added to the KB, NOT the KB statements negated. (−2 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 PECMT522 Module 1**

- 🔹 **KR = encoding knowledge formally** for an expert system to reason with.
- 🔹 **Two main KR families:** *Symbolic* (logic, rules, frames) and *Sub-symbolic* (neural, NOT in KTU scope).
- 🔹 **Eight knowledge types:** Declarative, Procedural, Heuristic, Meta, Structural, Episodic, Common Sense, Volatile.
- 🔹 **Six KR schemes:** Propositional Logic, FOPL, Semantic Networks, Frames, Production Rules, Scripts & Conceptual Dependency.
- 🔹 **FOPL > PL** because FOPL adds variables, quantifiers ($\forall, \exists$), predicates, and functions.
- 🔹 **Semantic Networks** = Nodes + Edges. Key link: `is-a` (triggers inheritance).
- 🔹 **Frames** = Minsky's 1975 model. Slots + Values + Inheritance. Supports `if-needed` and `if-added` demons.
- 🔹 **Production Rules** = Most commercial ES scheme. Supports Forward and Backward Chaining.
- 🔹 **Modus Ponens**: From $P \rightarrow Q$ and $P$, conclude $Q$.
- 🔹 **Universal Instantiation (UI)**: From $\forall x \, P(x)$, infer $P(c)$ for any constant $c$.
- 🔹 **Resolution**: A single inference rule that, combined with CNF, makes automated theorem proving tractable.
- 🔹 **Properties of Good KR**: Representational adequacy, Inferential adequacy, Efficiency, Transparency, Naturalness.
- 🔹 **CD (Schank, 1973)**: 11 primitive ACTs; key ones = `PTRANS`, `ATRANS`, `MTRANS`, `MBUILD`, `INGEST`.
- 🔹 **Scripts** = Schank's extension of CD; represents stereotypical events (e.g., Restaurant Script).
- 🔹 **Quillian (1968)** pioneered Semantic Networks; **Minsky (1975)** introduced Frames; **Schank (1973)** introduced CD.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Knowledge Representations**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Productions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT522 - EXPERT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Productions # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of **Expert Systems** and classical Artificial Intelligence, a **Production** (or **Production Rule**) is a conditional statement of the form **"IF THEN "** that encodes a single piece of heuristic or domain knowledge. A collection of such rules, together with the machinery that interprets them, forms a **Production System**, which is the formal computational model upon which most rule-based expert systems (e.g., MYCIN, CLIPS, OPS5, Jess) are built. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECMT522 Module 1)** > Productions form the atomic knowledge representation unit in a rule-based expert system. Every production is a *modular*, *independent* chunk of knowledge, allowing the knowledge base to be incrementally updated without rewriting the entire program. A production has three parts: 1. **Antecedent (LHS – Left Hand Side / Condition Part):** A Boolean expression that tests the contents of *Working Memory* (WM). 2. **Conclusion (RHS – Right Hand Side / Action Part):** A sequence of operations (assert, retract, modify) that alters the Working Memory. 3. **Satisfiability Test:** The matching process that checks whether a rule's antecedent is satisfied by the current WM. $$ \text{Production } P_i : \text{IF } (\text{Condition}_1) \land (\text{Condition}_2) \land \dots \text{ THEN } (\text{Action}_1), (\text{Action}_2), \dots $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > In the KTU syllabus, "Productions" is treated both as a *single rule* and as a *complete system architecture* (the Production System model by Newell & Simon). Examiners expect students to clearly distinguish between the two usages. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine you are a **chef in a busy kitchen** following a stack of recipe cards. Each card has the form: > *"IF the pan is hot AND onions are in the pan THEN add garlic for 30 seconds."* - The **recipe cards** = Production Rules (long-term knowledge) - The **state of the stove, the pan, the ingredients on the counter** = Working Memory (current facts) - **You, the chef** = The Control System / Interpreter (the engine that picks the right card, checks the current state, and executes the step) Just as a chef continually scans the recipe deck to see which card's "IF" matches the current state of the kitchen, a production system runs a *recognize-act cycle* against its rules. > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid:** *Condition (LHS) → Match → Conflict Set → Resolve → Action (RHS).* Memorise this 5-word loop — it is the single most-tested concept from this topic. ## 1.3 Key Vocabulary (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology) | Term | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Production Rule** | A single IF-THEN knowledge unit | *IF fever = high THEN prescribe paracetamol* | | **Working Memory (WM)** | The dynamic database of currently known facts | `{patient has fever, temperature = 102}` | | **Production Memory (PM)** | The static collection of all rules | The rule base of MYCIN | | **Recognize-Act Cycle** | The interpreter's main loop | Match → Select → Execute | | **Conflict Set** | The set of all rules whose LHS is currently true | All rules that can fire *right now* | | **Conflict Resolution** | The strategy that picks one rule from the conflict set | First-match, specificity, recency | | **Firing** | The act of executing a rule's RHS | "This rule has fired" | | **Inference Engine** | The software that runs the cycle | The shell of an expert system | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Production System Architecture A production system, in the strict sense of the KTU Module-1 syllabus, has **three logically distinct components** (proposed by Newell & Simon in the 1970s as a cognitive model of human problem solving): ### 2.1.1 The Production Rule Base (Long-Term Memory) A finite, often large, set of condition-action rules. Each rule is *modular*: it does not call or reference any other rule directly. Communication between rules is **implicit**, mediated only through the Working Memory. > [!NOTE] > **Why modularity matters:** The knowledge engineer can add, delete, or modify a single rule without recompiling the system. This is the *defining feature* that makes rule-based systems easy to maintain — and is the #1 reason KTU examiners love this question. ### 2.1.2 The Working Memory (Short-Term / Dynamic Memory) A globally accessible, mutable data structure holding the **current problem state** as a set of *working memory elements* (WMEs). Each WME is a tagged tuple, e.g.: $$ \text{WME} = \langle \text{class}, \text{attribute}, \text{value} \rangle $$ Example: `(patient John) (has-symptom fever) (temperature 102)` — three WMEs in medical diagnosis. ### 2.1.3 The Interpreter (Control System / Inference Engine) The procedural engine that runs the **Recognize-Act Cycle** in a continuous loop until a *termination condition* (goal reached, no rules fire, or a halt action is executed) is met. ## 2.2 The Three Phases of the Recognize-Act Cycle The cycle has three well-defined phases; examiners frequently test the *order* and the *purpose* of each phase. ### Phase 1 — Match (Pattern Matching) The interpreter scans the entire production memory and identifies every rule whose **LHS is satisfied** by the current contents of the Working Memory. $$ \text{Match Phase} : \mathcal{R} \times \text{WM} \rightarrow \text{Conflict Set } \mathcal{C} $$ where $\mathcal{R}$ is the rule base and $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{R}$ is the set of *instantiated* rules that may legally fire. ### Phase 2 — Conflict Resolution (Select) When more than one rule is in the conflict set, the system must pick *exactly one* to execute. Common strategies (board-favourite): 1. **Refraction** — A rule that has already fired on the current data is barred from firing again on the same data. 2. **Recency** — Prefer rules that reference the most recently added WMEs. 3. **Specificity** — Prefer the rule with the most conditions (more specific = more informative). 4. **Priority / Salience** — Numeric preference given to rules at design time. 5. **First-found / LEX / MEA** — Various OPS5 ordering strategies. > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists **recency** and **specificity** as the two strategies students must be able to *define with an example*. Always quote both. ### Phase 3 — Act (Execute / Fire) The selected rule's RHS is executed. Common actions: - **MAKE / ASSERT** — Add a new fact to WM. - **REMOVE / RETRACT** — Delete an existing fact. - **MODIFY** — Change the value field of an existing WME. - **HALT** — Stop the system. - **I/O** — Print, query the user, or read a sensor. After execution, the cycle returns to Phase 1. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table consolidates the formal notations, conflict-resolution heuristics, and termination conditions that appear in KTU board questions. (Note: `\vert` and `\mid` are used in place of the vertical bar to keep the markdown table intact.) | # | Concept | Formal Expression | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Production rule | $P_i : \text{IF } C_i \text{ THEN } A_i$ | Atomic knowledge chunk | | 2 | Working Memory Element | $\text{WME} = \langle \text{class}, \text{attr}, \text{value} \rangle$ | Tuple store of current facts | | 3 | Match function | $\text{Match} : \mathcal{R} \times \text{WM} \to 2^{\mathcal{R}}$ | Builds the conflict set $\mathcal{C}$ | | 4 | Conflict set | $\mathcal{C} = \lbrace P_i \mid P_i.\text{LHS} \models \text{WM} \rbrace$ | Set of fireable rules | | 5 | Select function | $\text{Select} : \mathcal{C} \to P_k$ | Picks one rule from $\mathcal{C}$ | | 6 | Act function | $\text{Act} : P_k \times \text{WM} \to \text{WM}'$ | Updates WM | | 7 | Recency preference | $\text{prefer } P_i \text{ if } \max(t(\text{WME}_{P_i}))$ | Newest WME wins | | 8 | Specificity preference | $\text{prefer } P_i \text{ if } \vert C_i \vert > \vert C_j \vert$ | More conditions win | | 9 | Termination | $\mathcal{C} = \varnothing \;\;\text{or}\;\; \text{HALT action executed}$ | System stops | | 10 | Forward chaining trigger | New WME added to WM | Data-driven reasoning | | 11 | Backward chaining trigger | Goal stack pop | Goal-driven reasoning | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Production systems are not merely academic. They are deployed in: - **Industrial process control** (Siemens, Honeywell DCS) — rules like *"IF pressure > 5 bar AND temp < 200 °C THEN open relief valve."* - **Network intrusion detection** (Snort, Suricata) — every signature is essentially a production rule. - **Medical diagnosis** (MYCIN, INTERNIST) — classical KTU case study. - **Business rule engines** (Drools, Jess) — modern commercial successors of OPS5. - **Reactive robotics and embedded AI** — small-footprint rule engines for autonomous vehicles. > [!TIP] > When KTU asks *"Give two real-world applications of production systems,"* always cite one **industrial control** example and one **medical/business** example to show breadth. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section walks through an *exhaustive* worked demonstration of the production-system cycle on a small medical example, mirroring the kind of step-by-step solution that earns full marks in a 14-mark KTU question. ## 3.1 Problem Setup (Medical Diagnostic Example) **Working Memory (initial state) — WM₀** | WME ID | Class | Attribute | Value | |---|---|---|---| | W₁ | patient | name | Raj | | W₂ | patient | age | 35 | | W₃ | symptom | type | fever | | W₄ | reading | kind | temperature | | W₅ | reading | value | 102 | **Production Memory (Rule Base) — 𝓡** | Rule ID | LHS (Conditions) | RHS (Actions) | |---|---|---| | R₁ | W₃ = fever ∧ W₅ ≥ 100 | MAKE diagnosis = "viral-fever" | | R₂ | W₃ = fever ∧ W₅ ≥ 104 | MAKE diagnosis = "high-fever" | | R₃ | diagnosis = "high-fever" | MAKE prescription = "consult-doctor" | | R₄ | diagnosis = "viral-fever" | MAKE prescription = "paracetamol" | | R₅ | prescription = "paracetamol" | HALT | ## 3.2 Iteration 1 of the Recognize-Act Cycle ### Step 1 — MATCH Phase We test the LHS of every rule against the current WM (WM₀ = {W₁, W₂, W₃, W₄, W₅}): - **R₁** needs `symptom = fever` ✓ (W₃) **AND** `temperature ≥ 100` ✓ (W₅ = 102). → *Satisfied*. - **R₂** needs `symptom = fever` ✓ (W₃) **AND** `temperature ≥ 104` ✗ (W₅ = 102). → *Not satisfied*. - **R₃** needs `diagnosis = high-fever`. WM has no diagnosis WME. → *Not satisfied*. - **R₄** needs `diagnosis = viral-fever`. WM has no diagnosis WME. → *Not satisfied*. - **R₅** needs `prescription = paracetamol`. WM has no prescription WME. → *Not satisfied*. **Result of MATCH:** $$ \mathcal{C}_1 = \lbrace R_1 \rbrace $$ ### Step 2 — SELECT (Conflict Resolution) Phase The conflict set $\mathcal{C}_1$ contains only one rule, so the selection is trivial: $$ \text{SELECT}(\mathcal{C}_1) = R_1 $$ ### Step 3 — ACT (Execute) Phase We fire R₁. Its RHS is **MAKE diagnosis = viral-fever**. This adds a new WME to working memory: $$ W_6 : \langle \text{diagnosis}, \text{value}, \text{viral-fever} \rangle $$ The new working memory is: $$ \text{WM}_1 = \text{WM}_0 \cup \lbrace W_6 \rbrace = \lbrace W_1, W_2, W_3, W_4, W_5, W_6 \rbrace $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why refraction doesn't matter here:** Refraction prevents a rule from re-firing on the *same* data. R₁'s data is unchanged, but a *new* fact (W₆) was just produced. Whether R₁ will be re-considered in Cycle 2 depends on whether its LHS is still satisfied — which it is — but the typical OPS5-style interpreter would still allow re-matching; only **refraction** would block it. ## 3.3 Iteration 2 of the Recognize-Act Cycle ### Step 1 — MATCH Phase Re-evaluate all rules against WM₁: - **R₁** → Still satisfied, **but** a *refraction* check may bar it (it just fired on the *same* WMEs W₃, W₅). For this example, assume **refraction is OFF**; R₁ is in the conflict set. - **R₂** → Still not satisfied. - **R₃** → Needs `diagnosis = high-fever`; WM₁ has `diagnosis = viral-fever` → *Not satisfied*. - **R₄** → Needs `diagnosis = viral-fever` ✓ (W₆) → *Satisfied*. - **R₅** → Needs `prescription = paracetamol`; not in WM yet → *Not satisfied*. **Conflict set:** $$ \mathcal{C}_2 = \lbrace R_1, R_4 \rbrace $$ ### Step 2 — SELECT (Conflict Resolution) Phase Now we have a *genuine* conflict. Apply the resolution strategies in order: 1. **Refraction** (if enabled): R₁ is barred (it just fired on the same W₃, W₅). R₁ is removed. 2. **Specificity**: Both R₁ and R₄ have 2 conditions. Tied. 3. **Recency**: R₄ references W₆, which was added *most recently*. R₄ wins. $$ \text{SELECT}(\mathcal{C}_2) = R_4 $$ ### Step 3 — ACT (Execute) Phase Fire R₄. RHS: **MAKE prescription = paracetamol**. New WME: $$ W_7 : \langle \text{prescription}, \text{value}, \text{paracetamol} \rangle $$ $$ \text{WM}_2 = \text{WM}_1 \cup \lbrace W_7 \rbrace = \lbrace W_1, W_2, W_3, W_4, W_5, W_6, W_7 \rbrace $$ ## 3.4 Iteration 3 of the Recognize-Act Cycle ### Step 1 — MATCH Phase - **R₁** → Barred by refraction (if enabled) / or satisfied. - **R₄** → Barred by refraction (it just fired on W₆). - **R₅** → Needs `prescription = paracetamol` ✓ (W₇) → *Satisfied*. **Conflict set:** $$ \mathcal{C}_3 = \lbrace R_5 \rbrace $$ ### Step 2 — SELECT Phase $$ \text{SELECT}(\mathcal{C}_3) = R_5 $$ ### Step 3 — ACT Phase Fire R₅. RHS: **HALT**. The interpreter stops. $$ \text{Termination: } \text{HALT action executed} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{System stops after WM}_2. $$ ## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix | Cycle | WM (after cycle) | Conflict Set $\mathcal{C}$ | Selected Rule | Reason for Selection | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | {W₁..W₅, W₆} | {R₁} | R₁ | Only rule satisfied | | 2 | {W₁..W₅, W₆, W₇} | {R₁, R₄} | R₄ | Refraction + Recency (W₆) | | 3 | {W₁..W₅, W₆, W₇} | {R₅} | R₅ | Only rule satisfied (HALT) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board-tip:** When asked *"Trace the recognize-act cycle for the given set of rules and facts,"* the expected answer is **exactly this kind of table** — a clear WM column, a conflict-set column, a selected-rule column, and a one-line reason. Lose marks if any of the four columns are missing. ## 3.6 Analytical Derivation of Termination Guarantees A production system is *guaranteed* to halt in two cases (frequently asked as a 3-mark sub-question): **Case 1 — No rule fires.** If MATCH produces an empty conflict set: $$ \mathcal{C}_k = \varnothing $$ then the interpreter exits with **"no applicable rule"** status. **Case 2 — Explicit HALT action.** Any rule's RHS may contain the `HALT` action, which terminates the cycle immediately after execution. **Non-termination (Undesirable):** The cycle can theoretically run forever if a rule keeps adding facts that keep re-activating the same rule. In practice, this is prevented by **refraction** plus an optional **iteration cap**. > [!TIP] > A common 3-mark KTU question: *"Why is refraction necessary?"* Answer in one line: *Refraction prevents a rule from firing infinitely on the same unchanged data, thereby guaranteeing eventual termination of the recognize-act cycle.* # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Production System — High-Level Architecture This block diagram shows the three logical components of a production system and how they interact during one cycle of execution. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Working Memory
Dynamic Facts Store] -->|Read WMEs| B[Interpreter
Inference Engine] R[Production Memory
Static Rule Base] -->|Read rules| B B -->|1 MATCH phase| C[Conflict Set Builder] C -->|Conflict Set C| D[Conflict Resolver
Recency Specificity Priority] D -->|Selected rule Pk| E[Rule Executor
Act Phase] E -->|MAKE REMOVE MODIFY HALT| A E -->|Updated WM| B F[User / Sensor] -->|Input facts| A A -->|Output facts| G[User / Actuator] ``` ## 4.2 The Recognize-Act Cycle (Detailed Flow) The interpreter's main loop, drawn in Mermaid with explicit decision diamonds. ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Start Interpreter]) --> init[Initialize WM from input] init --> match[MATCH PHASE: scan all rules against WM] match --> checkC{Conflict Set C empty?} checkC -- Yes --> stop1([HALT: No applicable rule]) checkC -- No --> select[SELECT PHASE: apply conflict resolution] select --> pick[Choose exactly one rule Pk from C] pick --> act[ACT PHASE: execute RHS of Pk] act --> action{Action type} action -- MAKE --> assertW[Add new WME to WM] action -- REMOVE --> retractW[Delete WME from WM] action -- MODIFY --> modifyW[Update value of WME in WM] action -- HALT --> stop2([HALT: explicit termination]) assertW --> match retractW --> match modifyW --> match ``` ## 4.3 Conflict-Resolution Strategy Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD begin[Multiple rules in conflict set C] --> refr{Refraction check} refr -- Rule fired already on same data --> remove[Remove from C] refr -- New data --> spec{Specificity check} spec -- Higher number of conditions --> win1[Prefer this rule] spec -- Tie --> rec{Recency check} rec -- References newest WME --> win2[Prefer this rule] rec -- Tie --> prio{Priority salience assigned} prio -- Yes --> win3[Use numeric priority] prio -- No --> lex{LEX or MEA ordering} lex -- Yes --> win4[Apply LEX MEA] lex -- No --> win5[Pick first found] remove --> next[Continue to next rule] next --> spec ``` ## 4.4 Forward Chaining vs Backward Chaining (Data vs Goal Driven) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FWD[Forward Chaining Data Driven] F1[Initial Facts] --> F2[Match all rules] F2 --> F3[Resolve conflict] F3 --> F4[Fire rule add new facts] F4 --> F5{Goal reached} F5 -- No --> F2 F5 -- Yes --> F6[Output solution] end subgraph BWD[Backward Chaining Goal Driven] G1[Goal to prove] --> G2[Find rules whose RHS matches goal] G2 --> G3{Any rule found} G3 -- No --> G7[BACKTRACK try another goal] G3 -- Yes --> G4[Set sub-goals from LHS of selected rule] G4 --> G5{All sub-goals proven} G5 -- No --> G4 G5 -- Yes --> G6[Goal proven fire rule] G6 --> G2 end ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Expectation:** When the question asks for a *block diagram of a production system*, marks are awarded for showing the **three components** (Working Memory, Production Memory, Interpreter) and the **arrows in both directions** between WM and the interpreter. The above diagrams meet that bar. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define a Production Rule. Give one example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **production rule** is a conditional knowledge unit of the form **IF THEN **, used to represent heuristic knowledge in a rule-based expert system. - **1 mark** — Formal definition with the IF-THEN structure. - **1 mark** — Identification that it is modular and forms part of the production memory. - **1 mark** — A valid example. *Example:* **IF** the patient has a temperature greater than 100 °F **AND** has been coughing for more than 3 days **THEN** conclude that the patient may have pneumonia. --- ### Q2. List any two conflict-resolution strategies used in a production system. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The interpreter must pick one rule when more than one rule's LHS is satisfied. Two common strategies are: 1. **Recency (1 mark)** — Prefer the rule that references the *most recently added* working memory element. This biases the system toward reacting to the freshest evidence. 2. **Specificity (1 mark)** — Prefer the rule with the *largest number of conditions* in its LHS, on the ground that a more specific rule carries more diagnostic information. *(+1 mark for stating the purpose:* conflict resolution picks a single rule from the conflict set so the cycle can proceed deterministically.*)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Students frequently *describe* the recognize-act cycle but forget to **state the conflict set at every cycle** — **lose 2 marks**. > - Writing "WM is updated" without showing *which* WME was added or removed is treated as an incomplete step — **lose 1 mark per cycle**. > - Conflict-resolution strategies must be **defined + illustrated with an example**; merely naming them is worth only 1 of the 2 marks allocated. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — *Choose either A or B.* **Q-A.(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a production system. List its three main components and state the role of each. **[7 marks]** **Model Answer (7 marks):** A production system, in the Newell-Simon sense, has three logically distinct components: 1. **Production Memory (PM) — 1 mark.** A static, long-term database of production rules. It holds *all* the IF-THEN knowledge the system possesses. 2. **Working Memory (WM) — 1 mark.** A dynamic, short-term database of working memory elements (WMEs) representing the *current problem state*. WM is the only medium through which rules communicate with one another. 3. **Interpreter (Inference Engine / Control System) — 1 mark.** A procedural engine that runs the *recognize-act cycle*: it matches rules against WM, resolves conflicts, and fires the chosen rule. **Block diagram — 2 marks** (See SECTION 4.1). The examiner expects a labelled diagram with arrows showing WM and PM feeding into the Interpreter, and the Interpreter feeding actions back to WM. **Cycle description — 2 marks:** - **Match:** The interpreter scans all rules and builds the *conflict set* $\mathcal{C}$ of rules whose LHS is satisfied. - **Conflict resolution:** Picks exactly one rule from $\mathcal{C}$. - **Act:** Executes the chosen rule's RHS, updating WM (or halting). --- **Q-A.(b)** Explain the **Match-Select-Execute** cycle in detail. Illustrate with a small example involving at least four rules and five initial facts. Show at least **two cycles** of execution in a tabular form. **[7 marks]** **Model Answer (7 marks):** *See SECTION 3.2 to 3.4 above for the complete worked example.* The student is expected to: - Define each of the three phases (1 mark). - Give a small but non-trivial rule set + initial WM (1 mark). - Show **Cycle 1** with a non-empty conflict set of size 1 (1 mark). - Show **Cycle 2** with a *non-trivial* conflict set of size ≥ 2 and **explicitly state the conflict-resolution rule used** (2 marks). - Produce the summary table (1 mark). - State the termination condition (1 mark). > [!TIP] > The KTU valuation key requires that the conflict resolution be **named and applied** in at least one cycle, *not just described abstractly*. Following the table in §3.5 above guarantees full marks. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative choice for internal-option.* **Q-B.(a)** Define the terms *Working Memory*, *Conflict Set*, *Firing a Rule*, and *HALT action*. How does *refraction* prevent infinite looping in a production system? **[7 marks]** **Model Answer (7 marks):** - **Working Memory (WM) (1 mark):** The dynamic, mutable database of currently-known facts. Each fact is represented as a *working memory element* (WME), typically a tuple of the form `(class attribute value)`. - **Conflict Set (1 mark):** The subset of the production memory whose LHS conditions are all currently satisfied by the contents of WM. Formally: $$ \mathcal{C} = \lbrace P_i \in \mathcal{R} \mid P_i.\text{LHS} \models \text{WM} \rbrace $$ - **Firing a Rule (1 mark):** The act of *executing* the right-hand side (RHS / action part) of a rule that has been selected from the conflict set by the conflict-resolver. - **HALT action (1 mark):** A special RHS action that, when executed, immediately terminates the recognize-act cycle. The system returns the final WM as its output. - **Refraction (3 marks):** Refraction is a conflict-resolution heuristic that *removes a rule from the conflict set if it has already fired on the same set of WMEs* during the current cycle. The standard form is: *a rule is barred from re-matching the same instantiation for one cycle after firing.* This guarantees that no rule can fire infinitely on unchanged data, thus ensuring **eventual termination** of the interpreter loop. Refraction is essential because, without it, a rule whose RHS adds a new fact that re-satisfies its own LHS could cause the system to cycle forever. --- **Q-B.(b)** Compare **forward chaining** and **backward chaining** in a production system. Which one is more suitable for **monitoring** applications and why? Give one real-world example of each. **[7 marks]** **Model Answer (7 marks):** | Aspect | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining | Marks | |---|---|---|---| | Direction | Data → Conclusion | Goal → Sub-goals → Data | 1 | | Trigger | New fact added to WM | Goal pushed on the goal stack | 1 | | Control | Data-driven | Goal-driven | 1 | | Search | Breadth-wise through the rule set | Depth-wise through goal tree | 1 | | Suitable for | Monitoring, control, real-time systems | Diagnostic, query-answering, theorem proving | 1 | **Monitoring suitability (1 mark):** Forward chaining is the natural choice for monitoring. Sensors continuously push new facts into WM, and the system must react *as soon as* a relevant fact appears. There is no specific pre-stated goal — the system simply keeps reacting to fresh evidence. Backward chaining would have to invent a goal for every fresh datum, which is wasteful. **Real-world examples (1 mark each, total 2 marks):** - *Forward chaining:* **Snort** intrusion detection system — every network packet is asserted as a fact and matched against signature rules. - *Backward chaining:* **MYCIN** medical expert system — the goal "identify the infecting organism" is decomposed into sub-goals that test specific evidence. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this the night before the exam):** - A **production** is an **IF THEN ** rule — the *atomic* knowledge unit of a rule-based expert system. - A **production system** has exactly **three** components: **Working Memory (dynamic)**, **Production Memory (static rules)**, and the **Interpreter / Inference Engine** that runs the cycle. - The interpreter loop is the **recognize-act cycle**: **Match → Select (conflict resolution) → Act**. - **Match** builds the **conflict set** $\mathcal{C}$ of all rules whose LHS is currently satisfied. - **Select** picks *exactly one* rule from $\mathcal{C}$ using strategies: **Refraction, Recency, Specificity, Priority, LEX/MEA**. - **Act** executes the RHS — typical actions: **MAKE / REMOVE / MODIFY / HALT**. - **Refraction** prevents a rule from re-firing on the same data → guarantees **termination**. - A system terminates when either $\mathcal{C} = \varnothing$ or a **HALT** action is executed. - **Forward chaining** = data-driven (best for monitoring); **backward chaining** = goal-driven (best for diagnosis). - Production systems are used in **process control, IDS, medical ES, business rule engines, and reactive robotics**. - In a KTU answer, always show the **WM after every cycle**, the **conflict set**, the **selected rule**, and **why it was selected** — missing any one of these costs marks.

topics.title: Semantic Nets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT522 - EXPERT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Semantic Nets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semantic Net (Semantic Network):** A directed graph structure used for knowledge representation in Artificial Intelligence, where **nodes** represent objects, concepts, or events, and **labeled directed edges (arcs)** represent the semantic relationships between them. It was first proposed by **Ross Quillian (1968)** as a model of human associative memory.

A semantic net formally consists of the tuple:

$$ SN = (V, E, L) $$

Where:
- $V$ = Finite set of **vertices (nodes)** representing entities/concepts
- $E \subseteq V \times V$ = Set of **directed edges (arcs)** representing relations
- $L$ = Function mapping each edge to a **relation label** $L: E \rightarrow R$, where $R$ is the set of semantic relation types

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Semantic Nets are categorized as a *representational scheme* under knowledge representation paradigms, sitting alongside Frames, Scripts, Conceptual Graphs, and Ontologies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The "Family Tree of Ideas":**
> Imagine a **mind-map** you draw when brainstorming. Circles contain nouns (people, things, places) and arrows between them carry verbs describing *how* one idea relates to another. A semantic net is exactly that — a digital mind-map where the computer can traverse these arrows to perform logical inference.
>
> **Example:** "Ramu is a Dog" becomes a node `Ramu` connected by an arrow labeled `IS-A` to another node `Dog`. The computer then *knows* that because Ramu IS-A Dog, Ramu inherits all properties of Dog (e.g., has 4 legs, barks).

### 1.3 Core Vocabulary (KTU Board Terminology)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Node (Vertex)** | Represents an object, concept, or event |
| **Edge (Arc/Link)** | Represents a binary relation between two nodes |
| **Token** | A specific instance node (e.g., `Ramu`) |
| **Type** | A generic class node (e.g., `Dog`, `Mammal`) |
| **Inheritance** | A type hierarchy where subtypes inherit properties from supertypes |
| **ISA** | "is-a" relation defining class membership (e.g., Dog ISA Mammal) |
| **AKO** | "a-kind-of" relation (synonymous with ISA) |
| **HASA** | "has-a" relation denoting attribute/possession |
| **PART-OF** | Denotes structural/component relationships |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The *power* of a semantic net lies in **inheritance through ISA links** — this drastically reduces knowledge-base redundancy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Components of a Semantic Network

A complete semantic net system has **three structural pillars**:

1. **Lexical Level** — Defines the vocabulary of nodes and links used.
2. **Structural Level** — Defines the constraints on how nodes and arcs can combine.
3. **Semantic Level** — Associates meaning to the network and enables inference.

### 2.2 Types of Relations in Semantic Nets

| Relation Type | Direction | Example | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ISA` | Token → Type | Ramu $\rightarrow$ Dog | Class membership |
| `AKO` (A-Kind-Of) | Type → SuperType | Dog $\rightarrow$ Mammal | Taxonomy hierarchy |
| `HASA` | Object → Part | Car $\rightarrow$ Engine | Attribute / component |
| `PART-OF` | Part → Whole | Wheel $\rightarrow$ Bicycle | Structural decomposition |
| `OWNERSHIP` | Owner → Owned | Ramu $\rightarrow$ Car | Possession |
| `AGENT` | Action → Actor | Bite $\rightarrow$ Dog | Thematic role |
| `LOCATION` | Object → Place | Fish $\rightarrow$ Aquarium | Spatial context |
| `TIME` | Event → Time | Meeting $\rightarrow$ 10AM | Temporal context |

### 2.3 The Inheritance Algorithm (The "How")

**Step-by-step logic** of property inheritance:

1. Query arrives asking: *"Does Ramu have a tail?"*
2. System locates node `Ramu`.
3. System checks for direct property `has-tail` attached to `Ramu`.
4. If absent, follow outgoing `ISA` link from `Ramu` to `Dog`.
5. Check if `Dog` has property `has-tail = yes`.
6. If still absent, follow `ISA` from `Dog` to `Mammal` (transitive inheritance).
7. Return the first matching property found (or NULL).

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why':** This mimics how humans reason by generalization — we don't memorize that every dog has a tail; we learn the rule once at the `Dog` level.

### 2.4 Inference Rules in Semantic Nets

| Rule | Logic | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Inheritance** | $\text{If } x \text{ ISA } y \text{ and } y \text{ has property } p, \text{ then } x \text{ has } p$ | Dog ISA Mammal + Mammal.has-fur = true $\Rightarrow$ Dog.has-fur = true |
| **Transitivity** | $\text{If } a \rightarrow b \text{ and } b \rightarrow c, \text{ then } a \rightarrow c$ | "Ramu ISA Dog" and "Dog ISA Mammal" $\Rightarrow$ "Ramu ISA Mammal" |
| **Symmetry** (if relation is symmetric) | $\text{If } a \text{ relates to } b, \text{ then } b \text{ relates to } a$ | "Sibling-of" is symmetric |
| **Inverse** | $\text{If } a \text{ "is-parent-of" } b, \text{ then } b \text{ "is-child-of" } a$ | Parent-Child inverse pair |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Notation / Form | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Network definition | $SN = (V, E, L)$ | Set of nodes, edges, labels |
| Path length | $P(u, v) = n$ | A path of $n$ edges from $u$ to $v$ |
| Reachability | $u \rightsquigarrow v$ | $v$ is reachable from $u$ via directed path |
| Transitive closure | $E^+ = E \cup \{e \vert \exists \text{ path from } u \text{ to } v\}$ | All reachable relations |
| Inheritance depth | $d(\text{type})$ | Number of ISA edges to root |
| Fan-out | $\deg^+(v)$ | Out-degree of node $v$ (number of outgoing links) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Utility:** Semantic nets power **biomedical ontologies** (e.g., SNOMED-CT, Gene Ontology), **search-engine knowledge graphs** (Google Knowledge Graph), and **semantic web standards** (RDF, OWL — formally descended from semantic net theory).

### 2.6 Advantages & Limitations (High-Yield KTU Comparison)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **frequently asked KTU question** — expect a 7-mark Part-B question on "Discuss advantages and limitations of semantic nets."

**Advantages:**
- **Visual transparency** — Knowledge is human-readable.
- **Inferential efficiency** — Inheritance reduces redundancy.
- **Flexibility** — New nodes/links added without disturbing existing structure.
- **Default reasoning** — Easy to model exceptions and defaults.

**Limitations:**
- **No standard semantics** — Different implementations interpret links differently.
- **No quantifier support** — Cannot natively express "all" or "some" (unlike FOL).
- **Combinatorial explosion** — Large networks become intractable to traverse.
- **Inheritance conflicts** — Multiple inheritance creates diamond problem.
- **Lack of procedural attachment** — Cannot represent rules or "how-to" knowledge.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Building a Semantic Net for an Animal Kingdom

**Problem Statement:** Represent the following facts in a semantic network and answer the query: *"Does a Sparrow have wings?"*

**Given Facts:**
- F1: Tweety is a Sparrow.
- F2: Sparrow is a Bird.
- F3: Bird is an Animal.
- F4: Bird has Wings.
- F5: Animal has Heart.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 1:** Identify the *types* (classes) from the facts.

$$
\text{Types} = \{\text{Animal}, \text{Bird}, \text{Sparrow}\}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the *tokens* (instances) from the facts.

$$
\text{Tokens} = \{\text{Tweety}\}
$$

**Step 3:** Identify the *attributes* (HASA relations).

$$
\text{Attributes} = \{(\text{Bird}, \text{Wings}), \ (\text{Animal}, \text{Heart})\}
$$

**Step 4:** Identify the *class relations* (ISA relations).

$$
\text{ISA Edges} = \{(\text{Tweety}, \text{Sparrow}), \ (\text{Sparrow}, \text{Bird}), \ (\text{Bird}, \text{Animal})\}
$$

**Step 5:** Assemble the network structure. See **SECTION 4** for the rendered Mermaid diagram.

**Step 6:** Execute the query *"Does a Sparrow have wings?"*

- Locate node `Sparrow`.
- Check for direct `has-wings` property $\rightarrow$ **Not present.**
- Follow `ISA` link `Sparrow` $\rightarrow$ `Bird`.
- Check `Bird` for `has-wings` $\rightarrow$ **Found: TRUE.**
- Return: **YES, a Sparrow has wings (inherited from Bird).**

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Transitive Closure Table

The transitive closure $E^+$ gives all *implicit* ISA relations derivable from explicit ones.

| Source | Intermediate | Target | Explicit / Inferred? | Path Length |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tweety | Sparrow | Bird | Inferred via transitivity | 2 |
| Tweety | Sparrow, Bird | Animal | Inferred via transitivity | 3 |
| Sparrow | Bird | Animal | Inferred via transitivity | 2 |
| Tweety | — | Sparrow | **Explicit** (F1) | 1 |
| Sparrow | — | Bird | **Explicit** (F2) | 1 |
| Bird | — | Animal | **Explicit** (F3) | 1 |

**Mathematical formulation of transitive closure:**

$$
E^+ = E \cup E^2 \cup E^3 \cup \dots \cup E^n
$$

Where $E^k$ denotes paths of exactly $k$ edges. For a finite acyclic net with maximum depth $n$, this terminates at $E^n$.

### 3.4 Property Inheritance Resolution Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **exhaustive pseudo-formal specification** of how a semantic net engine resolves a property query. Every step is explicitly written — no skips.

**Algorithm: `InheritProperty(node v, property p)`**

1. **Initialize** result $\leftarrow$ `NULL`.
2. **Check direct property**: 
   - If `v.property[p]` exists, then result $\leftarrow$ `v.property[p]`.
   - Return result.
3. **Identify ISA parents**: Let $S = \{s \ \vert \ (v, s) \in E \text{ and } L(v, s) = \text{ISA}\}$.
4. **If $S$ is empty**: Return `NULL` (no further inheritance possible).
5. **For each parent $s \in S$ (in order of declaration priority)**:
   - Recursively call `InheritProperty(s, p)`.
   - If recursive result $\neq$ `NULL`, propagate and return.
6. **Return** final result (or `NULL` if no parent provides property $p$).

**Time Complexity:** In the worst case (linear chain of depth $n$), this is $O(n)$ lookups.

### 3.5 Multi-Inheritance & The Diamond Problem

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Question Pitfall:** Students often overlook the **diamond problem** in semantic nets.

Consider:
- `Flying` has property `can-fly = TRUE`
- `Penguin` is a `Bird` (and thus inherits `can-fly = TRUE`)
- `Penguin` is also a `Swimming` thing
- But *factually* penguins cannot fly.

**Resolution strategies:**

| Strategy | Mechanism | Drawback |
|---|---|---|
| **Default with Exception** | Mark Penguin's local `can-fly = FALSE` to override inheritance | Requires manual exception lists |
| **Prioritized Paths** | Assign weights to ISA links | Needs domain knowledge |
| **Cancellative Inheritance** (Touretzky, 1986) | Distinguish strict from defeasible inheritance | Complex reasoning logic |
| **Netted Inheritance** | Shortest-path reasoning in net | May produce counterintuitive results |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Animal Kingdom Semantic Net (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeTweety["Tweety - TOKEN Instance"]
    nodeSparrow["Sparrow - TYPE Class"]
    nodeBird["Bird - TYPE Class"]
    nodeAnimal["Animal - TYPE Class"]
    nodeWings["Wings - ATTRIBUTE"]
    nodeHeart["Heart - ATTRIBUTE"]
    nodeFly["Can Fly - ATTRIBUTE"]

    nodeTweety -- ISA --> nodeSparrow
    nodeSparrow -- ISA --> nodeBird
    nodeBird -- ISA --> nodeAnimal

    nodeBird -- HASA --> nodeWings
    nodeAnimal -- HASA --> nodeHeart
    nodeBird -- HASA --> nodeFly
```

### 4.2 Generic Knowledge Representation Architecture (Block Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    layer1["LEXICAL LEVEL - Vocabulary of nodes and links"]
    layer2["STRUCTURAL LEVEL - Constraints on valid graphs"]
    layer3["SEMANTIC LEVEL - Meaning and inference rules"]
    layer4["INFERENCE ENGINE - Pattern matcher and traverser"]
    layer5["USER INTERFACE - Query and response"]

    layer1 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer5
    layer5 -- feedback --> layer4
```

### 4.3 ISA vs HASA vs PART-OF Distinction Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    subA["TAXONOMY RELATIONSHIPS"]
    subB["ATTRIBUTE RELATIONSHIPS"]
    subC["STRUCTURAL RELATIONSHIPS"]

    subA --> exA1["Dog ISA Mammal"]
    subA --> exA2["Sparrow ISA Bird"]

    subB --> exB1["Dog HASA Tail"]
    subB --> exB2["Car HASA Engine"]

    subC --> exC1["Wheel PART-OF Bicycle"]
    subC --> exC2["Engine PART-OF Car"]
```

### 4.4 Inference Path Resolution Flow (For Query: Does Sparrow have Wings?)

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["START - Query Does Sparrow have wings"]
    s1["Locate node Sparrow"]
    s2["Check direct HASA wings on Sparrow"]
    s3{"Direct property found?"}
    s4["Return TRUE - direct property"]
    s5["Follow ISA link Sparrow to Bird"]
    s6["Check HASA wings on Bird"]
    s7{"Inherited property found?"}
    s8["Return TRUE - inherited from Bird"]
    s9["Follow ISA Bird to Animal"]
    s10["Check HASA wings on Animal"]
    s11{"Property found at root?"}
    s12["Return TRUE - from root"]
    s13["Return NULL - no such property"]

    start --> s1 --> s2 --> s3
    s3 -- yes --> s4
    s3 -- no --> s5 --> s6 --> s7
    s7 -- yes --> s8
    s7 -- no --> s9 --> s10 --> s11
    s11 -- yes --> s12
    s11 -- no --> s13
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define a semantic net. What are its basic components?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A semantic net is a directed graph used for knowledge representation in AI, where **nodes** represent objects, concepts or events, and **labeled directed edges (arcs)** represent semantic relationships between them. Its three basic components are:
1. **Nodes (Vertices)** — represent entities, objects, or concepts.
2. **Edges (Arcs/Links)** — represent the binary relationships between nodes.
3. **Labels** — describe the type of relationship (e.g., `ISA`, `HASA`, `PART-OF`).

A semantic net is formally represented as $SN = (V, E, L)$ where $V$ is the set of nodes, $E$ is the set of edges, and $L$ is the labeling function for edges. *(Award 1 mark each for definition, components, and formal tuple.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Distinguish between `ISA` and `HASA` relations in a semantic net with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `ISA` Relation | `HASA` Relation |
|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | "is-a" — denotes class membership | "has-a" — denotes attribute or possession |
| **Purpose** | Establishes taxonomy hierarchy | Establishes attribute ownership |
| **Direction** | Instance/Subtype $\rightarrow$ Supertype | Object $\rightarrow$ Attribute |
| **Example** | Tweety **ISA** Sparrow | Car **HASA** Engine |
| **Inheritance** | Inherits all supertype properties | Does not establish inheritance |

*(Award 1 mark for table distinction, 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Construct a semantic network to represent the following facts and answer the query: *"Is Pluto a mammal?"*
> Facts:
> 1. Pluto is a Dog.
> 2. Dog is a Mammal.
> 3. Mammal is an Animal.
> 4. Dog has 4 legs.
> 5. Mammal has fur.
> 6. Animal has heart.
>
> **(b)** Discuss the advantages and limitations of semantic networks as a knowledge representation scheme.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Semantic Net Construction (7 marks):**

Step 1: Identify the nodes. Types: `{Animal, Mammal, Dog}`. Token: `{Pluto}`. Attributes: `{(Dog, 4-legs), (Mammal, fur), (Animal, heart)}`.

Step 2: Identify ISA links:
- `Pluto` ISA `Dog`
- `Dog` ISA `Mammal`
- `Mammal` ISA `Animal`

Step 3: Construct the network diagram (as shown in Section 4, replacing the bird example with the dog one).

Step 4: Query resolution — "Is Pluto a mammal?"

- Locate node `Pluto`.
- Check direct `ISA` edge from `Pluto` $\rightarrow$ lands on `Dog` (not Mammal directly).
- **Apply transitive closure**: From `Pluto` $\rightarrow$ `Dog` $\rightarrow$ `Mammal` $\rightarrow$ `Animal`.
- Using transitivity of ISA: $\text{Pluto ISA Dog} \land \text{Dog ISA Mammal} \Rightarrow \text{Pluto ISA Mammal}$.
- **Answer: YES**, Pluto is (indirectly) a Mammal via transitive inheritance.

**Part (b) — Advantages and Limitations (7 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **Visual Clarity** — Graphs are intuitive and easily understood by humans.
2. **Inferential Power** — Inheritance via ISA links enables automatic property propagation.
3. **Modularity** — New nodes and links can be added without restructuring the existing network.
4. **Default Reasoning** — Supports non-monotonic reasoning through exceptions.
5. **Reduced Redundancy** — Common properties stored once at the type level.

**Limitations:**
1. **No Universal Semantics** — Standards like KL-ONE or RDF/OWL must be imposed; ad-hoc networks are ambiguous.
2. **Lack of Expressive Power** — Cannot easily express quantifiers ("all", "some") without extending to FOL.
3. **No Procedural Knowledge** — Cannot natively represent "how-to" rules.
4. **Combinatorial Explosion** — Real-world knowledge bases can have millions of nodes; traversal becomes slow.
5. **Inheritance Anomalies** — Multiple inheritance (e.g., the diamond problem) creates conflicts.
6. **Difficulty in Validation** — Hard to check logical consistency of large networks.

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **inheritance** in semantic networks. How does the `ISA` hierarchy enable property inheritance? Illustrate with an example involving at least three levels of hierarchy.
>
> **(b)** Compare semantic networks with **Frames** as knowledge representation techniques. List at least four comparison points.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Inheritance in Semantic Networks (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Inheritance is the mechanism by which properties and relations defined at a higher (more general) class are automatically available to its lower (more specific) subclasses or instances, without explicit redefinition.

**ISA Hierarchy (2 marks):** The `ISA` relation forms a directed acyclic graph (DAG) that defines class-subclass relationships. It enables:
- **Direct inheritance** — one level.
- **Transitive inheritance** — multiple levels via path traversal.
- **Multiple inheritance** — a node can have multiple `ISA` parents.

**Illustrative Example (4 marks):**

Consider a three-level hierarchy:

- **Level 1 (Root):** `LivingThing` has property `has-life = TRUE`.
- **Level 2 (Mid):** `Animal` ISA `LivingThing`; adds property `has-senses = TRUE`.
- **Level 3 (Specific):** `Dog` ISA `Animal`; adds property `barks = TRUE`.
- **Instance:** `Bruno` ISA `Dog`.

**Query:** *"Does Bruno have life?"*

- `Bruno` does not have direct property `has-life`.
- Follow ISA: `Bruno` $\rightarrow$ `Dog` (no property) $\rightarrow$ `Animal` (no property) $\rightarrow$ `LivingThing` (found: `has-life = TRUE`).
- **Answer:** YES, Bruno has life, inherited through two levels of ISA hierarchy.

This demonstrates **multi-level transitive inheritance**.

**Part (b) — Semantic Networks vs Frames (7 marks):**

| Comparison Point | Semantic Network | Frame |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Graph of nodes & labeled arcs | Slot-and-filler structure (object-oriented) |
| **Inheritance** | Through ISA links (transitive) | Through AKO/ISA slots in frames |
| **Default Values** | Not directly supported | Slots can have default values |
| **Procedural Attachment** | Not supported | Demons / attached procedures supported |
| **Expressiveness** | Limited to binary relations | Can represent complex structured objects |
| **Origin** | Quillian (1968) — cognitive model | Minsky (1975) — vision/script model |
| **Use Case** | Lightweight knowledge graphs | Rich object hierarchies with rules |

*(Award 1 mark for table completeness, 1 mark for two structural differences, 1 mark for inheritance mechanism, 1 mark for default/procedural support, and 3 marks for clarity of explanation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `ISA` with `HASA`** — Students mix up the direction; `ISA` goes *up* the hierarchy (instance → class), `HASA` goes *out* to attributes.
> 2. **Forgetting transitivity** — When asked "Is X a Y?", many students stop at one ISA link. Always check the **transitive closure**.
> 3. **Not drawing diagrams** — Even in theory questions, a labeled diagram is **expected** and earns 1–2 free marks.
> 4. **Skipping formal notation** — Always include $SN = (V, E, L)$ for a full-mark definition.
> 5. **Ignoring the diamond problem** — In multi-inheritance questions, mention the conflict and resolution strategy.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Semantic Nets**

- **Definition:** Semantic net = directed graph $(V, E, L)$ of concepts and relations.
- **Three levels:** Lexical, Structural, Semantic.
- **Core relations:** `ISA` (taxonomy), `HASA` (attribute), `PART-OF` (structure).
- **Inheritance:** Properties propagate upward (from general to specific) via `ISA` links.
- **Transitive closure:** $E^+ = E \cup E^2 \cup \dots \cup E^n$ — derives implicit ISA links.
- **Multi-inheritance pitfall:** Diamond problem → handled via exception lists, weighted paths, or Touretzky's netted inheritance.
- **Advantages:** Visual, modular, supports default reasoning, reduced redundancy.
- **Limitations:** No universal semantics, no quantifier support, no procedural attachment, scalability issues.
- **Origins:** Ross Quillian (1968); formalized later in KL-ONE, RDF, OWL.
- **Modern descendants:** Google Knowledge Graph, biomedical ontologies, semantic web.
- **Comparison keywords for exams:** vs Frames, vs Production Rules, vs FOPL, vs Conceptual Graphs.
- **Key equation to memorize:** $SN = (V, E, L)$.

> **Closing Note for KTU Students:** Semantic nets are the *conceptual grandparent* of all modern knowledge graphs. A clear understanding of inheritance, transitivity, and the diamond problem will directly help you in **ontology engineering** and **RDF/OWL-based** professional roles post-B.Tech.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Schemata
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT522 - EXPERT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Schemata ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of **Expert Systems** and Artificial Intelligence, a **Schema** (plural: *Schemata*) is a structured, declarative knowledge representation framework used to organize, store, and manipulate generic knowledge about a particular concept, object, situation, or event. Originally proposed by the cognitive psychologist **Frederic Bartlett (1932)** and later formalized for AI by **Marvin Minsky (1975)** as part of his **Frame Theory**, a schema acts as a stereotyped data structure containing slots (attributes) and fillers (values) that capture both factual and procedural knowledge. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Schemata):** > A schema is essentially a "mental template" or "blueprint" of knowledge. It represents an object, situation, or category by a collection of attributes (slots) and their values (fillers), allowing an expert system to reason about entities in a structured, hierarchical, and inheritable manner. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine you walk into a **restaurant** for the very first time. Even though you have never been to *that particular* restaurant, you already have a mental picture of what to expect: - There will be a **menu** (a slot) - You will be **seated by a host/waiter** (a slot) - You will **order food** (a slot) - You will **pay the bill at the end** (a slot) That pre-built expectation — the generic "restaurant template" stored in your brain — is exactly what a **Schema** is in an expert system. It is a pre-defined skeleton that captures the common, expected structure of a class of things, leaving room for specific details to be filled in (e.g., *this* restaurant has vegetarian options, *that* one has a sea view). > [!IMPORTANT] > A schema is **NOT** a single fact or a single rule. It is a **packaged bundle of related knowledge** about a category, complete with default assumptions, exceptions, and procedures to follow when expected information is missing. ### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Meaning (Plain English) | |---|---| | **Schema / Frame** | A structured knowledge template for a concept | | **Slot** | An attribute or property of the schema | | **Filler** | The value assigned to a slot | | **Default Value** | Pre-assumed value used if actual data is unavailable | | **Constraint** | Rules restricting what fillers are valid | | **Inheritance** | Mechanism by which a child schema gets properties from a parent | | **Instance** | A specific occurrence of a schema (a particular object) | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Conceptual Origin – From Bartlett to Minsky - **Bartlett (1932)**: Proposed that human memory stores generalized knowledge structures, which he called *schemata*, used to interpret new experiences. - **Minsky (1975)**: Adapted the idea to AI and called these structures *frames*. In expert systems, the terms **schema** and **frame** are often used interchangeably. - **Winston (1977)**: Further refined structural descriptions using schemata for pattern recognition. > [!NOTE] > **Why Schemata were needed:** > Earlier AI systems relied on flat lists of facts (semantic networks) or simple rules. They failed to capture: > 1. **Default knowledge** ("birds usually fly") > 2. **Stereotyped situations** (a hospital visit, a wedding) > 3. **Hierarchical generalization** (a robin *is-a* bird, hence it likely flies) ### 2.2 Structural Anatomy of a Schema A schema is composed of the following key elements: 1. **Schema Name / Class Identifier** — uniquely names the concept (e.g., `BIRD`, `HOSPITAL_VISIT`, `CAR`). 2. **Slots (Attributes / Roles)** — define *what kind of information* the schema holds. - *Example slots for a CAR schema:* `make`, `model`, `year`, `engine_type`, `owner`, `current_speed`. 3. **Fillers (Slot Values)** — concrete or abstract values occupying slots. - *Example filler:* `make = Toyota`, `year = 2022`. 4. **Default Values** — fallback fillers used when actual value is unknown. - *Example:* `num_wheels = 4` (default for any car unless told otherwise). 5. **Constraints (Type / Range / Cardinality)** — restrictions on valid fillers. - *Example:* `year` must be an integer between 1900 and 2026. 6. **Procedural Attachments (Demons / If-Needed / If-Added)** — small procedures triggered when a slot is accessed or modified. - *If-Needed:* compute `current_speed` on request. - *If-Added:* update `maintenance_log` when a new `owner` is added. 7. **Inheritance Links (AKO / IS-A)** — pointers to parent schemas from which slots/fillers are inherited. - *Example:* `ROBIN` is-a `BIRD` is-a `ANIMAL`. 8. **Exception Values** — overrides to defaults for special cases. - *Example:* for the schema `PENGUIN` (a bird), `can_fly = false` (exception to default). ### 2.3 Types of Schemata (KTU High-Yield Classification) | Type of Schema | Purpose | Real-World Example | |---|---|---| | **Conceptual / Object Schema** | Describes a class of objects | `CAR`, `BIRD`, `DOCTOR` | | **Event Schema** | Describes a typical sequence of actions | `BIRTHDAY_PARTY`, `SURGERY` | | **Script Schema** | Detailed, time-ordered event script (Schank & Abelson) | `RESTAURANT_SCRIPT`, `DOCTOR_VISIT_SCRIPT` | | **Role Schema** | Describes a participant's role in an event | `PATIENT`, `WAITER` | | **Story / Episode Schema** | Stores specific past episodes with context | `My_visit_to_Kerala_in_2024` | | **Causal Schema** | Links causes to effects | `RAIN → WET_ROADS` | ### 2.4 Inheritance Hierarchy & Exception Handling Schemata are typically arranged in a **taxonomy** (a hierarchical tree). Properties of a parent schema are inherited by all child schemas unless explicitly overridden. **Rules governing inheritance:** - A child schema inherits all slots and default fillers of its parent(s). - A child can **add new slots** specific to itself. - A child can **override** a parent's default filler with an **exception value**. - Multiple inheritance is allowed but may create **conflicts** (resolution via specificity, recency, or user priority). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Default Reasoning in Expert Systems:** > If the system is told "Tweety is a bird", it can *deduce* (by default) that Tweety can fly — without being explicitly told. If later told "Tweety is a penguin", the system must **retract** the default and apply the exception `can_fly = false`. This is known as **non-monotonic reasoning** and is central to schema-based systems. ### 2.5 Schemata vs. Semantic Networks (High-Yield Comparison) | Feature | Semantic Network | Schemata | |---|---|---| | **Basic Unit** | Node + labeled edge | Frame with slots/fillers | | **Structure** | Flat graph | Hierarchical, structured record | | **Defaults** | Not supported | Supported via default fillers | | **Procedures** | Absent | Demons (if-needed, if-added) | | **Inheritance** | Limited (via ISA links) | Full, with exception handling | | **Expressiveness** | Lower | Higher | | **Use case** | Simple taxonomic relations | Complex real-world knowledge | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Medical Diagnosis Systems** (e.g., MYCIN-style extensions): schema for `PATIENT` holds `symptoms`, `history`, `test_results`. - **Automotive Fault Diagnosis**: schema for `ENGINE` with slots for `temperature`, `vibration`, `oil_level`, each linked to demons that trigger diagnostic rules. - **Geological Exploration (PROSPECTOR)**: schemata represent rock types, mineral deposits, and exploration evidence. - **Natural Language Understanding**: schemata help systems disambiguate sentences by activating expected context (e.g., activating the `RESTAURANT_SCRIPT` upon seeing words like "menu", "waiter", "bill"). ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Schemata are qualitative representations; there are **no numerical equations**. The "formulas" below are the formal structural relations used to reason with them. | # | Structural / Logical Relation | LaTeX Form | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | IS-A / AKO (A-Kind-Of) | $\text{CHILD} \sqsubseteq \text{PARENT}$ | Child schema is a kind of parent | | 2 | Instance-Of | $x \in C$ | Object $x$ is an instance of class $C$ | | 3 | Slot Inheritance | $\text{slot}(C) \gets \text{slot}(P) \ \ \forall \ C \sqsubseteq P$ | Child inherits parent's slots | | 4 | Default Inheritance | $C \sqsubseteq P \land \exists\, d_P \Rightarrow d_C = d_P$ | Child takes parent's default if no override | | 5 | Exception Override | $C \sqsubseteq P \land \exists\, e_C \Rightarrow \text{use } e_C \text{ over } d_P$ | Exception beats default | | 6 | Trigger Condition (Demon) | $\text{If-Needed}(S, v) \rightarrow f(v)$ | Run function $f$ to compute $v$ | | 7 | Constraint Satisfaction | $\text{value}(s) \in \text{Domain}(s)$ | Filler must lie in slot's domain | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example: Building a Schema Hierarchy **Problem Statement:** Construct a schema-based knowledge representation for the domain of **Vehicles**, including: - A generic `VEHICLE` schema - A `CAR` and a `TRUCK` schema (children of `VEHICLE`) - A `SPORTS_CAR` schema (child of `CAR`) - A specific instance: `my_car` (instance of `SPORTS_CAR`) Show how the system deduces that `my_car` has 4 wheels and a petrol engine without being explicitly told. --- ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Construction **Step 1 — Define the top-level (root) schema `VEHICLE`** This is the most general template. It captures what is true of *all* vehicles. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{VEHICLE}: \ & \\ \text{name} & : \text{VEHICLE} \\ \text{superclass} & : \text{null (root)} \\ \text{slots:} \ & \\ \ \ \ \text{num\_wheels} & : \text{integer, default} = 4 \\ \ \ \ \text{power\_source} & : \text{string, default} = \text{"unknown"} \\ \ \ \ \text{max\_speed} & : \text{integer, range} = [0,\, 500] \\ \ \ \ \text{has\_engine} & : \text{boolean, default} = \text{true} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic of Step 1:** The root schema provides the broadest defaults. `num_wheels = 4` is a sensible *default* because the vast majority of vehicles are 4-wheeled, but a truck (18 wheels) or a motorcycle (2 wheels) can override it later. --- **Step 2 — Define the child schema `CAR`** `CAR` inherits everything from `VEHICLE` and adds car-specific slots. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CAR} \sqsubseteq \text{VEHICLE}: \ & \\ \text{inherited} & : \text{num\_wheels} = 4,\ \text{has\_engine} = \text{true} \\ \text{new slots:} \ & \\ \ \ \ \text{num\_doors} & : \text{integer, default} = 4 \\ \ \ \ \text{fuel\_type} & : \text{string, default} = \text{"petrol"} \\ \ \ \ \text{transmission} & : \text{string, default} = \text{"manual"} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic of Step 2:** No defaults are overridden at the `CAR` level. Inheritance is straightforward — `CAR` quietly takes all of `VEHICLE`'s defaults. --- **Step 3 — Define the child schema `TRUCK` (with an exception)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TRUCK} \sqsubseteq \text{VEHICLE}: \ & \\ \text{overrides:} \ & \\ \ \ \ \text{num\_wheels} & : \text{default overridden to } 6 \text{ (exception)} \\ \text{new slots:} \ & \\ \ \ \ \text{cargo\_capacity} & : \text{float, range} = [0,\, 100]\ \text{tonnes} \\ \ \ \ \text{cabin\_type} & : \text{string, default} = \text{"single"} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic of Step 3:** This is where **exception handling** comes in. A truck breaks the "4 wheels" default. The schema records this exception explicitly so that a generic vehicle query ("how many wheels does a truck have?") is answered with the overridden value `6`, not the inherited default `4`. --- **Step 4 — Define the grandchild schema `SPORTS_CAR`** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SPORTS\_CAR} \sqsubseteq \text{CAR}: \ & \\ \text{overrides:} \ & \\ \ \ \ \text{fuel\_type} & : \text{default overridden to } \text{"premium petrol"} \\ \ \ \ \text{transmission} & : \text{default overridden to } \text{"automatic"} \\ \text{new slots:} \ & \\ \ \ \ \text{acceleration\_0\_60} & : \text{float, range} = [2.0,\, 8.0]\ \text{seconds} \\ \ \ \ \text{has\_spoiler} & : \text{boolean, default} = \text{true} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic of Step 4:** `SPORTS_CAR` inherits *everything* from both `CAR` and (transitively) `VEHICLE`, then layers in sport-specific refinements. --- **Step 5 — Create an instance `my_car`** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{my\_car} \in \text{SPORTS\_CAR}: \ & \\ \text{make} & : \text{"Ferrari"} \\ \text{model} & : \text{"488 GTB"} \\ \text{year} & : 2023 \\ \text{color} & : \text{"Rosso Corsa red"} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 3.3 Deduction Step (Why Inheritance is Powerful) **Question posed to the system:** *"What is the fuel type and number of wheels of my_car?"* $$ \begin{aligned} \text{fuel\_type(my\_car)} & \xrightarrow{\text{Instance-Of}} \text{look in SPORTS\_CAR} \\ & \rightarrow \text{override exists: "premium petrol"} \\ & = \text{"premium petrol"} \\[4pt] \text{num\_wheels(my\_car)} & \xrightarrow{\text{Instance-Of}} \text{look in SPORTS\_CAR} \\ & \rightarrow \text{no override} \rightarrow \text{go to CAR} \\ & \rightarrow \text{no override} \rightarrow \text{go to VEHICLE} \\ & \rightarrow \text{default = } 4 \\ & = 4 \end{aligned} $$ > **Conclusion:** The system correctly deduced both answers **without ever being told explicitly** in the instance. This is the power of schema-based inheritance and default reasoning. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Full Inheritance Table | Property | VEHICLE | CAR | TRUCK | SPORTS\_CAR | my\_car | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | `num_wheels` (default) | 4 | 4 (inherited) | **6 (exception)** | 4 (inherited) | 4 (resolved) | | `has_engine` (default) | true | true (inherited) | true (inherited) | true (inherited) | true (resolved) | | `num_doors` (default) | — | 4 | — | 4 (inherited) | 4 (resolved) | | `fuel_type` (default) | — | petrol | — | **premium petrol (exception)** | premium petrol (resolved) | | `cargo_capacity` | — | — | 0–100 t | — | not applicable | | `make` (specific) | — | — | — | — | Ferrari (given) | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Inheritance Hierarchy of the Vehicle Schemata ```mermaid graph TD rootA[VEHICLE
num_wheels = 4
has_engine = true] childA[CAR
num_doors = 4
fuel_type = petrol] childB[TRUCK
num_wheels = 6 OVERRIDE
cargo_capacity] grandA[SPORTS_CAR
fuel_type = premium petrol OVERRIDE
has_spoiler = true] instA[my_car
make = Ferrari
model = 488 GTB] rootA --> childA rootA --> childB childA --> grandA grandA --> instA ``` ### 4.2 Internal Anatomy of a Single Schema (Frame Structure) ```mermaid graph TD frameA[SCHEMA NAME: SPORTS_CAR] slot1[SLOT: fuel_type] fill1[FILLER: premium petrol] con1[CONSTRAINT: string] slot2[SLOT: num_wheels] fill2[FILLER: 4 inherited] con2[CONSTRAINT: integer 2 to 18] slot3[SLOT: acceleration_0_60] fill3[FILLER: not yet known] demon1[IF NEEDED: compute_from_specs] frameA --> slot1 --> fill1 slot1 --> con1 frameA --> slot2 --> fill2 slot2 --> con2 frameA --> slot3 --> fill3 slot3 --> demon1 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How a Schema-Based Reasoner Operates ```mermaid graph LR userA[USER QUERY] --> ifaceA[Input Interface] ifaceA --> parserA[Query Parser] parserA --> matcherA[Schema Matcher] matcherA --> inheritA[Inheritance Engine] inheritA --> defaultA[Default Reasoner] defaultA --> demonA[Demon Trigger Module] demonA --> constraintA[Constraint Checker] constraintA --> explainA[Explanation Generator] explainA --> outputA[Final Answer to User] kbA[(Schema Knowledge Base)] --> matcherA kbA --> inheritA kbA --> defaultA kbA --> constraintA ``` > **Reading the architecture:** > 1. The user asks a question. > 2. The **Query Parser** extracts the relevant concept (e.g., "my_car"). > 3. The **Schema Matcher** locates the matching frame in the knowledge base. > 4. The **Inheritance Engine** walks up the IS-A chain to gather all relevant slots. > 5. The **Default Reasoner** applies defaults where fillers are missing. > 6. The **Demon Trigger Module** fires any *if-needed* procedures. > 7. The **Constraint Checker** validates the final answer. > 8. The **Explanation Generator** produces a human-readable trace. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]** *Define a schema. List its main components.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A schema is a structured, frame-based knowledge representation that organizes generic knowledge about a concept, object, or event as a collection of labeled slots and their values. Main components are: **(1)** Schema name/class, **(2)** Slots (attributes), **(3)** Fillers (values), **(4)** Default values, **(5)** Constraints, **(6)** Inheritance links (IS-A/AKO), and **(7)** Procedural attachments (demons). *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing components.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]** *Differentiate between default values and exception values in a schema with an example.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **default value** is a filler automatically assumed by the system when actual data is unavailable — it represents the most common case *(1 mark)*. An **exception value** is an explicit override applied by a child schema to a parent's default, used when the common case does not hold *(1 mark)*. **Example (1 mark):** In the `BIRD` schema, the default filler for slot `can_fly` is `true`. The `PENGUIN` schema (a child of `BIRD`) sets `can_fly = false` as an **exception**. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice) --- #### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper] **(a)** Explain the concept of **schemata** in expert systems. Describe the **structural components** of a schema in detail with a suitable example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **inheritance hierarchy** of schemata. How are **defaults and exceptions** handled? Give an example from the medical diagnosis domain. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution to Question A ##### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Definition (2 marks):** A schema (also called a *frame*, as proposed by Minsky in 1975) is a structured knowledge representation structure that captures generic knowledge about a stereotyped situation, object, or event. It is essentially a **record-like template** with named slots and their corresponding fillers, enabling an expert system to reason by matching, inheriting, and defaulting. **Structural Components (5 marks — 1 mark each, then synthesis):** 1. **Schema Name** — uniquely identifies the class (e.g., `PATIENT`). 2. **Slots (Attributes)** — labeled fields holding information (e.g., `name`, `age`, `symptoms`). 3. **Fillers (Values)** — concrete or symbolic values occupying slots (e.g., `age = 45`). 4. **Default Values** — pre-assumed fillers for missing data (e.g., `body_temperature = 98.6°F`). 5. **Constraints** — restrictions on valid fillers (e.g., `age` is integer in [0, 120]). 6. **Inheritance Links** — `IS-A` / `AKO` pointers to parent schemas (e.g., `CHILD_PATIENT` is-a `PATIENT`). 7. **Procedural Attachments (Demons)** — `if-needed` and `if-added` procedures (e.g., compute `BMI` on demand). **Synthesis example (continuation):** A `PATIENT` schema may have slots: `name`, `age`, `gender`, `symptoms`, `medical_history`, `allergies`. Each slot has a type constraint. The `allergies` slot may have an `if-added` demon that cross-checks against prescribed drugs to prevent adverse interactions. --- ##### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Inheritance Diagram (3 marks):** ```mermaid graph TD rootMed[PERSON] p1[PATIENT] p2[DOCTOR] c1[CHILD_PATIENT] c2[ADULT_PATIENT] c3[CARDIOLOGIST] instM[Patient_X] rootMed --> p1 rootMed --> p2 p1 --> c1 p1 --> c2 p2 --> c3 c2 --> instM ``` **Defaults and Exceptions Explanation (3 marks):** - **Defaults** propagate downward through the IS-A chain unless overridden. For instance, `PERSON` schema may have default `has_heart = true`. `CHILD_PATIENT` and `ADULT_PATIENT` both inherit this. - **Exceptions** are explicit overrides in a child schema. For example, the `OPEN_HEART_SURGERY_PATIENT` schema (a subclass of `ADULT_PATIENT`) may set `heart_status = "bypassed"` — an exception to the inherited default. - The system uses **specificity ordering** to resolve conflicts: more specific (deeper in hierarchy) wins over more general. **Medical Diagnosis Example (1 mark):** Consider a `FLU_PATIENT` schema (is-a `PATIENT`). It inherits `body_temperature = 98.6°F` (default from `PERSON`), but overrides it with `body_temperature = 101°F` (exception indicating fever). The `TREATMENT` slot has an `if-needed` demon that, when accessed, automatically suggests "antiviral medication + rest + fluids" based on the current symptom fillers. --- #### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice] **(a)** Compare **schemata** and **semantic networks** as knowledge representation techniques. Which is more expressive and why? **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are **scripts**? Explain the components of a script with the classic **RESTAURANT script** example. How do scripts extend schemata? **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution to Question B ##### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Point-by-point comparison (5 marks — 1 mark per significant point):** | Aspect | Semantic Network | Schema | |---|---|---| | **Basic unit** | Node + labeled edge | Frame with slots/fillers | | **Structure** | Flat or shallow graph | Deep hierarchical record | | **Defaults** | Not natively supported | First-class feature | | **Procedures** | Absent | Demons (if-needed, if-added) | | **Constraints** | Not enforced | Enforced on fillers | | **Inheritance** | Limited (ISA edge only) | Rich, with exception handling | | **Reasoning** | Mostly taxonomic | Default, causal, procedural | **Expressiveness verdict (2 marks):** Schemata are more expressive than semantic networks because they can: 1. Encode **default knowledge** with exceptions (non-monotonic). 2. Attach **active procedures** to slots, enabling computation on demand. 3. Enforce **type and range constraints** for consistency. 4. Represent **stereotyped situations** (events, scripts) in a single coherent structure. Semantic networks are more compact and intuitive for simple taxonomic relations, but quickly become inadequate for complex, real-world expert reasoning. --- ##### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Definition of Script (1 mark):** A *script*, introduced by **Schank and Abelson (1977)**, is a specialized type of schema that represents a **stereotyped sequence of events** in a particular context.
