Scripts are time-ordered and capture the expected flow of actions for situations like visiting a doctor, eating at a restaurant, or boarding a flight. **Components of a Script (3 marks):** 1. **Entry Conditions** — conditions that must be true before the script begins (e.g., "customer is hungry, restaurant is open"). 2. **Props** — physical objects involved (e.g., menu, table, food, bill). 3. **Roles** — participants and their expected behaviors (e.g., CUSTOMER, WAITER, CHEF). 4. **Scenes** — sequential time-ordered episodes (e.g., entering, ordering, eating, paying). 5. **Results** — conditions true after the script ends (e.g., "customer is full, bill is paid"). **RESTAURANT Script Example (2 marks):** - **Entry Conditions:** Customer is hungry; restaurant has seating. - **Props:** Menu, table, food, money, bill. - **Roles:** CUSTOMER (orders, eats, pays), WAITER (greets, takes order, serves, presents bill), CHEF (prepares food). - **Scenes:** 1. *Entering:* customer walks in, waiter greets and seats. 2. *Ordering:* waiter hands menu, customer chooses, waiter takes order. 3. *Eating:* chef cooks, waiter serves, customer eats. 4. *Paying:* waiter presents bill, customer pays, customer leaves. - **Results:** Customer is no longer hungry; restaurant has revenue. **How Scripts Extend Schemata (1 mark):** Scripts are schemata specialized for **temporal/causal event sequences**. While a generic schema may describe the static properties of a "restaurant", a script encodes the *dynamic flow of actions and actors* that occur during a restaurant visit, enabling story understanding, plan recognition, and expectation-based inference in NLP and expert systems. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes Students Make:** > 1. **Confusing schema with semantic network:** Many students write "schema = a network of nodes". This is **wrong** — a schema is a *frame with slots and fillers*, not a graph node. Loses up to **2 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting exceptions:** When asked about defaults, students often give only the default and skip the **exception override mechanism**. Loses up to **2 marks**. > 3. **Mixing up scripts and frames:** Scripts are a *type* of schema (event schema), not a separate unrelated concept. Saying "scripts are completely different from frames" loses marks. > 4. **No diagram in Q(b):** A question that explicitly says *"with a neat diagram"* but is answered textually only gets **partial credit** (typically 60% of allotted marks for that sub-part). > 5. **Skipping procedural attachments:** Demons (if-needed, if-added) are a **distinguishing feature** of schemata vs. simple frames. Omitting them is treated as an incomplete answer. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Schemata (Module 1)** - ✅ A **schema (frame)** is a structured knowledge template with **slots, fillers, defaults, constraints, inheritance links, and procedural attachments (demons)**. - ✅ The concept was proposed by **Minsky (1975)** based on Bartlett's earlier cognitive theory. - ✅ **Default reasoning** allows the system to assume common-case values when data is missing. - ✅ **Exception values** in child schemas override parent defaults — this is the basis of **non-monotonic reasoning**. - ✅ **Inheritance** flows downward through IS-A / AKO links; deeper (more specific) schemas take priority. - ✅ **Procedural attachments (demons)**: *if-needed* (compute on demand), *if-added* (react to changes). - ✅ **Types of schemata**: Conceptual, Event, Script (Schank & Abelson), Role, Story, Causal. - ✅ **Schemata vs. Semantic Networks**: Schemata are richer — they support defaults, procedures, and constraints; semantic networks are simpler ISA graphs. - ✅ **Scripts** are time-ordered event schemata with **Entry Conditions, Props, Roles, Scenes, and Results**. - ✅ **Engineering applications**: medical diagnosis (MYCIN), fault diagnosis, geological exploration (PROSPECTOR), NLP understanding. - ✅ **Always draw the inheritance diagram** when a question asks for one — it carries significant marks. ---

topics.title: Frames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT522 - EXPERT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Frames

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Marvin Minsky, 1975):**
> A **Frame** is a data structure used in Artificial Intelligence to represent stereotyped knowledge about a concept, object, or situation. It consists of a collection of attributes (called **slots**) and their associated values (called **fillers**), along with attached procedures (called **facets** or **daemons**) that control how the frame is used, updated, or inherited from.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Expert Systems - PECMT522), a frame is considered one of the four primary **knowledge representation schemes** alongside **Predicate Logic, Semantic Networks, and Production Rules**. Frames are particularly powerful for representing **declarative and procedural knowledge** about objects, events, and situations in a structured, object-oriented manner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context (Syllabus Highlight):**
> Frames were introduced by **Marvin Minsky** in his seminal 1974 paper *"A Framework for Representing Knowledge"* published in the MIT AI Laboratory Memo. The motivation was to address the brittleness of early rule-based and logic-based systems by capturing **commonsense reasoning** and **default assumptions**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a hotel reception. You already have a mental "template" of what a hotel room looks like — even before you open the door. You expect:
- A bed (likely)
- A bathroom (almost certainly)
- A TV (probably)
- A window (definitely)

This mental template, with **default values**, **slots for specific details**, and the ability to **inherit** common features (every room in this hotel probably has a Wi-Fi router), is precisely what a **Frame** represents in an Expert System.

A frame is essentially a **data record** (similar to a struct in C or a class in OOP), where each field (slot) can contain:
- A specific value
- A pointer to another frame
- A default value to be used if no specific value is provided
- A procedure (daemon) that runs when the slot is accessed or modified

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Frame** | The entire knowledge structure representing one concept/object |
| **Slot** | An attribute or property of the frame (e.g., `name`, `age`) |
| **Facet** | Sub-structures that constrain or describe a slot's value |
| **Filler** | The actual value stored in a slot |
| **Default Value** | A pre-assigned value used when no specific value is given |
| **Daemon (Procedural Attachment)** | Procedures triggered by slot access/modification |
| **Instance Frame** | A specific object (e.g., "my car") |
| **Class Frame** | A generic category (e.g., "Car") |
| **Inheritance** | Mechanism for a frame to acquire slots/values from a parent |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exam:**
> Think **"FSFD"** — **F**rame → **S**lot → **F**acet → **D**aemon. This is the structural hierarchy examiners frequently test.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Components of a Frame (Bulleted Logic Decomposition)

A complete frame in KTU syllabus context consists of the following layered structure:

- **1. Frame Name / Frame ID**
  - Unique identifier for the frame (e.g., `CAR_01`, `EMPLOYEE_RECORDS`).
  - 'Why' needed: Distinguishes one knowledge unit from another in the knowledge base.
  - 'How' used: Acts as the lookup key for retrieval and inheritance.

- **2. Slots (Attributes / Properties)**
  - Named components representing characteristics of the entity.
  - Example: A `CAR` frame might have slots like `make`, `model`, `year`, `engine_type`, `owner`.
  - Each slot can be of a specific **data type** (string, integer, boolean, frame-reference, list).

- **3. Facets (Slot Descriptors)**
  - Facets describe additional constraints, defaults, and procedural attachments for a slot.
  - Common facet types:
    - **Value Facet** — The actual value or list of values.
    - **Default Facet** — Used if no explicit value is provided (`IF-NEEDED`).
    - **Type Facet** — Restricts allowed values (e.g., `must be integer between 0-200`).
    - **Range Facet** — Specifies legal ranges or enumerated values.
    - **If-Needed Facet** — Procedure executed to compute the slot's value on demand.
    - **If-Added Facet** — Procedure executed when a value is added to the slot.
    - **If-Deleted Facet** — Procedure executed when a value is removed.
    - **Documentation Facet** — Natural language comment explaining the slot.

- **4. Inheritance Links**
  - A frame can inherit slots and default values from one or more **parent (superclass) frames** using an `AKO` (A-Kind-Of) or `IS-A` link.
  - Supports **multiple inheritance** and **exception handling**.

- **5. Messages (Procedural Attachment / Daemons)**
  - Demons are `IF-ADDED`, `IF-REMOVED`, `IF-MODIFIED` procedures that fire automatically upon state changes.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Concept Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Notation / Representation** | **Function / Use** |
|------------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Frame Definition | `FRAME <name> <AKO <parent>>` | Declares a new frame and its parent |
| Slot Declaration | `slot: <slot_name>` | Defines a property |
| Facet (Value) | `value: <filler>` | Assigns a value |
| Facet (Default) | `default: <filler>` | Default if no value present |
| Facet (Range) | `range: [min, max]` or `{v1, v2, ...}` | Restricts values |
| If-Needed | `if-needed: <procedure>` | Lazy evaluation of slot |
| Inheritance Operator | `AKO` (A-Kind-Of), `IS-A`, `Instance-Of` | Establishes hierarchy |
| Daemon Types | `IF-ADDED`, `IF-REMOVED`, `IF-MODIFIED` | Side-effect procedures |
| Conflict Resolution | `Override / Dominance` rule | Resolves multi-inheritance conflicts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Notational Rule:**
> When multiple parent frames contribute values to the same slot, KTU examiners often test the **Dominance / Override Rule** — the frame declared **first** in the parent list (or the **most specific** frame) takes precedence (this is the *depth-first, left-to-right* traversal used in languages like Flavors and LOOPS).

### 2.3 Classification of Frames (Engineering Taxonomy)

Frames can be classified along multiple axes, all of which are **high-yield** for KTU Module 1 questions:

- **By Granularity**
  - **Class Frame** — represents a category (e.g., `MAMMAL`).
  - **Instance Frame** — represents a specific member (e.g., `DOLPHIN_01`).

- **By Function**
  - **Conceptual Frames** — describe objects/ideas (e.g., `CHAIR`, `LOVE`).
  - **Situational Frames** — describe events (e.g., `BIRTHDAY_PARTY`, `EARTHQUAKE`).
  - **Story Frames** — high-level narrative structures used in NLP.

- **By Inheritance Behavior**
  - **Single-Inheritance Frames** — one parent.
  - **Multiple-Inheritance Frames** — many parents (requires conflict resolution).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Frames

- **Diagnostic Expert Systems** — MYCIN-like medical systems use frames to represent diseases, symptoms, and patient profiles.
- **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** — Frames model engineering components with parametric slots.
- **Natural Language Processing** — FrameNet uses semantic frames for word sense disambiguation.
- **Robotics** — Object recognition systems use frames to identify expected sensor signatures.
- **Intelligent Tutoring Systems** — Student-model frames track learner progress and misconceptions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Building a Class-Instance Frame Hierarchy (Step-by-Step Construction)

**Problem Statement:**
Construct a complete frame system representing the following knowledge:
- A `VEHICLE` (class) has `wheels`, `engine`, `max_speed`.
- A `CAR` (subclass) inherits from `VEHICLE` and adds `doors`, `fuel_type`.
- A specific instance `MY_CAR` (Toyota Camry, 2022) is an instance of `CAR` with `owner = "John"`, `color = "Blue"`.

**Step 1 — Declare the Top-Level Class Frame (VEHICLE)**

```
FRAME: VEHICLE
  AKO:        OBJECT
  slot: wheels
    value:    4
    type:     integer
  slot: engine
    value:    yes
    type:     boolean
  slot: max_speed
    range:    [0, 500]      # km/h
    default:  120
```

*Logic:* The `VEHICLE` frame establishes baseline properties every vehicle will inherit. The `AKO: OBJECT` marks it as a generic top-level class.

**Step 2 — Declare the Subclass Frame (CAR) with Inheritance**

```
FRAME: CAR
  AKO:        VEHICLE
  slot: doors
    range:    {2, 3, 4, 5}
    default:  4
  slot: fuel_type
    range:    {petrol, diesel, electric, hybrid}
    default:  petrol
  slot: max_speed              # OVERRIDDEN default
    default:  180
```

*Logic:* The `CAR` frame **inherits** `wheels` and `engine` from `VEHICLE` but **overrides** the `max_speed` default (180 km/h for cars, vs 120 for generic vehicles).

**Step 3 — Declare the Instance Frame (MY_CAR)**

```
FRAME: MY_CAR
  Instance-Of: CAR
  slot: owner
    value:    "John"
  slot: color
    value:    "Blue"
    type:     string
  slot: fuel_type             # OVERRIDDEN
    value:    hybrid
  slot: year_of_make
    value:    2022
```

*Logic:* `MY_CAR` inherits from `CAR` → `VEHICLE` (transitive inheritance), but explicitly overrides `fuel_type` to `hybrid`.

**Step 4 — Resolution Walkthrough (Inheritance Chain Evaluation)**

When a query asks *"What is the max_speed of MY_CAR?"*, the system executes:

$$
\text{Resolve}(MY\_CAR.max\_speed) = \text{Search}(\{MY\_CAR, CAR, VEHICLE\}, \text{depth-first left-to-right})
$$

The traversal proceeds:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A:} \quad & \text{Check } MY\_CAR.\text{max\_speed} \rightarrow \text{NOT FOUND} \\
\text{Step B:} \quad & \text{Check } CAR.\text{max\_speed} \rightarrow \text{FOUND (default = 180)} \\
\text{Conclusion:} \quad & \text{Return } 180 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Inheritance eliminates redundancy. We never had to repeat `wheels = 4` in `MY_CAR` because the value is pulled from the ancestor frame.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Procedural Attachment (Daemon / If-Needed)

**Problem Statement:**
Design an `EMPLOYEE` frame with an `IF-NEEDED` daemon that computes the `gross_salary` slot as `basic + hra + da`.

**Frame Construction:**

```
FRAME: EMPLOYEE
  AKO:              PERSON
  slot: basic
    value:          30000
    type:           integer
  slot: hra
    value:          0.40 * basic
    if-needed:      COMPUTE_HRA()
  slot: da
    value:          0.10 * basic
    if-needed:      COMPUTE_DA()
  slot: gross_salary
    if-needed:      CALC_GROSS(basic, hra, da)
```

*Logic:* When the expert system requires `gross_salary`, the `IF-NEEDED` daemon is invoked, which in turn triggers the daemons for `hra` and `da`, producing the final computed value.

**Pseudocode of the daemon logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CALC\_GROSS}(b, h, d) &= b + h + d \\
&= b + (0.40 \cdot b) + (0.10 \cdot b) \\
&= b \cdot (1 + 0.40 + 0.10) \\
&= 1.50 \cdot b
\end{aligned}
$$

So for `basic = 30000`:

$$
\text{gross\_salary} = 1.50 \times 30000 = 45000
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU questions often ask you to **trace a daemon chain**. Always show: (1) which slot triggers the daemon, (2) what variables it accesses, (3) what it returns, and (4) any side effects (like `IF-ADDED` logging).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison with Other KR Schemes

| **Criterion** | **Predicate Logic** | **Semantic Network** | **Frame** | **Production Rule** |
|---------------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| Structure | Formal (FOL) | Graph (nodes + arcs) | Record (slots + facets) | IF-THEN clauses |
| Default Reasoning | Weak | Possible via inheritance | **Strong (default facets)** | Weak |
| Procedural Knowledge | Hard | Hard | **Easy (daemons)** | Native |
| Inheritance | None (manual) | Implicit (IS-A arc) | **Explicit + Multiple** | None |
| Best Use Case | Mathematical proof | Concept mapping | Object-oriented domains | Heuristic control |
| Brittleness | High | Medium | **Low** | High |

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Multi-Inheritance Conflict Resolution

**Problem Statement:**
A frame `AMPHIBIOUS_VEHICLE` inherits from BOTH `CAR` and `BOAT`. Both parents define a `max_speed` default. Resolve the conflict using the **Depth-First, Left-to-Right (DFLR)** rule.

**Declaration Order:**

```
FRAME: AMPHIBIOUS_VEHICLE
  AKO:  CAR, BOAT          # CAR listed FIRST
```

**Conflict Resolution (KTU Exam Standard):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rule:} \quad & \text{DFLR: traverse first-listed parent fully before second.} \\
\text{Traversal Path:} \quad & AMPHIBIOUS\_VEHICLE \rightarrow CAR \rightarrow VEHICLE \rightarrow BOAT \\
\text{Lookup:} \quad & \text{max\_speed} \text{ found in } CAR \text{ first} = 180 \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \text{Use } 180 \text{ km/h from CAR (CAR dominates)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** If the question says *"CAR is the dominant parent"*, the answer is straightforward. If it says *"List order is BOAT, CAR"*, then `BOAT`'s `max_speed` wins. Always **re-state the dominance rule** in your answer to earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Internal Anatomy of a Single Frame

This diagram maps the **structural anatomy** of a generic frame, showing the relationship between the frame name, its slots, and the various facet types.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["FRAME NAME : VEHICLE"] --> B["AKO LINK : OBJECT"]
    A --> C["SLOT 1 : wheels"]
    A --> D["SLOT 2 : engine"]
    A --> E["SLOT 3 : max_speed"]

    C --> C1["VALUE : 4"]
    C --> C2["TYPE : integer"]
    C --> C3["DEFAULT : 4"]

    D --> D1["VALUE : yes"]
    D --> D2["TYPE : boolean"]

    E --> E1["DEFAULT : 120"]
    E --> E2["RANGE : 0 to 500"]
    E --> E3["IF NEEDED : COMPUTE MAX"]
    E --> E4["IF ADDED : LOG EVENT"]
```

**Interpretation:** Each slot branches into multiple facets. The presence of `IF-NEEDED` and `IF-ADDED` represents the **procedural attachment** capability unique to frames.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Class / Instance Inheritance Hierarchy

This diagram illustrates the **multi-level inheritance chain** and demonstrates how an instance frame inherits attributes transitively from its class and super-class frames.

```mermaid
graph TD
    obj["OBJECT (Root)"]
    veh["VEHICLE (Class)"]
    car["CAR (Subclass)"]
    boat["BOAT (Subclass)"]
    amph["AMPHIBIOUS VEHICLE (Multiple Inherit)"]
    myc["MY CAR (Instance)"]

    obj --> veh
    veh --> car
    veh --> boat
    car --> amph
    boat --> amph
    car --> myc
```

**Interpretation:**
- `MY_CAR` inherits transitively through `CAR → VEHICLE → OBJECT`.
- `AMPHIBIOUS_VEHICLE` demonstrates **multiple inheritance** from both `CAR` and `BOAT`, requiring the **DFLR conflict resolution rule** described in Section 3.4.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Daemon Activation Flow (Procedural Attachment)

This flow diagram shows how an `IF-NEEDED` daemon is triggered during a query, and how it may cascade to invoke other daemons.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q["User Query : gross_salary"] --> S1{"EMPLOYEE.gross_salary has IF NEEDED?"}
    S1 -- YES --> S2["Invoke CALC GROSS procedure"]
    S1 -- NO --> S3["Return static value"]

    S2 --> T1["Need basic value"]
    T1 --> T2{"Has IF NEEDED?"}
    T2 -- NO --> T3["Use stored basic = 30000"]

    S2 --> U1["Need hra value"]
    U1 --> U2{"Has IF NEEDED?"}
    U2 -- YES --> U3["Invoke COMPUTE HRA"]
    U3 --> U4["Return 0.40 x 30000 = 12000"]

    S2 --> V1["Need da value"]
    V1 --> V2{"Has IF NEEDED?"}
    V2 -- YES --> V3["Invoke COMPUTE DA"]
    V3 --> V4["Return 0.10 x 30000 = 3000"]

    T3 --> SUM["Sum b plus h plus d"]
    U4 --> SUM
    V4 --> SUM
    SUM --> R["Return gross salary = 45000"]
```

**Interpretation:** This block-level functional flow captures the **lazy evaluation** mechanism of frames — values are computed only when queried, not stored statically.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Frame System Architecture (Overall)

This is a **high-level architecture diagram** showing how a complete frame-based knowledge base is organized in an expert system.

```mermaid
graph LR
    UI["User Interface"] --> IE["Inference Engine"]
    IE --> FS["Frame Storage KB"]
    IE --> DM["Daemon Processor"]
    FS --> F1["Class Frames"]
    FS --> F2["Instance Frames"]
    FS --> F3["Meta Frames"]
    DM --> P1["IF NEEDED Daemons"]
    DM --> P2["IF ADDED Daemons"]
    DM --> P3["IF REMOVED Daemons"]
    IE --> EX["Explanation Module"]
    EX --> UI
```

**Interpretation:** The inference engine queries the frame storage, which contains different frame categories. Daemon processors handle procedural attachments. The explanation module returns reasoning traces to the user — a hallmark of expert systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a Frame. List and explain any four types of facets used in frames.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Frame** is a knowledge representation structure that organizes information about a stereotyped object or situation into a collection of **slots** and **fillers**, where each slot can have **facets** describing its properties, constraints, default values, and procedures.

The four main types of facets are:

1. **Value Facet** — Stores the actual concrete value of the slot.
2. **Default Facet** — Stores a default value used when no specific value is provided (enables default reasoning).
3. **If-Needed Facet** — A procedure (daemon) that is invoked only when the slot's value is requested and not currently filled.
4. **If-Added Facet** — A procedure triggered automatically when a new value is added to the slot (used for consistency checks, logging, or cascading updates).

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Note (3 marks):** Definition = 1 mark, Four facets = 0.5 each. Always draw a small example frame for bonus clarity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]**
*With a suitable example, explain the concept of inheritance in frame-based systems.*

**Model Answer:**

**Inheritance** is a mechanism by which a frame automatically acquires the slots, default values, and procedures of one or more **parent frames**, thereby avoiding redundant knowledge representation.

**Example:**

```
FRAME: BIRD         (parent)
  slot: can_fly     value: yes
  slot: legs        value: 2

FRAME: PENGUIN     (child, AKO BIRD)
  slot: can_fly     default: no      # OVERRIDDEN
  slot: habitat     value: antarctica
```

`PENGUIN` inherits `legs = 2` from `BIRD` but **overrides** `can_fly = no`, demonstrating the power of inheritance combined with exception handling.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3(a) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (Module 1 Choice A)**

*With neat diagrams and examples, explain the structure of a frame. Discuss the various types of facets used in frames in detail.*

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Frame Structure (3 marks)**

A frame is a structured record consisting of:
- **Frame Name** — Unique identifier
- **AKO / IS-A Link** — Connects to parent frame
- **Slots** — Attributes of the entity
- **Facets** — Constraints and procedures on slots
- **Filler** — The actual value stored

**Step 2 — Diagram of Generic Frame (2 marks)**

```
+-------------------------------------+
| FRAME NAME : <identifier>           |
| AKO          : <parent_frame>       |
+-------------------------------------+
| SLOT 1 : <slot_name>                |
|   - value    : <filler>             |
|   - type     : <data_type>          |
|   - default  : <default_value>      |
|   - range    : [min, max]           |
|   - if-needed: <procedure>          |
+-------------------------------------+
| SLOT 2 : <slot_name>                |
|   - ...                             |
+-------------------------------------+
```

**Step 3 — Detailed Facet Description (7 marks)**

| Facet Type | Purpose | Example |
|------------|---------|---------|
| `value` | Direct value | `value: blue` |
| `default` | Used when slot is empty | `default: 4` |
| `type` | Enforces data type | `type: integer` |
| `range` | Restricts legal values | `range: [0, 150]` |
| `if-needed` | Lazy computation | `if-needed: COMPUTE_AGE()` |
| `if-added` | Triggered on value insertion | `if-added: VALIDATE()` |
| `if-deleted` | Triggered on value removal | `if-deleted: CASCADE()` |
| `documentation` | Human-readable comment | `doc: "Age in years"` |

**Step 4 — Real-World Example (2 marks)**

```
FRAME: STUDENT
  AKO:           PERSON
  slot: roll_no
    type:        integer
    range:       [1, 9999]
  slot: cgpa
    type:        float
    range:       [0.0, 10.0]
    default:     0.0
    if-needed:   COMPUTE_CGPA()
    if-added:    CHECK_ELIGIBILITY()
  slot: department
    range:       {CSE, ECE, MECH, CIVIL}
    default:     CSE
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students confuse **facets** with **slots**. Remember — slots are the *attributes* (like `cgpa`), facets are the *properties of those attributes* (like `range: [0,10]`). Drawing the wrong hierarchy in the diagram will cost you 2-3 marks.

---

**Q3(b) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model] (Module 1 Choice B)**

*Explain the concepts of class frames, instance frames, and inheritance in frame-based systems. Demonstrate multiple inheritance with a worked example showing conflict resolution.*

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Class Frames (3 marks)**

A **class frame** describes a *category* of objects. It contains properties common to all members of the class.

**Example:**
```
FRAME: MAMMAL
  AKO:         ANIMAL
  slot: warm_blooded   value: yes
  slot: has_fur        default: yes
  slot: feeds_milk     value: yes
```

**Step 2 — Instance Frames (3 marks)**

An **instance frame** represents a *specific* member of a class. It inherits from one or more class frames and provides concrete values.

**Example:**
```
FRAME: WHALE_01
  Instance-Of: MAMMAL
  slot: name           value: "Blue"
  slot: habitat        value: ocean
  slot: has_fur        value: no       # OVERRIDDEN
```

**Step 3 — Inheritance Mechanism (4 marks)**

Inheritance works in three modes:
1. **Single Inheritance** — One parent frame.
2. **Multiple Inheritance** — Many parent frames.
3. **Default Inheritance** — Inherits slot *definitions* and *defaults* (not necessarily values).

The lookup order uses the **Depth-First, Left-to-Right (DFLR)** traversal.

**Step 4 — Multiple Inheritance Worked Example (4 marks)**

```
FRAME: FLYING_MAMMAL
  AKO:   MAMMAL, BIRD        # Multiple parents

FRAME: MAMMAL
  slot: body_covering   default: fur

FRAME: BIRD
  slot: body_covering   default: feathers
```

**Conflict Resolution:**

When we query `FLYING_MAMMAL.body_covering`:
- The system first checks `MAMMAL` (leftmost in AKO list).
- Finds `body_covering = fur`.
- **Result:** `fur` is returned (MAMMAL dominates BIRD under DFLR rule).

**Resolution Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Query:} \quad & \text{FLYING\_MAMMAL.body\_covering} \\
\text{Search Order:} \quad & \text{FLYING\_MAMMAL} \rightarrow \text{MAMMAL} \rightarrow \text{ANIMAL} \rightarrow \text{BIRD} \rightarrow \text{ANIMAL} \\
\text{First Match:} \quad & \text{MAMMAL.body\_covering} = \text{fur} \\
\text{Final Answer:} \quad & \text{fur (MAMMAL dominates)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (14-Mark Question):** A common error is forgetting to **specify which dominance rule** the system uses. Always state the rule (DFLR, specificity, or explicit user-defined) before performing the resolution. Examiners allocate 2 marks purely for naming the rule.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Frames (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** A frame is a structured knowledge representation consisting of slots, facets, and values, introduced by Minsky (1975).
- **Core Components:** Frame Name, AKO link, Slots, Facets, Fillers, Daemons.
- **Facet Types:** value, default, type, range, if-needed, if-added, if-deleted, documentation.
- **Inheritance Types:** Single, Multiple, Default.
- **Conflict Resolution:** Depth-First Left-to-Right (DFLR) is the standard rule.
- **Class vs Instance:** Class = category; Instance = specific member.
- **Procedural Attachment (Daemons):** IF-NEEDED, IF-ADDED, IF-REMOVED, IF-MODIFIED.
- **Advantages:** Encapsulation, default reasoning, modularity, easy knowledge base extension.
- **Disadvantages:** Complex to maintain, rigid structure, slow inference for large hierarchies.
- **KTU Frequently Tested:** Definition, Facet types, Inheritance examples, Daemon explanation, Conflict resolution.
- **Comparison Memory Aid:** Frames ≈ OOP Classes; Slots ≈ Attributes; Daemons ≈ Methods; Inheritance ≈ Class Inheritance.

> **End of Module 1 Topic: Frames — KTU 2024 Scheme Study Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logic and Sets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT522 - EXPERT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Logic and Sets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Logic?

In the context of **Expert Systems**, **Logic** is a formal, mathematical language used to represent knowledge and perform automated reasoning. It allows an expert system to manipulate facts and rules to derive new conclusions from existing knowledge, mimicking the deductive reasoning of a human expert.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Logic is the study of the principles of valid inference and demonstrative reasoning. In Artificial Intelligence and Expert Systems, it provides the syntactic and semantic framework for representing knowledge using well-formed formulas (WFFs) and deriving conclusions through formal proof procedures.*

### 1.1.1 Propositional Logic (Sentential Logic)

A **Proposition** is a declarative statement that is either unambiguously **True (T)** or **False (F)** — never both, never neither.

Examples:
- ✅ "The patient has a fever." (Proposition — can be T or F)
- ❌ "What time is it?" (Not a proposition — it's a question)
- ❌ "x + 3 = 7" (Not a proposition unless x is defined — it becomes a *predicate*)

### 1.1.2 Predicate Logic (First-Order Logic - FOL)

While propositional logic treats statements as atomic, **Predicate Logic** breaks them into:
- **Objects** (e.g., patient, disease, drug)
- **Predicates** (e.g., *HasFever(patient)*)
- **Quantifiers** (Universal `∀`, Existential `∃`)

This is the backbone of languages like **Prolog**, used to build expert systems.

### 1.1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Logic as a Courtroom 🏛️

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:**
> Think of logic as a courtroom trial.
> - The **facts** (evidence presented) are like **propositions**.
> - The **rules of law** (e.g., "If a person is guilty, then there must be evidence") are like **logical rules / implications**.
> - The **judge** acts as the **inference engine** — applying rules to facts to reach a **verdict** (new conclusion).
> Just as a judge cannot accept "I feel they are guilty" without logical evidence, an expert system cannot conclude anything without formally valid logical inference.

---

## 1.2 What is a Set?

A **Set** is a well-defined, unordered collection of distinct objects (called **elements** or **members**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *A set is a collection of distinct objects considered as an object in itself. The objects in a set are called its elements or members. The relationship "x is an element of A" is denoted as x ∈ A. If x is not an element, it is denoted as x ∉ A.*

### 1.2.1 Notation

Let A be a set. Then we write:

$$
A = \{ x \mid P(x) \}
$$

where P(x) is a property that characterizes the members of A.

Example:
- $A = \{ 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 \}$ (Roster form)
- $A = \{ x \mid x \text{ is a prime number less than 15} \}$ (Set-builder form)

### 1.2.2 Conceptual Analogy — Sets as Filing Cabinets 🗄️

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:**
> Think of a **set** as a labeled filing cabinet drawer.
> - Each **drawer contains** related items (the elements).
> - You can **combine drawers** (Union, A ∪ B) to create a bigger collection.
> - You can find **common items** between drawers (Intersection, A ∩ B).
> - You can **remove** items (Difference, A − B).
> - The **empty drawer** is the empty set **∅**.
> - The **entire office** is the universal set **U**.
> In an expert system, sets help classify diseases, symptoms, drugs, patients — and rules operate over these sets to draw medical or diagnostic conclusions.

---

## 1.3 Why Logic & Sets are Vital for Expert Systems

| Aspect | Role in Expert System |
|---|---|
| **Propositional Logic** | Encodes simple IF–THEN rules (e.g., IF fever AND cough THEN flu) |
| **Predicate Logic (FOL)** | Encodes complex relational knowledge (e.g., ∀x (Patient(x) ∧ HasSymptom(x,Fever) → HasDisease(x,Infection))) |
| **Inference Rules** | Drive the *inference engine* to derive new facts from existing ones |
| **Set Theory** | Classifies knowledge into domains, supports taxonomic reasoning (subsets, supersets) |
| **Set Operations** | Underpin rule matching, pattern recognition, and uncertainty handling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics & Symbols (Memorize!):**
> - Truth values: **T = 1 = True**, **F = 0 = False**
> - Cardinality of a finite set S: **|S|** (number of elements)
> - Empty set: **∅** or **{ }**
> - Universal set: **U**
> - Universal quantifier: **∀** (means "for all")
> - Existential quantifier: **∃** (means "there exists")
> - Logical AND: **∧**, Logical OR: **∨**, Logical NOT: **¬** or **~**
> - Implication: **→**, Biconditional: **↔**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Propositional Logic — The Five Fundamental Connectives

Let P and Q be propositions. We form compound propositions using **logical connectives**:

| Symbol | Connective | Mnemonic | KTU Exam Favorite |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| ¬P | NOT (Negation) | Flips the truth value | $\neg(\neg P) \equiv P$ |
| P ∧ Q | AND (Conjunction) | True only if BOTH true | Used in rule antecedents |
| P ∨ Q | OR (Disjunction) | True if AT LEAST ONE true | Used in disjunction of conditions |
| P → Q | IMPLIES (Conditional) | False ONLY when P is True & Q is False | Forms the core of IF–THEN rules |
| P ↔ Q | IF AND ONLY IF (Biconditional) | True when both have the SAME truth value | Used for definitions |

### 2.1.1 Truth Tables — The Heart of Propositional Logic

**Truth Table for the Five Connectives:**

| P | Q | ¬P | P ∧ Q | P ∨ Q | P → Q | P ↔ Q |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| T | T | F | T | T | T | T |
| T | F | F | F | T | **F** | F |
| F | T | T | F | T | T | F |
| F | F | T | F | F | T | T |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:**
> The only case where **P → Q** is **FALSE** is when the hypothesis P is **True** but the conclusion Q is **False**. This is the **counterexample** to an implication. Every expert system rule verification depends on this truth-functional behavior.

### 2.1.2 Tautology, Contradiction, and Contingency

- **Tautology (T)**: A compound proposition that is **always True** for all possible truth assignments.
  - Example: $P \lor \neg P$ (Law of Excluded Middle)

- **Contradiction (⊥)**: A compound proposition that is **always False**.
  - Example: $P \land \neg P$ (Law of Non-Contradiction)

- **Contingency**: A proposition that is **True for some** assignments and **False for others**.

---

## 2.2 Logical Equivalences — The KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

Two propositions are **logically equivalent** (written $P \equiv Q$) if they have the **identical truth table**. The following equivalences are tested *every year* in KTU exams:

| Law | AND form | OR form |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Identity** | $P \land T \equiv P$ | $P \lor F \equiv P$ |
| **Domination** | $P \land F \equiv F$ | $P \lor T \equiv T$ |
| **Idempotent** | $P \land P \equiv P$ | $P \lor P \equiv P$ |
| **Double Negation** | $\neg(\neg P) \equiv P$ | — |
| **Commutative** | $P \land Q \equiv Q \land P$ | $P \lor Q \equiv Q \lor P$ |
| **Associative** | $(P \land Q) \land R \equiv P \land (Q \land R)$ | $(P \lor Q) \lor R \equiv P \lor (Q \lor R)$ |
| **Distributive** | $P \lor (Q \land R) \equiv (P \lor Q) \land (P \lor R)$ | $P \land (Q \lor R) \equiv (P \land Q) \lor (P \land R)$ |
| **De Morgan's** | $\neg(P \land Q) \equiv \neg P \lor \neg Q$ | $\neg(P \lor Q) \equiv \neg P \land \neg Q$ |
| **Absorption** | $P \land (P \lor Q) \equiv P$ | $P \lor (P \land Q) \equiv P$ |
| **Negation** | $P \land \neg P \equiv F$ | $P \lor \neg P \equiv T$ |
| **Implication** | $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \lor Q$ | — |
| **Contrapositive** | $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg Q \rightarrow \neg P$ | — |
| **Exportation** | $(P \land Q) \rightarrow R \equiv P \rightarrow (Q \rightarrow R)$ | — |

---

## 2.3 Predicate Logic (First-Order Logic — FOL)

### 2.3.1 Quantifiers — The Heart of FOL

| Quantifier | Symbol | Read As | Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Universal** | $\forall x$ | "For all x" | $P(x)$ is true for **every** x in the domain |
| **Existential** | $\exists x$ | "There exists an x" | $P(x)$ is true for **at least one** x in the domain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantifier Distribution Rules (Frequently Tested!):**
> - $\forall x\, (P(x) \land Q(x)) \equiv \forall x\, P(x) \land \forall x\, Q(x)$
> - $\exists x\, (P(x) \lor Q(x)) \equiv \exists x\, P(x) \lor \exists x\, Q(x)$
> - $\neg \forall x\, P(x) \equiv \exists x\, \neg P(x)$ **(Negation of Universal)**
> - $\neg \exists x\, P(x) \equiv \forall x\, \neg P(x)$ **(Negation of Existential)**

### 2.3.2 Example — FOL in Medical Expert System

> "Every patient with fever and cough has an infection."
> 
> $$\forall x\, \big( \text{Patient}(x) \land \text{HasFever}(x) \land \text{HasCough}(x) \rightarrow \text{HasInfection}(x) \big)$$

---

## 2.4 Inference Rules — The Engine of Expert Systems

These rules allow the inference engine to derive new facts. They are the **WHY** of expert systems.

| Rule | Form | In Words |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Modus Ponens** | $P,\ P \rightarrow Q \therefore Q$ | If P is true and P→Q, then Q is true |
| **Modus Tollens** | $\neg Q,\ P \rightarrow Q \therefore \neg P$ | If Q is false and P→Q, then P is false |
| **Hypothetical Syllogism** | $P \rightarrow Q,\ Q \rightarrow R \therefore P \rightarrow R$ | Chain implications |
| **Disjunctive Syllogism** | $P \lor Q,\ \neg P \therefore Q$ | Eliminate one disjunct |
| **Addition** | $P \therefore P \lor Q$ | Widen the conclusion |
| **Simplification** | $P \land Q \therefore P$ | Drop a conjunct |
| **Resolution** | $P \lor Q,\ \neg Q \lor R \therefore P \lor R$ | The most powerful — used in automated theorem provers |

---

## 2.5 Set Theory — Core Definitions

| Concept | Notation | Definition |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Element** | $x \in A$ | x belongs to set A |
| **Subset** | $A \subseteq B$ | Every element of A is in B |
| **Proper Subset** | $A \subset B$ | $A \subseteq B$ and $A \neq B$ |
| **Equality** | $A = B$ | $A \subseteq B$ and $B \subseteq A$ |
| **Empty Set** | $\emptyset$ | The set with no elements |
| **Universal Set** | $U$ | The set of all elements under consideration |
| **Power Set** | $\mathcal{P}(A)$ | Set of all subsets of A |
| **Cardinality** | $\vert A \vert$ | Number of elements in finite set A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cardinality Formula for Power Set:**
> 
> $$\vert \mathcal{P}(A) \vert = 2^{\vert A \vert}$$
> 
> Example: If $|A| = 3$, then $|\mathcal{P}(A)| = 2^3 = 8$.

---

## 2.6 Set Operations — The Engine of Classification

Let A and B be subsets of a universal set U.

| Operation | Notation | Definition | Venn Diagram Region |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Union** | $A \cup B$ | All elements in A or B (or both) | Both circles filled |
| **Intersection** | $A \cap B$ | All elements common to both A and B | Overlap region |
| **Difference** | $A - B$ | Elements in A but not in B | A minus the overlap |
| **Complement** | $\bar{A}$ (or $A^c$) | All elements of U not in A | Everything outside A |
| **Symmetric Difference** | $A \oplus B$ | Elements in A or B but not both | Both circles minus overlap |

---

## 2.7 Set Identities — The Mirror Image of Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** *Every logical equivalence has a set-theoretic counterpart.* Replace **∧ by ∩**, **∨ by ∪**, **T by U**, **F by ∅**, and **¬ by complement.**

| Law | Set Form |
|:---|:---|
| **Identity** | $A \cap U = A,\quad A \cup \emptyset = A$ |
| **Domination** | $A \cap \emptyset = \emptyset,\quad A \cup U = U$ |
| **Idempotent** | $A \cap A = A,\quad A \cup A = A$ |
| **Complement** | $A \cup \bar{A} = U,\quad A \cap \bar{A} = \emptyset$ |
| **Commutative** | $A \cap B = B \cap A,\quad A \cup B = B \cup A$ |
| **Associative** | $(A \cap B) \cap C = A \cap (B \cap C)$ |
| **Distributive** | $A \cup (B \cap C) = (A \cup B) \cap (A \cup C)$ |
| **De Morgan's** | $\overline{A \cap B} = \bar{A} \cup \bar{B},\quad \overline{A \cup B} = \bar{A} \cap \bar{B}$ |
| **Absorption** | $A \cap (A \cup B) = A,\quad A \cup (A \cap B) = A$ |
| **Double Complement** | $\overline{\bar{A}} = A$ |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Medical Diagnosis Systems** (MYCIN) | Use IF–THEN rules in propositional/FOL to diagnose bacterial infections |
| **Fault Diagnosis in Manufacturing** | Set operations classify which components belong to which subsystem |
| **Legal Expert Systems** | Use inference rules (Modus Ponens) to apply statutes to case facts |
| **Configuration Systems** (e.g., computer assembly) | Set theory + logic verifies component compatibility |
| **Fuzzy Logic Controllers** | Generalization of propositional logic to handle uncertainty (0 to 1) |
| **Knowledge Base Validation** | Set operations (∩, ∪) merge and verify multiple rule bases |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Implication Equivalence $(P \rightarrow Q) \equiv (\neg P \lor Q)$

We must prove that $P \rightarrow Q$ and $\neg P \lor Q$ have identical truth tables.

**Step-by-step construction:**

By definition, $P \rightarrow Q$ is **false only** when P is true and Q is false; true otherwise.

Now compute $\neg P \lor Q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \lor Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Truth Table Comparison:**

| P | Q | $\neg P$ | $\neg P \lor Q$ | $P \rightarrow Q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| T | T | F | F ∨ T = **T** | **T** |
| T | F | F | F ∨ F = **F** | **F** |
| F | T | T | T ∨ T = **T** | **T** |
| F | F | T | T ∨ F = **T** | **T** |

The two rightmost columns are **identical** (TTFT), therefore:

$$
P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \lor Q \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** An IF–THEN rule in an expert system can be rewritten as a disjunction, which is critical for resolution-based inference engines.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — De Morgan's Law for Sets: $\overline{A \cup B} = \bar{A} \cap \bar{B}$

**Step 1:** To prove set equality, we must show that every element of $\overline{A \cup B}$ is also in $\bar{A} \cap \bar{B}$, and vice versa.

**Step 2 (Forward Direction $\subseteq$):**  
Let $x \in \overline{A \cup B}$. By definition of complement:

$$
x \notin A \cup B
$$

Since $x$ is not in $A \cup B$, $x$ cannot be in $A$ and cannot be in $B$:

$$
x \notin A \quad \text{AND} \quad x \notin B
$$

By definition of complement, this means $x \in \bar{A}$ and $x \in \bar{B}$. Therefore:

$$
x \in \bar{A} \cap \bar{B}
$$

**Step 3 (Reverse Direction $\supseteq$):**  
Let $x \in \bar{A} \cap \bar{B}$. Then:

$$
x \in \bar{A} \quad \text{AND} \quad x \in \bar{B}
$$

By complement definition: $x \notin A$ and $x \notin B$. So $x$ cannot be in $A \cup B$, hence:

$$
x \in \overline{A \cup B}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\overline{A \cup B} = \bar{A} \cap \bar{B} \quad \blacksquare
$$

The analogous derivation holds for $\overline{A \cap B} = \bar{A} \cup \bar{B}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Modus Ponens Validity via Truth Table

We want to prove that whenever premises $P$ and $P \rightarrow Q$ are both true, the conclusion $Q$ must be true.

**Step-by-step proof:**

| P | Q | $P \rightarrow Q$ | P ∧ (P → Q) | Conclusion Q |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| T | T | T | **T** | **T** ✓ |
| T | F | **F** | **F** | (premise P→Q fails, rule inapplicable) |
| F | T | T | **F** | (premise P fails, rule inapplicable) |
| F | F | T | **F** | (premise P fails, rule inapplicable) |

The **only row** where both premises $P$ and $P \rightarrow Q$ are simultaneously true is Row 1, where $Q = T$.

Therefore Modus Ponens is **logically valid**:

$$
P,\ P \rightarrow Q \;\therefore\; Q \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Engineering Significance:** This is the foundational inference step used in every forward-chaining expert system (e.g., CLIPS, Jess).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Quantifier Negation

**Claim:** $\neg \forall x\, P(x) \equiv \exists x\, \neg P(x)$

**Proof by logical reasoning:**

> "It is **not** the case that P(x) holds for **every** x"

means exactly:

> "There **exists at least one** x for which P(x) **does not** hold"

Hence:

$$
\neg \forall x\, P(x) \equiv \exists x\, \neg P(x)
$$

By the same logic:

$$
\neg \exists x\, P(x) \equiv \forall x\, \neg P(x)
$$

**Verification Example:**  
Original: "Not all birds can fly."  
Equivalent: "There exists a bird that cannot fly."  
Both convey the same meaning. ✓

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Power Set Cardinality (Combinatorial Proof)

**Claim:** If $A$ has $n$ elements, then $\mathcal{P}(A)$ has $2^n$ elements.

**Proof:** Each subset of A is constructed by deciding, for **each element** of A, whether to **include** it (1) or **exclude** it (0). For $n$ elements, the number of binary decision sequences is:

$$
2 \times 2 \times 2 \times \cdots \times 2 \quad (n \text{ times}) = 2^n
$$

Therefore:

$$
\vert \mathcal{P}(A) \vert = 2^{\vert A \vert} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Example:** If $A = \{a, b, c\}$, then $|\mathcal{P}(A)| = 2^3 = 8$ subsets:
$\emptyset,\ \{a\},\ \{b\},\ \{c\},\ \{a,b\},\ \{a,c\},\ \{b,c\},\ \{a,b,c\}$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Logical Connectives vs. Set Operations

| Logical Operation | Set-Theoretic Analog | Truth Value | Element Inclusion |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| AND ($\land$) | Intersection ($\cap$) | True when both True | Element in both sets |
| OR ($\lor$) | Union ($\cup$) | True when at least one True | Element in either set |
| NOT ($\neg$) | Complement ($\bar{A}$) | Flips True/False | Element outside the set |
| IMPLIES ($\rightarrow$) | $\bar{A} \cup B$ | Equivalent to $\neg A \lor B$ | — |
| Biconditional ($\leftrightarrow$) | $(A \cap B) \cup (\bar{A} \cap \bar{B})$ | True when both same | — |
| True (T) | Universal Set (U) | Always True | All elements |
| False (F) | Empty Set ($\emptyset$) | Always False | No elements |
| Tautology | Universe $U$ | Always holds | All elements in $U$ |
| Contradiction | Empty Set $\emptyset$ | Never holds | No elements |

This duality is known as **Boolean Algebra on Sets**, the algebraic backbone of expert system rule bases.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — Cardinality of Combined Sets

**Problem:** Given $|A| = 30$, $|B| = 25$, $|C| = 20$, $|A \cap B| = 8$, $|A \cap C| = 7$, $|B \cap C| = 5$, $|A \cap B \cap C| = 2$, find $|A \cup B \cup C|$.

**Step 1 — Apply Inclusion-Exclusion Principle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert A \cup B \cup C \vert = & \; \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert + \vert C \vert \\
& - \vert A \cap B \vert - \vert A \cap C \vert - \vert B \cap C \vert \\
& + \vert A \cap B \cap C \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert A \cup B \cup C \vert = & \; 30 + 25 + 20 - 8 - 7 - 5 + 2 \\
= & \; 75 - 20 + 2 \\
= & \; 57
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $|A \cup B \cup C| = 57$.

**Expert System Interpretation:** If A, B, C represent sets of patients with three overlapping symptoms, then 57 distinct patients exhibit at least one of these symptoms — used in epidemiological reasoning.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a proposition. State and explain the five logical connectives with truth tables.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **proposition** is a declarative statement that is either **True (T)** or **False (F)**, but not both. The five logical connectives are:

| Connective | Symbol | Truth Condition |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Negation | $\neg P$ | True iff P is False |
| Conjunction | $P \land Q$ | True iff both P and Q are True |
| Disjunction | $P \lor Q$ | True iff at least one of P, Q is True |
| Implication | $P \rightarrow Q$ | False only when P is True and Q is False |
| Biconditional | $P \leftrightarrow Q$ | True iff P and Q have the same truth value |

**Truth Table (compact form):**

| P | Q | $\neg P$ | $P \land Q$ | $P \lor Q$ | $P \rightarrow Q$ | $P \leftrightarrow Q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| T | T | F | T | T | T | T |
| T | F | F | F | T | F | F |
| F | T | T | F | T | T | F |
| F | F | T | F | F | T | T |

**(3 Marks — Complete)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and prove De Morgan's Laws for sets.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**De Morgan's Laws for Sets:**

$$
\overline{A \cup B} = \bar{A} \cap \bar{B}
$$

$$
\overline{A \cap B} = \bar{A} \cup \bar{B}
$$

**Proof of First Law:**

Let $x \in \overline{A \cup B}$. Then $x \notin A \cup B$, which means $x \notin A$ AND $x \notin B$. Hence $x \in \bar{A}$ and $x \in \bar{B}$, so $x \in \bar{A} \cap \bar{B}$. The reverse inclusion follows similarly.

The second law is proved analogously using the dual reasoning.

**(3 Marks — Complete)**

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Provided — Answer ANY ONE full question.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the following with examples:
**(i)** Propositional Logic and Predicate Logic
**(ii)** Universal and Existential Quantifiers

**(b)** Translate the following statements into First-Order Logic (FOL):

**(i)** Every doctor who treats patients is experienced.
**(ii)** Some patients have allergies.
**(iii)** There is no patient who likes every medicine.
**(iv)** All birds can fly, except penguins.

Then apply **Modus Ponens** and **Modus Tollens** to derive one new fact in each case from a suitable premise.

---

### **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — (7 Marks)

**(i) Propositional Logic vs. Predicate Logic:**

- **Propositional Logic** treats entire declarative statements as atomic units. It uses letters like P, Q, R to represent whole propositions.
  - Example: $P = $ "It is raining", $Q = $ "The ground is wet". The rule "If it is raining then the ground is wet" is written $P \rightarrow Q$.

- **Predicate Logic (FOL)** breaks propositions into **predicates**, **objects**, and **quantifiers**, allowing expression of internal structure and relations.
  - Example: $\text{Raining}(t)$ represents "It is raining at time t", and $\text{Wet}(\text{Ground})$ represents the predicate of the ground being wet.

**(ii) Quantifiers:**

- **Universal Quantifier ($\forall$):** Asserts that a property holds for **every** object in the domain.
  - Example: $\forall x\, \text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \text{HasID}(x)$ — Every student has an ID.

- **Existential Quantifier ($\exists$):** Asserts that a property holds for **at least one** object in the domain.
  - Example: $\exists x\, \text{Student}(x) \land \text{Honors}(x)$ — Some student has honors.

#### Part (b) — (7 Marks)

**(i)** "Every doctor who treats patients is experienced."

Let D(x) = "x is a doctor", T(x) = "x treats patients", E(x) = "x is experienced".

$$
\forall x\, \big( D(x) \land T(x) \rightarrow E(x) \big)
$$

**(ii)** "Some patients have allergies."

Let P(x) = "x is a patient", A(x) = "x has allergies".

$$
\exists x\, \big( P(x) \land A(x) \big)
$$

**(iii)** "There is no patient who likes every medicine."

Let P(x) = "x is a patient", M(y) = "y is a medicine", L(x, y) = "x likes y".

$$
\neg \exists x\, \forall y\, \big( P(x) \land M(y) \rightarrow L(x, y) \big)
$$

Equivalently (by De Morgan's for quantifiers):

$$
\forall x\, \exists y\, \big( P(x) \land M(y) \land \neg L(x, y) \big)
$$

**(iv)** "All birds can fly, except penguins."

Let B(x) = "x is a bird", P(x) = "x is a penguin", F(x) = "x can fly".

$$
\forall x\, \big( B(x) \land \neg P(x) \rightarrow F(x) \big)
$$

**Applying Modus Ponens:**

- Premise 1: DrSmith is a doctor and treats patients. $D(\text{DrSmith}) \land T(\text{DrSmith})$
- Premise 2: $\forall x\, (D(x) \land T(x) \rightarrow E(x))$
- **Conclusion:** $E(\text{DrSmith})$ — DrSmith is experienced.

**Applying Modus Tollens:**

- Premise 1: $\neg E(\text{DrJones})$ — DrJones is not experienced.
- Premise 2: $\forall x\, (D(x) \land T(x) \rightarrow E(x))$
- **Conclusion:** $\neg(D(\text{DrJones}) \land T(\text{DrJones}))$ — DrJones does not treat patients (or is not a doctor).

**(14 Marks — Complete)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **Set Theory** with definitions of:
**(i)** Universal Set, Empty Set, Power Set, Subset
**(ii)** Prove De Morgan's Laws using Venn Diagrams.

**(b)** In a survey of 100 patients in a hospital:
- 50 have Fever (F)
- 40 have Cough (C)
- 30 have Headache (H)
- 20 have both Fever and Cough
- 15 have both Fever and Headache
- 10 have both Cough and Headache
- 5 have all three symptoms

**Find:**
**(i)** Number of patients with at least one symptom.
**(ii)** Number of patients with none of the symptoms.
**(iii)** Verify the results using set identities.

---

### **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — (7 Marks)

**(i) Key Set Definitions:**

- **Universal Set (U):** The set of **all** possible elements under consideration for a given context.
- **Empty Set ($\emptyset$):** The set with **no elements**; cardinality $|\emptyset| = 0$.
- **Power Set ($\mathcal{P}(A)$):** The set of **all subsets** of A. If $|A| = n$, then $|\mathcal{P}(A)| = 2^n$.
- **Subset ($A \subseteq B$):** Every element of A is also in B.

**(ii) De Morgan's Laws — Venn Diagram Proof:**

For $\overline{A \cup B} = \bar{A} \cap \bar{B}$: The shaded region outside both A and B is identical to the intersection of the regions outside A and outside B.

For $\overline{A \cap B} = \bar{A} \cup \bar{B}$: The shaded region outside the overlap of A and B equals the union of the regions outside A and outside B.

Both laws are visually verifiable by drawing two overlapping circles inside a rectangle representing U.

#### Part (b) — (7 Marks)

**Given:** $|U| = 100$, $|F| = 50$, $|C| = 40$, $|H| = 30$, $|F \cap C| = 20$, $|F \cap H| = 15$, $|C \cap H| = 10$, $|F \cap C \cap H| = 5$.

**(i) Apply Inclusion-Exclusion Principle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert F \cup C \cup H \vert = & \; \vert F \vert + \vert C \vert + \vert H \vert \\
& - \vert F \cap C \vert - \vert F \cap H \vert - \vert C \cap H \vert \\
& + \vert F \cap C \cap H \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
= & \; 50 + 40 + 30 - 20 - 15 - 10 + 5 \\
= & \; 120 - 45 + 5 \\
= & \; 80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Number of patients with at least one symptom = 80.**

**(ii) Number of patients with no symptom:**

$$
\vert \overline{F \cup C \cup H} \vert = \vert U \vert - \vert F \cup C \cup H \vert = 100 - 80 = 20
$$

**(iii) Verification using De Morgan's Identity:**

$$
\overline{F \cup C \cup H} = \bar{F} \cap \bar{C} \cap \bar{H}
$$

The 20 patients outside all three sets satisfy this identity. ✓

**(14 Marks — Complete)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks!**
> 1. **Confusing $\subset$ with $\subseteq$:** Many students write $A \subset B$ when they actually mean $A \subseteq B$. The former is **proper** subset (strict).
> 2. **Quantifier Negation Mistakes:** $\neg \forall x\, P(x) \neq \forall x\, \neg P(x)$. Correct: $\exists x\, \neg P(x)$.
> 3. **Implication Truth Table:** Students wrongly mark $P \rightarrow Q$ as False when $P$ is False. Remember: False → Anything is **True** (vacuous truth).
> 4. **Inclusion-Exclusion Sign Errors:** The third term in 3-set inclusion-exclusion is **+** (not −). Many students flip this.
> 5. **Set vs. Element Confusion:** Writing $\{1\} \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ is WRONG. Correct: $\{1\} \subseteq \{1, 2, 3\}$.
> 6. **Confusing $\in$ and $\subseteq$:** $x \in A$ means x is an element of A; $A \subseteq B$ means A is a subset of B.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🔑 High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> 
> ✅ **Propositional Logic:** Know all 5 connectives (¬, ∧, ∨, →, ↔) and their truth tables **by heart**.
> 
> ✅ **Predicate Logic:** Understand quantifier duality — $\neg\forall = \exists\neg$ and $\neg\exists = \forall\neg$.
> 
> ✅ **Logical Equivalences:** Memorize at minimum — De Morgan's, Distributive, Absorption, Implication, Contrapositive.
> 
> ✅ **Inference Rules:** Modus Ponens ($P, P \rightarrow Q \Rightarrow Q$) and Modus Tollens ($\neg Q, P \rightarrow Q \Rightarrow \neg P$) appear **every semester**.
> 
> ✅ **Resolution:** The most powerful inference rule — used in automated theorem provers and Prolog.
> 
> ✅ **Set Operations:** $\cup, \cap, -, \bar{A}$ — every KTU question uses at least 2 of these.
> 
> ✅ **Power Set Cardinality:** $|\mathcal{P}(A)| = 2^{|A|}$ — frequently asked as 2 or 3-mark question.
> 
> ✅ **De Morgan's Laws for Sets:** $\overline{A \cup B} = \bar{A} \cap \bar{B}$ and $\overline{A \cap B} = \bar{A} \cup \bar{B}$.
> 
> ✅ **Inclusion-Exclusion:** $|A \cup B \cup C| = \sum |A_i| - \sum |A_i \cap A_j| + |A \cap B \cap C|$.
> 
> ✅ **Boolean Duality:** Logic and Sets are isomorphic — replace $\land \to \cap$, $\lor \to \cup$, $T \to U$, $F \to \emptyset$, $\neg \to$ complement.
> 
> ✅ **Expert System Linkage:** Every IF–THEN rule is $P \rightarrow Q$; every inference step uses Modus Ponens; classification uses sets.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
