For large $n$, the dominant term is $h \approx \log_2 n$, which is the **logarithmic search complexity** of binary trees — a key reason expert systems use trees for fast lookup.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A semantic network representing animal taxonomy has 47 concepts (nodes). 6 of them are leaves (concrete instances). If the network is a tree, how many internal (non-leaf) nodes and how many edges does it have?

**Solution:**

Number of internal nodes:

$$n_{\text{internal}} = n - n_{\text{leaves}} = 47 - 6 = 41$$

Number of edges (tree property):

$$\vert E \vert = n - 1 = 47 - 1 = 46$$

Verify using the binary-tree-like leaf identity (only if the tree is a *full* $k$-ary tree — here we do not assume that, so the count stands on the tree property alone).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Knowledge Representation Stack

The diagram below maps how trees and graphs sit inside an expert system and how they are processed.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Query / Fact] --> B[Inference Engine]
    B --> C{Knowledge Base Type}
    C --> D1[Rule Base - IF THEN]
    C --> D2[Semantic Network - Graph]
    C --> D3[Frame System - Tree and Slot Net]
    C --> D4[Conceptual Graph - Sowa]
    D2 --> E1[Graph Traversal - BFS, DFS, A*]
    D3 --> E2[Tree Search - Match and Unify]
    D1 --> F[Forward / Backward Chaining]
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Explanation Facility]
    F --> H[Working Memory]
    G --> I[User Response]
    H --> I
```

## 4.2 Semantic Network Example (Animal Domain)

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[LivingThing] -->|ISA| A1[Animal]
    R -->|ISA| P1[Plant]
    A1 -->|ISA| M1[Mammal]
    A1 -->|ISA| B1[Bird]
    M1 -->|ISA| D1[Dog]
    M1 -->|ISA| C1[Cat]
    B1 -->|ISA| S1[Sparrow]
    D1 -->|HASCOLOR| COL[Brown]
    D1 -->|EATS| F1[Meat]
    C1 -->|HASCOLOR| COL2[White]
    S1 -->|CAN| F2[Fly]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `ISA` is an inheritance edge (Dog ISA Mammal ⇒ Dog inherits warm-blooded).
- `HASCOLOR`, `EATS`, `CAN` are semantic attribute edges.

## 4.3 Decision Tree for a Medical Expert System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Patient has fever?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Temperature > 101F?}
    Q1 -- No --> N1[Possible: Common Cold]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Cough present?}
    Q2 -- No --> N2[Possible: Mild Viral Fever]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Chest pain?}
    Q3 -- No --> N3[Possible: Flu]
    Q4 -- Yes --> N4[Diagnosis: Pneumonia]
    Q4 -- No --> N5[Diagnosis: Bronchitis]
```

## 4.4 Inheritance Network (Concept Hierarchy with Exceptions)

```mermaid
graph TD
    B[Bird] -->|ISA| S[Sparrow]
    B -->|ISA| P[Penguin]
    B -->|HABITAT| SKY[Air]
    P -->|HABITAT| W[Water]
    P -.->|OVERRIDE| SKY
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **dashed edge** from `Penguin` to `Air` represents an *override* edge — Penguins do not fly even though they inherit the `CAN FLY` property from `Bird`. This is the basis of **non-monotonic reasoning with inheritance networks** — a high-yield KTU Module 2 topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a semantic network. How is it different from a frame? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **semantic network** is a graph-based knowledge representation in which concepts are represented as **nodes** and the relationships between them as **labeled, directed edges**. It is primarily used to represent *associative* and *inheritance* knowledge.

**Difference from a frame:**

| Aspect | Semantic Network | Frame |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Flat graph of nodes and edges | Hierarchical tree of frames with slots |
| Granularity | Relation between two concepts | Object with named slots and fillers |
| Inheritance | Implicit via edge traversal | Explicit via `ISA` / `AKO` links |
| Typical use | Conceptual association | Structured object description |

(1 mark definition + 1 mark core distinction + 1 mark tabular comparison.)

---

### Q2. State the Handshaking Lemma. Why is it relevant to expert system graphs? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** For any undirected graph $G = (V, E)$,

$$\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$$

That is, the sum of the degrees of all vertices equals twice the number of edges.

**Relevance (2 marks):** In expert system semantic networks, this lemma is used to:
- Verify graph consistency after knowledge-base updates.
- Detect *dangling* or *unconnected* concepts (degree-zero nodes).
- Estimate the **relational density** of a knowledge base — a high average degree suggests a richly connected (and thus more inferentially powerful) knowledge base.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a semantic network. How does inheritance work in such a network? Illustrate with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define a tree. Prove that a tree with $n$ vertices has exactly $n-1$ edges. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

A semantic network is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V$ = set of *concepts* (e.g., objects, events, properties).
- $E$ = set of *labeled, directed edges* (e.g., `ISA`, `HASPART`, `EATS`).

**Diagram (refer Section 4.2 above):**
- `LivingThing → ISA → Animal`
- `Animal → ISA → Mammal`
- `Mammal → ISA → Dog`

**Inheritance Mechanism:**
- A child node inherits all properties of its ancestors via the `ISA` link.
- For example, `Dog ISA Mammal ISA Animal ISA LivingThing` ⇒ `Dog` inherits `is_alive`, `can_move`, `is_warm_blooded` (from Mammal), all the way up.
- If `Dog` has a `HASCOLOR: Brown`, this is local to `Dog` and not inherited upward.

**Exception Handling:** If `Penguin ISA Bird` but `Penguin CAN FLY = False`, an *override* edge is added — this is the basis of **non-monotonic inheritance**.

**Mark split:** 2 marks diagram, 2 marks definition, 2 marks inheritance explanation, 1 mark example.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Definition (1 mark):** A **tree** is a connected, acyclic graph. Equivalently, it is a graph in which there is exactly one simple path between any two vertices.

**Theorem (1 mark):** A tree with $n$ vertices has exactly $n-1$ edges.

**Proof (5 marks):**

*Base case:* For $n = 1$, $\vert E \vert = 0 = 1 - 1$. ✓

*Inductive hypothesis:* Assume the result holds for all trees with $k$ vertices.

*Inductive step:* Let $T$ be a tree with $k + 1$ vertices. Since $T$ has at least 2 vertices and is finite, it has at least one **leaf** $v$ (a vertex of degree 1). Remove $v$ and its incident edge to get $T' = T - \{v\}$.

$T'$ is still a tree (it remains connected and acyclic), and has $k$ vertices. By the inductive hypothesis, $\vert E(T') \vert = k - 1$.

Therefore:

$$\vert E(T) \vert = \vert E(T') \vert + 1 = (k - 1) + 1 = k = (k+1) - 1$$

Hence the result holds for $k+1$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** What is a frame-based system? With a suitable example, show how slots, facets, and inheritance operate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between BFS and DFS as graph-search strategies used inside an inference engine. When would you prefer each in a knowledge base of medical rules? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

A **frame** (Minsky, 1975) is a structured data record representing a stereotyped object or concept. Each frame has:
- **Frame name** (e.g., `Mammal`)
- **Slots** — named attributes (e.g., `body-covering`, `reproduction`, `habitat`)
- **Facets** — properties of slots (e.g., `value`, `default`, `if-needed`, `if-added`)
- **Inheritance links** — `ISA` (is-a) and `AKO` (a-kind-of)

**Example (3 marks):**

| Frame | Slot | Facet | Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mammal | body-covering | default | hair |
| Mammal | reproduction | default | live-birth |
| Dog ISA Mammal | body-covering | default | fur |
| Dog | sound | default | bark |

**How it works (2 marks):**
- When asked "Does a dog have hair?", the system first checks Dog's slots; if not present, follows `ISA Mammal` and returns `Mammal.body-covering = hair`.
- `if-needed` facets trigger procedures when a slot is queried but empty.
- `if-added` facets run side-effects when a slot is filled — used for forward chaining.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

| Criterion | BFS (Breadth-First Search) | DFS (Depth-First Search) |
|---|---|---|
| Data structure | Queue (FIFO) | Stack (LIFO) |
| Order | Explores level by level | Explores depth-wise |
| Optimality | Finds shortest path in unweighted graphs | Not optimal in general |
| Memory | High (stores all neighbours) | Low (only one path) |
| Termination on infinite graphs | Guaranteed if goal reachable | May loop forever |
| Use in medical KB | Diagnostic rules with shallow evidence (e.g., triage) | Deep causal chains (e.g., differential diagnosis) |

**Preference in medical expert systems (2 marks):**
- **Prefer BFS** when the diagnostic tree is shallow and the physician wants the *most common / shortest-rule-chain* explanation first.
- **Prefer DFS** when the search space is deep and we want to fully explore one line of reasoning (e.g., tracing a rare disease through multiple organ systems) before backtracking.

**Mark split:** 2 marks BFS definition/working, 2 marks DFS definition/working, 2 marks comparison table, 1 mark application.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse `Tree` with `Decision Tree`.** A decision tree is a *use* of a tree; a semantic tree is a different beast. Examiners deduct marks if the boundary is blurred.
> 2. **The property $n-1$ holds ONLY for connected acyclic graphs.** Many students wrongly apply it to forests (where $\vert E \vert = n - c$ for $c$ components) or to graphs with cycles.
> 3. **In inheritance questions, explicitly mention exception handling and non-monotonic reasoning.** KTU's Module 2 (2024 Scheme) explicitly tests *default reasoning with override edges*.
> 4. **Handshaking Lemma works only for undirected graphs.** Directed graphs use in-degree = out-degree sums.
> 5. **For semantic network diagrams, label every edge.** An unlabeled diagram loses 1–2 marks outright.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Graph** $G = (V, E)$; **Tree** = connected, acyclic graph; **Forest** = disjoint union of trees.
- **Tree edge count:** $\vert E \vert = n - 1$ (universal identity — memorize).
- **Handshaking Lemma:** $\sum \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$ (undirected).
- **Directed graphs:** Sum of in-degrees = Sum of out-degrees = $\vert E \vert$.
- **Inheritance** propagates from root to leaves via `ISA` / `AKO` edges.
- **Exception/override edges** enable non-monotonic reasoning.
- **Semantic Networks** = directed labeled graphs; **Frames** = tree-like slot-filler structures with inheritance.
- **BFS** uses a queue (FIFO), is optimal for shortest path; **DFS** uses a stack (LIFO), uses less memory.
- **Conceptual Graphs (Sowa)** unify logic and graph KR.
- **Knowledge Graphs** (modern descendant) power Google Search, Alexa, Watson.
- **High-yield keywords for KTU answer writing:** *inheritance, default, override, non-monotonic, semantic relation, slot, facet, search complexity $O(\log n)$ vs $O(n)$*.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "TRACI":** **T**ree has $n-1$ edges, **R**ooted trees have one root, **A**cyclic = no cycles, **C**onnected = one path, **I**nheritance follows parent-to-child edges.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State and Problem Spaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT522 - EXPERT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** State and Problem Spaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 2 — STATE AND PROBLEM SPACES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a **State**

In the formal language of Artificial Intelligence and Knowledge-Based Systems, a **state** is a discrete, well-defined representation of the configuration of a system at a specific instant in time. Every state encodes the complete set of variables, symbols, or facts required to fully describe the situation the problem-solver is currently in.

$$
S = \{ s_1, s_2, s_3, \dots, s_n \}
$$

where each $s_i$ is a symbolic, numeric, or logical descriptor of a single attribute of the world. The collection of *all* attributes at one moment is the state.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Canonical):**  
> A *State* is a snapshot of the problem's world at any point, capturing everything the solver needs to know to decide the next move.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of **State Space**

The **State Space** is the entire mathematical universe of all possible states that are *reachable* (or *legal*) for a given problem. If a problem has $n$ discrete variables each of which can take $k$ values, then in the worst case:

$$
\vert S_{total} \vert = k^n
$$

This set is rendered as a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where:
- Each vertex $v \in V$ corresponds to a state.
- Each edge $e \in E$ corresponds to a permitted transition (operator).

### 1.3 Formal Definition of **Problem Space**

A **Problem Space** (sometimes called the *search space* or *task environment*) is a richer mathematical structure that augments the state space with the following four essential components (a 4-tuple):

$$
\mathcal{P} = \langle S, s_0, O, G \rangle
$$

| Component | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| State Set | $S$ | All legal configurations |
| Initial State | $s_0$ | Where the problem begins |
| Operators | $O$ | Set of legal actions $o: S \rightarrow S$ |
| Goal Test | $G: S \rightarrow \{\text{True}, \text{False}\}$ | A predicate identifying goal states |

A **solution** is then a finite sequence of operators $\langle o_1, o_2, \dots, o_k \rangle$ that transforms $s_0$ into some $s_g \in S$ such that $G(s_g) = \text{True}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Ready Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU):**  
> *State Space* is just the set of states. *Problem Space* adds operators, initial state, and goal test. Many students lose marks by treating them as synonyms.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — A Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: Navigating a City in a Cab** 🏙️  
> - The **State** is your exact location at a given moment (e.g., *Main Avenue, near Clock Tower*).  
> - The **State Space** is the full road map of the city — every legal intersection and lane configuration.  
> - The **Initial State** is your current parking spot at home.  
> - The **Operators** are the legal driving moves (turn left, go straight 200 m, take the U-turn).  
> - The **Goal Test** is the question *"Am I parked at the airport?"*  
> - The **Solution** is the turn-by-turn route the cab driver will follow.

Just as a GPS software needs the map (state space), the rules of the road (operators), and a destination (goal) to compute a route, an expert system or AI agent needs the same four components to solve any problem.

### 1.5 Common Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Search Constants Used in State-Space Theory:**
> - **Branching Factor ($b$):** average number of successors from a state. Typical small puzzles: $b = 2$ to $b = 16$. Eight-puzzle: $b \approx 2.13$. Rubik's Cube: $b \approx 13.35$.  
> - **Depth of Solution ($d$):** the number of operators along the optimal path.  
> - **Maximum Path Length ($m$):** the deepest any path may extend.  
> - **State-Space Cardinality ($\vert S \vert$):** the total number of states, often astronomical. For example, the game of *Chess* has an estimated $\vert S \vert \approx 10^{47}$ legal positions; *Go* has $\vert S \vert \approx 10^{170}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of a Well-Formed Problem

A problem is *well-formed* only when each of the following four is unambiguously specified. Most expert-system failures occur because one of them is left implicit.

1. **Initial State** — The exact starting configuration $s_0$. The agent must know precisely where it begins. Example: *the water jugs in the classic Water-Jug problem are 4 L (empty) and 3 L (empty).*
2. **Successor Function** — A deterministic or stochastic map $O(s)$ returning a set of states reachable from $s$ via a single operator. The branching factor $b$ is the cardinality of the union of $O(s)$ across all $s$.
3. **Goal Test** — A predicate applied to a state to determine whether it satisfies the goal. Sometimes this is trivially a check (e.g., "are all 8 tiles in order?"); sometimes it requires expensive evaluation (e.g., *chess checkmate*).
4. **Path Cost Function** — A numerical $c(s, o, s')$ assigning a cost to each operator application. The total cost of a path is summed along the path:

$$
C(\text{path}) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c(s_{i-1}, o_i, s_i)
$$

## 2.2 State-Space Characterization

State spaces are classified along four orthogonal axes — these classifications are very common in KTU Part A questions.

| Property | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Observable vs. Partially Observable** | Sensors reveal full state | Hidden variables exist |
| **Deterministic vs. Stochastic** | Next state fixed by operator | Probabilistic transitions |
| **Discrete vs. Continuous** | Finite enumerable states | Real-valued variables (e.g., robotic arm angles) |
| **Static vs. Dynamic** | World unchanging during search | World evolves autonomously |

## 2.3 State-Space Graph vs. Search Tree

These two structures are mathematically different and KTU frequently asks for the distinction.

- **State-Space Graph (SSG):** A *graph* in which each state appears **only once**. Repeated states are detected and merged. Memory efficient.
- **Search Tree:** A *tree* rooted at $s_0$ in which every generated state is a fresh node, even if already visited. May contain exponentially many redundant nodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *path* in the state-space graph is the *same* as a *node-to-root traversal* in the search tree — but the graph stores it once, the tree may store it many times.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table is a **must-remember cheat sheet** for Part A and Part B derivations.

| # | Concept | Equation / Definition | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State-space size (discrete) | $\vert S \vert = k^n$ | Upper-bound estimate |
| 2 | Branching factor | $b = \dfrac{1}{\vert V \vert} \sum_{v \in V} \vert O(v) \vert$ | Empirical average |
| 3 | Path cost | $C = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c(s_{i-1}, o_i, s_i)$ | Solution quality |
| 4 | Solution depth | $d$ | Search depth target |
| 5 | Tree size (uniform $b$) | $1 + b + b^2 + \dots + b^d = \dfrac{b^{d+1}-1}{b-1}$ | Time complexity |
| 6 | Heuristic admissibility | $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ | A$^*$-style optimality |
| 7 | Effective branching factor | $b^* = (1 + b + b^2 + \dots + b^d)^{1/d}$ | Search efficiency metric |
| 8 | Goal predicate | $G(s) \rightarrow \{T, F\}$ | Goal test |
| 9 | Solution path | $\pi = \langle s_0, o_1, s_1, o_2, \dots, s_k \rangle$ | Final answer |

> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - $b = 1$ → only one path; the tree is a single chain.
> - $b = 0$ → no operators; trivial / no solution.
> - $h(n) = 0$ → uninformed search; $h(n) = h^*(n)$ → perfect heuristic (oracle).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

State and problem spaces are *the* fundamental abstraction of almost every engineering AI deployment:

- **Medical Diagnosis Expert Systems (MYCIN, INTERNIST):** State = patient's current symptom profile. Operators = "rule firings" that test or treat. Goal = confirmed disease hypothesis.
- **Industrial Robotics:** State = joint-angle vector $(q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_6)$. Operators = joint-velocity commands. Goal = TCP at target pose.
- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** State = pose, velocity, lane. Operators = steering/braking. Goal = destination reached.
- **Network Routing:** State = routing table snapshot. Operators = update rules. Goal = minimum-cost path.
- **Logistics & Supply Chain (KTU-CSE emphasis):** State = truck-distribution map. Operators = delivery moves. Goal = all packages delivered within time windows.

> [!NOTE]
> The **abstraction power** of state spaces is that virtually any *decision problem* — be it a game, a diagnosis, or a logistics schedule — can be cast into the same 4-tuple $\langle S, s_0, O, G \rangle$. This is why the concept is foundational in KTU Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — The Water-Jug Problem

> **Problem Statement (KTU Favourite):**  
> You have two jugs, A (capacity **4 L**) and B (capacity **3 L**). Neither has measurement marks. You can fill, empty, or pour from one jug to the other. Find a sequence of operations to obtain exactly **2 L** in jug A.

### Step 1: Define the State Representation

A state is the ordered pair $(a, b)$ where $a$ is the litres in A and $b$ is the litres in B, subject to the constraints:

$$
0 \le a \le 4, \quad 0 \le b \le 3
$$

So the **state-space cardinality** is:

$$
\vert S \vert = 5 \times 4 = 20
$$

The **initial state** is $s_0 = (0, 0)$. The **goal predicate** is:

$$
G((a, b)) = \text{True} \iff a = 2
$$

### Step 2: Enumerate the Operators

The six legal operators are:

| ID | Operator | Description | Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| $o_1$ | Fill A | $a \rightarrow 4$ | A not full |
| $o_2$ | Fill B | $b \rightarrow 3$ | B not full |
| $o_3$ | Empty A | $a \rightarrow 0$ | A not empty |
| $o_4$ | Empty B | $b \rightarrow 0$ | B not empty |
| $o_5$ | Pour A$\rightarrow$B | $\min(a, 3-b)$ transferred | A and B not empty |
| $o_6$ | Pour B$\rightarrow$A | $\min(b, 4-a)$ transferred | A and B not empty |

### Step 3: Build the State-Space Graph (Manually Traced)

Starting from $s_0 = (0, 0)$, applying the operators gives the reachable states. Below is a **complete trace** to the goal.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0,0) &\xrightarrow{o_1} (4,0) \xrightarrow{o_6} (1,3) \xrightarrow{o_4} (1,0) \\
&\xrightarrow{o_6} (1,0)\ \text{(no change)} \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

A cleaner alternative solution path:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_0 &= (0,0) \\
\xrightarrow{o_3 \text{ (no-op)}} & \text{(skipped)} \\
\xrightarrow{o_1} & (4,0) \\
\xrightarrow{o_5} & (1,3) \quad \text{— pour A→B, transferring } \min(4, 3-0) = 3 \\
\xrightarrow{o_4} & (1,0) \quad \text{— empty B} \\
\xrightarrow{o_5} & (0,1) \quad \text{— pour A→B, transferring } \min(1, 3-0) = 1 \\
\xrightarrow{o_1} & (4,1) \quad \text{— fill A} \\
\xrightarrow{o_5} & (2,3) \quad \text{— pour A→B, transferring } \min(4, 3-1) = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Goal reached:** $G((2,3)) = \text{True}$ because $a = 2$. The solution is a sequence of 6 operators. The **path cost** (assuming unit cost per operator) is:

$$
C = \sum_{i=1}^{6} 1 = 6
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — The 8-Puzzle (Sliding-Tile Game)

### Step 1: Encode the State

A state is a permutation of $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$ in a $3 \times 3$ grid, with $0$ denoting the blank tile.

$$
S = \{ \sigma \in \text{permutations of 9 elements} \}
$$

The **state-space cardinality** is exactly:

$$
\vert S \vert = 9! = 362880
$$

However, only **half** of these are reachable from any given state (a parity constraint), so the *effective* size is $9!/2 = 181440$.

### Step 2: Branching Factor

From any non-corner state, the blank can slide in 4 directions → 4 successors. From a corner, only 2 successors. The **average branching factor** is empirically:

$$
b = 2.13
$$

### Step 3: Operator Definitions

The two primitive operators (often encoded in code) are:
- **MOVE-BLANK-UP** — if blank not in top row.
- **MOVE-BLANK-DOWN** — if blank not in bottom row.
- **MOVE-BLANK-LEFT** — if blank not in leftmost column.
- **MOVE-BLANK-RIGHT** — if blank not in rightmost column.

Each operator swaps the blank with its neighbour.

### Step 4: Heuristic Estimation (for A$^*$-style search)

A commonly used heuristic is the **Manhattan Distance** — sum of the grid-axis distances of every tile from its goal position.

$$
h(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \vert x_i - x_i^* \vert + \vert y_i - y_i^* \vert
$$

The optimal-solution depth for the **standard 8-puzzle** is known to be 31. The Manhattan heuristic is *admissible* because for each tile, every move contributes at least 1 to Manhattan distance, and at most 1 to the actual path cost — thus $h(n) \le h^*(n)$.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Path-Cost Derivation for a Generic Search

Let the operator-cost function be:

$$
c(s, o, s') = \text{distance between the two grid cells in metres}
$$

A path traverses states $s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_3$. With per-step costs $c_1, c_2, c_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(\pi) &= c(s_0, o_1, s_1) + c(s_1, o_2, s_2) + c(s_2, o_3, s_3) \\
&= c_1 + c_2 + c_3
\end{aligned}
$$

If the goal is to *minimise* $C$, then any algorithm that always expands the lowest-$C$ partial path is optimal in uniform-cost environments (Dijkstra's algorithm).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — State-Space Cardinalities of Famous Problems

| Problem | $\vert S \vert$ (states) | $b$ | $d$ (typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8-Puzzle | $181440$ | $2.13$ | $22$ |
| 8-Queens | $8^8 = 16\,777\,216$ (config) / $4.0 \times 10^5$ (perms) | $8$ | $5$–$6$ |
| Tower of Hanoi (3 disks) | $3^3 = 27$ | $3$ | $7$ |
| Rubik's Cube | $4.3 \times 10^{19}$ | $13.35$ | $20$ (God's number) |
| 15-Puzzle | $10^{13}$ | $2.13$ | $80$ |
| Chess | $10^{47}$ | $35$ | $\sim 80$ (full game) |
| Go (19×19) | $10^{170}$ | $250$ | $150$ (avg game) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The branching factor and depth together determine **time complexity** $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$. For a modest $b = 10$ and $d = 12$, we get $10^{12}$ node expansions — illustrating why heuristic guidance is essential.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Problem-Solving Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts how an expert system / AI agent transforms a *problem* into a *solution* through the state-and-problem-space pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real World Problem] --> B[Problem Acquisition]
    B --> C[Define State Representation]
    C --> D[Enumerate Operators]
    D --> E[Build State Space]
    E --> F[Select Initial State s0]
    F --> G[Define Goal Predicate G of s]
    G --> H[Run Search Algorithm]
    H --> I{Goal Reached?}
    I -- No --> J[Apply Operator to Expand Node]
    J --> H
    I -- Yes --> K[Reconstruct Solution Path]
    K --> L[Return Sequence of Operators]
    L --> M[Real World Solution Action]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The process starts by abstracting the real-world problem into a state representation; the resulting problem space is searched by a control algorithm until a goal state is found. The successful path of operators is then mapped back to actions in the real world.

## 4.2 State-Space Graph of a Mini Water-Jug Sub-problem

A compact illustration of how states and operators are connected in the Water-Jug scenario from §3.1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[0,0] -- fill A --> S1[4,0]
    S1 -- pour A to B --> S2[1,3]
    S2 -- empty B --> S3[1,0]
    S3 -- pour A to B --> S4[0,1]
    S4 -- fill A --> S5[4,1]
    S5 -- pour A to B --> S6[2,3]
    S6 -- goal reached --> S7[GOAL a equals 2]
```

## 4.3 State-Space Graph vs. Search Tree (Conceptual Comparison)

The same logical states can be drawn as a compact graph or an exploded tree — both are valid representations but with different memory and time trade-offs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root s0] --> B[Successor 1]
    A --> C[Successor 2]
    A --> D[Successor 3]
    B --> E[Depth 2 Node A1]
    B --> F[Depth 2 Node A2]
    C --> G[Depth 2 Node B1]
    C --> H[Depth 2 Node B2]
    D --> I[Depth 2 Node C1]
    D --> J[Depth 2 Node C2]
    E --> K[Depth 3 Loopback to B - duplicate state detected in graph]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** In the search tree, every generated child is a fresh node. In the corresponding state-space graph, the state reached at depth 3 is the *same* as the state at depth 1, so it is *merged* — this is the memory benefit of graph search over tree search.

## 4.4 Operator-Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OP[Operators O] --> F[Fill Operators]
    OP --> E[Empty Operators]
    OP --> P[Pour Operators]
    F --> F1[Fill A]
    F --> F2[Fill B]
    E --> E1[Empty A]
    E --> E2[Empty B]
    P --> P1[Pour A to B]
    P --> P2[Pour B to A]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Instructions for KTU 2024:** Answer in **two or three crisp paragraphs** and a small diagram. Approximately **80–100 words** is the sweet spot.

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> *With a suitable example, explain the concept of a **state space**. Differentiate it from a **problem space**.*

**Model Answer (3-mark standard):**

A *state* is a complete description of the configuration of the world at one moment. The **state space** is the set of all such configurations that are reachable in a problem. For example, in the 8-puzzle, the state space consists of all 181 440 reachable permutations of the 9 tiles.

A **problem space** is a richer 4-tuple $\langle S, s_0, O, G \rangle$ that augments the state space with (i) an initial state $s_0$, (ii) a set of operators $O$, and (iii) a goal predicate $G$. Thus, the state space defines *where we can be*, while the problem space additionally defines *where we start*, *how we may move*, and *what we are trying to achieve*. In an exam, always state the 4-tuple explicitly to gain full credit.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> *Define the term **operator** in a state-space formulation. List the operators for the **4 L / 3 L water-jug problem**.*

**Model Answer (3-mark standard):**

An *operator* is a function $o: S \rightarrow S$ that maps a state to a successor state, modelling one legal action of the problem-solver. Operators are what make state spaces *traversable*.

For the 4 L / 3 L water-jug problem, the six legal operators are:  
(1) *Fill A* — set $a = 4$  
(2) *Fill B* — set $b = 3$  
(3) *Empty A* — set $a = 0$  
(4) *Empty B* — set $b = 0$  
(5) *Pour A→B* — transfer $\min(a, 3-b)$ litres from A to B  
(6) *Pour B→A* — transfer $\min(b, 4-a)$ litres from B to A  

A state $s = (a, b)$ is *valid* if $0 \le a \le 4$ and $0 \le b \le 3$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Sub-part (a) is usually a *definition/derivation* type; sub-part (b) is usually an *application/comparison* type.

---

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Define the 4-tuple $\langle S, s_0, O, G \rangle$ of a problem space. With a clear diagram, show the state-space graph of the water-jug problem (4 L and 3 L jugs, goal = 2 L in A). **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Solve the missionaries-and-cannibals problem (3 missionaries, 3 cannibals, 2-seat boat) by drawing its state-space graph up to the goal state. State the path cost. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Step 1 — Define the 4-tuple explicitly.** A problem space is a mathematical structure

$$
\mathcal{P} = \langle S, s_0, O, G \rangle
$$

- $S = \{(a, b) : 0 \le a \le 4, 0 \le b \le 3\}$ → 20 possible states.  
- $s_0 = (0, 0)$ → both jugs start empty.  
- $O = \{o_1, o_2, o_3, o_4, o_5, o_6\}$ as enumerated in §3.1.  
- $G((a, b)) = \text{True} \iff a = 2$.

**Step 2 — Trace the goal path.** The 6-step solution is:

$$
(0,0) \xrightarrow{\text{Fill A}} (4,0) \xrightarrow{\text{Pour A to B}} (1,3) \xrightarrow{\text{Empty B}} (1,0) \xrightarrow{\text{Pour A to B}} (0,1) \xrightarrow{\text{Fill A}} (4,1) \xrightarrow{\text{Pour A to B}} (2,3)
$$

**Step 3 — State-space graph (textual representation).**

```
(0,0) --Fill A--> (4,0) --Pour A→B--> (1,3) --Empty B--> (1,0)
                                                     |
                                                  Pour A→B
                                                     v
                                                  (0,1) --Fill A--> (4,1) --Pour A→B--> (2,3)  [GOAL]
```

**Step 4 — Path cost.** $C = 6$ (six unit-cost operator applications).

**Examiner's Tip:** Drawing the graph fully (not just listing operators) earns you the last 2 marks. Always mark the goal with a double-circle.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Represent the state.** A state is $(M, C, B)$ where $M$ = missionaries on the *original bank*, $C$ = cannibals on the *original bank*, $B \in \{L, R\}$ = boat location (L = left/original bank, R = right/opposite bank). Valid states satisfy $0 \le M, C \le 3$ and either $M \ge C$ or $M = 0$ on **both** banks.

**Step 2 — Operators.** The boat holds up to 2 people. Possible operator moves are: 1 missionary, 2 missionaries, 1 cannibal, 2 cannibals, or 1 missionary + 1 cannibal. So $\vert O \vert = 5$ legal trip types.

**Step 3 — Build the state-space graph (truncated to the goal).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_0 &= (3, 3, L) \\
\xrightarrow{1M, 1C \to R} & (2, 2, R) \\
\xrightarrow{1M \to L} & (3, 2, L) \\
\xrightarrow{2C \to R} & (3, 0, R) \\
\xrightarrow{1C \to L} & (3, 1, L) \\
\xrightarrow{2M \to R} & (1, 1, R) \\
\xrightarrow{1M, 1C \to L} & (2, 2, L) \\
\xrightarrow{2M \to R} & (0, 2, R) \\
\xrightarrow{1C \to L} & (0, 3, L) \\
\xrightarrow{2C \to R} & (0, 1, R) \\
\xrightarrow{1C \to L} & (0, 2, L) \\
\xrightarrow{2C \to R} & (0, 0, R) = s_g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Path cost.** $C = 11$ (eleven boat trips). This is the **optimal** solution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to *check the safety predicate* (missionaries must not be outnumbered on either bank) when enumerating states. The state $(1, 3, R)$ is **invalid** because 1 missionary is outnumbered by 3 cannibals on the right bank. Always include a *safety check* line in your derivation to gain full marks.

---

### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *(Alternative to Question A)*

**(a)** Compare and contrast **state-space graph** and **search tree** representations of a problem. Illustrate with a small example (3 coins problem: turn all three coins from HHH to TTT). **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Discuss any **four properties** used to classify problem spaces. Give one real-world example for each property pair. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a)

**Step 1 — Definitions.** A **state-space graph** stores each state exactly once. A **search tree** stores each *generated occurrence* of a state, including duplicates. Both are rooted at $s_0$, but the tree may contain many repeated nodes.

**Step 2 — The 3-Coin Problem.**

- States: $S = \{HHH, HHT, HTH, HTT, THH, THT, TTH, TTT\}$. So $\vert S \vert = 8$.
- Operators: *flip any single coin* → $b = 3$ from every state.
- Initial state: $s_0 = HHH$. Goal state: $s_g = TTT$.

**Step 3 — State-Space Graph (compact).**

```
              HHH
             /   \
           HHT   THH
          /   \ /   \
        HTT  HTH  TTH
          \   |   /
            TTT (goal)
```

Notice that *HTH* can be reached from both *HHT* and *THH*. In the **graph**, the *HTH* node is a single vertex with two incoming edges. In the **tree**, *HTH* appears **twice** — once as a child of *HHT* and once as a child of *THH*.

**Step 4 — Search Tree (excerpt).**

```
s0 = HHH
├── flip coin 1 → HTH
│   ├── flip coin 2 → HTT
│   │   └── flip coin 3 → TTT  [GOAL]
│   └── flip coin 3 → HTT  (duplicate)
└── flip coin 2 → HTH  (duplicate)
    └── ...
```

**Step 5 — Comparison Table.**

| Feature | State-Space Graph | Search Tree |
|---|---|---|
| Duplicates | None | Many |
| Memory | Lower | Higher |
| Cycle detection | Required | Implicit (depth limit) |
| Implementation | Hash set / closed list | Stack / queue |
| Time to construct | Higher (merging) | Lower |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Don't confuse the *tree* generated during search with the *state-space graph* of the problem. The state-space graph is a *property of the problem*; the search tree is a *property of the algorithm*.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**Four orthogonal classifications of problem spaces:**

1. **Observable vs. Partially Observable**  
   - Observable: Fully observable sensors. Example: *Chess* — both players see the entire board.  
   - Partially Observable: Sensor data is incomplete. Example: *Medical diagnosis from partial symptom reports.*

2. **Deterministic vs. Stochastic**  
   - Deterministic: Operator fully determines next state. Example: *8-puzzle* — sliding a tile yields a unique successor.  
   - Stochastic: Outcome has probabilities. Example: *Robot navigation on a slippery floor* — a "move forward" operator may or may not succeed.

3. **Discrete vs. Continuous**  
   - Discrete: Finite enumerable states. Example: *Tower of Hanoi with 3 disks* ($3^3 = 27$ states).  
   - Continuous: Real-valued state variables. Example: *Industrial robotic arm with joint angles in $[0, 2\pi]$* — uncountably infinite state space.

4. **Static vs. Dynamic**  
   - Static: The world does not change while the agent deliberates. Example: *Sliding-tile puzzle* — only the agent moves tiles.  
   - Dynamic: The world evolves autonomously. Example: *Taxi driving in traffic* — other cars move independently.

> A fifth property that often appears in the KTU syllabus: **Known vs. Unknown** — the agent may or may not know its own transition model.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common student error is treating these classifications as mutually exclusive. They are *independent* — for example, *Backgammon* is *discrete*, *stochastic*, *partially observable*, and *dynamic* — all four at once.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This Before the Exam)**

- **State** = a single configuration $s \in S$.  
- **State Space** = the set of all legal configurations $S$ (often huge, sometimes $10^{170}$).  
- **Problem Space** = $\langle S, s_0, O, G \rangle$ — the 4-tuple you *must* write down.  
- **Operator** = a function $o: S \rightarrow S$ that defines legal actions.  
- **Initial State** $s_0$ = where the problem begins.  
- **Goal Test** $G(s) \in \{T, F\}$ = a predicate identifying success states.  
- **Solution** = a sequence of operators $\langle o_1, \dots, o_k \rangle$ mapping $s_0$ to some $s_g$ with $G(s_g) = T$.  
- **Path Cost** $C = \sum c(s_{i-1}, o_i, s_i)$ — measure of solution quality.  
- **Branching Factor** $b$ = average number of successors from a node.  
- **Time complexity** of blind search $\approx \mathcal{O}(b^d)$.  
- **State-Space Graph** = one node per state (no duplicates).  
- **Search Tree** = one node per generated occurrence (with duplicates).  
- **Properties** = observable/deterministic/discrete/static (and their negations) — these are *independent*, not mutually exclusive.  
- **Heuristic $h(n) \le h^*(n)$** = admissibility condition for optimal search.  
- **Famous $\vert S \vert$ numbers** to remember: $9! = 362880$ (8-puzzle), $4.3 \times 10^{19}$ (Rubik's Cube), $10^{47}$ (Chess), $10^{170}$ (Go).  
- **Always draw the state-space graph** when the question asks for one — bullet-point lists without a graph lose 2–3 marks.  
- **Always state the goal predicate explicitly** in the 4-tuple.  
- **Always check safety / invariant constraints** (parity, balance, dominance) when generating states.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AND-OR Trees and Goals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT522 - EXPERT SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** AND-OR Trees and Goals ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **AND-OR Tree** is a specialized graph-based knowledge representation structure used in Artificial Intelligence and Expert Systems to model the **decomposition of a complex problem (goal) into sub-problems**, where some sub-problems must all be solved conjunctively (AND) and others offer alternative solution paths (OR). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):** > An AND-OR tree is a directed acyclic graph in which the root represents the *initial goal*, internal nodes represent *sub-goals*, and the structure of the tree captures the *logical dependencies* between parent goals and their constituent sub-goals. > - **OR Node** → A goal that can be satisfied by solving **any one** of its successor sub-goals. > - **AND Node** → A goal that can be satisfied **only if all** of its successor sub-goals are solved. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "Planning a Wedding"** 🏛️ > Imagine you are planning a wedding. The **top-level goal** is *"Have a successful wedding."* > - This is an **OR node** because you could either: *have a traditional Kerala-style wedding* **OR** *have a destination wedding*. > - But once you choose "traditional Kerala wedding," the sub-goals (book venue, hire caterer, buy jewelry, arrange music) must **all** be completed — that is an **AND node**. > - Each sub-goal might again be a choice (OR) or a mandatory set (AND), forming a hierarchical tree. > - A **solution tree** is a subtree that picks one valid path through OR-nodes and *all* branches at AND-nodes, reaching tasks you can actually perform (leaves). ### 1.3 Goals in Expert Systems > [!IMPORTANT] > **Goal (KTU Definition):** A *goal* is a desirable system state or a proposition that the inference engine attempts to prove or achieve. In goal-driven (backward-chaining) reasoning, the system starts from the goal and recursively decomposes it into *sub-goals* until primitive facts in the knowledge base are reached. | **Goal Type** | **Description** | **Example** | |---|---|---| | **Top-Level Goal** | The primary objective of the consultation | *"Diagnose engine fault"* | | **Sub-Goal** | An intermediate objective derived from a parent goal | *"Check fuel system"* | | **Leaf Goal** | A primitive goal that matches a fact or rule consequent | *"Battery voltage > 12V"* | ### 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > - **Cost of a solution graph** is measured in arbitrary **cost units (c.u.)** — usually edge traversal cost + heuristic estimate. > - **Heuristic admissibility constraint:** $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ where $h^{*}(n)$ is the true optimal cost. > - **Solution graph** is the minimal-cost subgraph that proves the root goal is *solvable*. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structural Components of an AND-OR Tree A complete AND-OR tree consists of the following logical entities: - **Root Node (Initial Goal):** The starting problem, denoted $G_{0}$. - **OR Node (Disjunctive Decomposition):** Goal is solved if *at least one* of its immediate successors is solved. $$G_{OR} = G_{1} \lor G_{2} \lor \ldots \lor G_{k}$$ - **AND Node (Conjunctive Decomposition):** Goal is solved if *all* of its immediate successors are solved. $$G_{AND} = G_{1} \land G_{2} \land \ldots \land G_{k}$$ - **Tip Nodes (Frontier):** Leaves of the partially expanded tree whose costs are estimated using heuristics. - **Solved Node:** A node whose corresponding goal has been proven achievable. - **Unsolved Node:** A node for which no successful sub-solution has been discovered yet. ### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind AND-OR Trees - **Why AND-OR trees?** Real-world expert reasoning rarely follows a single linear path. Medical diagnosis, fault detection, and planning all involve *combinations of evidence* (AND) and *alternative hypotheses* (OR). Pure OR-trees (used in state-space search) cannot model conjunctive dependencies. - **How are they built?** Knowledge engineers encode production rules of the form: $$IF \ (sub-goal_{1} \ AND \ sub-goal_{2} \ ... \ AND \ sub-goal_{n}) \ THEN \ (parent-goal)$$ $$IF \ (alternative_{1}) \ OR \ (alternative_{2}) \ THEN \ (parent-goal)$$ ### 2.3 AO* Search — The Core Algorithm The **AO\*** algorithm (Modified from Hart, Nilsson, and Raphael — *1971*) is the standard heuristic search procedure for AND-OR graphs. It performs a **best-first search**, expanding the most promising tip node and propagating revised cost estimates upward. #### Operational Steps of AO* (Conceptual Walkthrough) 1. Initialize the search tree $T$ with the root node $n_{0}$. Set $h(n_{0})$ = heuristic estimate. 2. **Repeat** until root is marked *SOLVED* or no successors remain: a. Traverse $T$ from root using a *marked-OR / unmarked-AND* path selection to find a **tip node** $t$ in the current frontier. b. **Expand** $t$ by generating all its successors. c. For each successor $s$ not yet in $T$, assign $h(s) =$ estimated cost. d. Create a *dummy* SOLVED node for any $s$ whose $h = 0$. e. **Propagate** new cost estimates upward: - If $n$ is an **OR node**: $f(n) = \min_{s \in \text{succ}(n)} \left[ c(n, s) + f(s) \right]$ - If $n$ is an **AND node**: $f(n) = \sum_{s \in \text{succ}(n)} \left[ c(n, s) + f(s) \right]$ - Mark the *best* successor (for OR) or *all* successors (for AND) as part of the partial solution graph. 3. **Termination:** Root is labeled SOLVED when a solution graph is found. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE FOR KTU ESE** | **Formula / Notation** | **Meaning** | **Used At** | |---|---|---| | $f(n) = \min_{s} \left[ c(n, s) + f(s) \right]$ | Cost of an OR node | OR-Node Cost Update | | $f(n) = \sum_{s} \left[ c(n, s) + f(s) \right]$ | Cost of an AND node | AND-Node Cost Update | | $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ | Admissibility condition | Heuristic Validation | | $f(n_{0}) = \text{cost of optimal solution graph}$ | Root cost = solution cost | Final Answer | | $f_{\text{AND}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} f(s_{i}) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} c(n, s_{i})$ | Aggregated AND cost | Multi-branch AND node | | $f_{\text{OR}} = \min_{i=1}^{k} \left\{ f(s_{i}) + c(n, s_{i}) \right\}$ | Best OR cost | Multi-branch OR node | | $\text{SOLVED}(n) = \text{True}$ | Node proven achievable | Termination check | > **Note on notation:** Vertical bars in tables are replaced with $\vert$ to avoid markdown parsing conflicts. Use $c(n, s)$ for *edge cost between nodes $n$ and $s$*. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Medical Expert Systems (MYCIN, CADUCEUS):** Differential diagnosis requires AND-OR reasoning — "If fever AND cough AND positive X-ray THEN pneumonia (OR viral, OR bacterial)." - **Industrial Fault Diagnosis:** Siemens and Honeywell use AND-OR trees in turbine/jet-engine fault isolation. - **Automated Planning (STRIPS, NOAH):** Hierarchical task networks are essentially AND-OR trees. - **VLSI Design Rule Checking:** Conjunctive and disjunctive conditions for layout validation. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example: Cost Propagation in an AND-OR Graph > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** > Consider an AND-OR graph where the root node **A** is an **OR node** with two successors **B** and **C** (edge costs $c(A,B) = 2$, $c(A,C) = 4$). > - Node **B** is an **AND node** with two successors **D** and **E** (edge costs $c(B,D) = 3$, $c(B,E) = 5$). > - Node **C** is an **OR node** with successors **F** and **G** (edge costs $c(C,F) = 1$, $c(C,G) = 2$). > - Heuristic estimates for tip nodes: $h(D) = 4$, $h(E) = 6$, $h(F) = 5$, $h(G) = 3$. > - **Find the optimal solution cost and the solution graph.** --- #### Step 1 — Initialize Tip Node Costs Tip nodes are $D, E, F, G$. Their $f$ values are simply their heuristic estimates since they have no successors. $$f(D) = h(D) = 4$$ $$f(E) = h(E) = 6$$ $$f(F) = h(F) = 5$$ $$f(G) = h(G) = 3$$ > **Reasoning:** A tip node's cost is the estimated cost to reach a goal from it. No further expansion means $f = h$. --- #### Step 2 — Compute Cost of AND Node B Node **B** is an **AND node**, so we **sum** the costs of all its successors plus edge costs. $$f(B) = \sum_{s \in \{D, E\}} \left[ c(B, s) + f(s) \right]$$ $$f(B) = \left[ c(B, D) + f(D) \right] + \left[ c(B, E) + f(E) \right]$$ $$f(B) = (3 + 4) + (5 + 6)$$ $$f(B) = 7 + 11 = 18$$ > **Reasoning:** Both D and E must be solved for B to be solved, so costs add. --- #### Step 3 — Compute Cost of OR Node C Node **C** is an **OR node**, so we take the **minimum** over its successors. $$f(C) = \min_{s \in \{F, G\}} \left[ c(C, s) + f(s) \right]$$ $$f(C) = \min \left\{ (1 + 5), \ (2 + 3) \right\}$$ $$f(C) = \min \left\{ 6, \ 5 \right\} = 5$$ > **Reasoning:** Choosing path A → C → G gives cost 5, which is cheaper than A → C → F (cost 6). So G is the marked successor of C. --- #### Step 4 — Compute Cost of OR Node A (Root) Node **A** is an **OR node** with successors B and C. $$f(A) = \min_{s \in \{B, C\}} \left[ c(A, s) + f(s) \right]$$ $$f(A) = \min \left\{ (2 + 18), \ (4 + 5) \right\}$$ $$f(A) = \min \left\{ 20, \ 9 \right\} = 9$$ > **Reasoning:** Going through C (cost 9) is cheaper than going through B (cost 20). The marked best successor is **C**. --- #### Step 5 — Identify the Solution Graph The **solution graph** consists of the path that achieved the minimum at the root and all marked successors recursively. $$\text{Solution Path} = A \rightarrow C \rightarrow G$$ $$\text{Optimal Solution Cost} = f(A) = 9$$ --- #### Final Answer Summary | **Node** | **Type** | **Cost $f$** | **Marked Successor(s)** | |---|---|---|---| | D | Tip | 4 | — | | E | Tip | 6 | — | | F | Tip | 5 | — | | G | Tip | 3 | — | | B | AND | 18 | D, E (both) | | C | OR | 5 | G | | **A (Root)** | **OR** | **9** | **C** | --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — AND vs OR Cost Comparison | **Criterion** | **OR Node** | **AND Node** | |---|---|---| | **Logical Operator** | Disjunction ($\lor$) | Conjunction ($\land$) | | **Cost Formula** | $f(n) = \min \left[ c + f \right]$ | $f(n) = \sum \left[ c + f \right]$ | | **Solution Requirement** | At least one successor solved | All successors solved | | **Marking Strategy** | Mark single best successor | Mark all successors | | **Failure Implication** | Failure of one branch is OK | Failure of any branch fails parent | | **Real-World Example** | "Try medicine A **or** medicine B" | "Need X-ray **and** blood test **and** ECG" | --- ### 3.3 Solvability Rules (KTU Hot Topic) > [!NOTE] > A node is **SOLVED** if: > - It is a **terminal node** that matches a known fact, **OR** > - It is an **OR node** and *at least one* of its successors is SOLVED, **OR** > - It is an **AND node** and *all* of its successors are SOLVED. > > A node is **UNSOLVABLE** if: > - It is a **terminal node** that does **not** match any known fact (and has no rules), **OR** > - It is an **OR node** and *all* successors are UNSOLVABLE, **OR** > - It is an **AND node** and *at least one* successor is UNSOLVABLE. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 AND-OR Tree Architecture (Mermaid Diagram) > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **AND-OR tree structure** for the worked example above. Node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard. ```mermaid graph TD A[Node A - OR Root Goal] B[Node B - AND Subgoal] C[Node C - OR Subgoal] D[Node D - Tip h=4] E[Node E - Tip h=6] F[Node F - Tip h=5] G[Node G - Tip h=3 SOLVED] A ---|"c=2"| B A ---|"c=4 BEST PATH"| C B ---|"c=3"| D B ---|"c=5"| E C ---|"c=1"| F C ---|"c=2 BEST"| G style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style E fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` > **Legend (Color Code):** > - 🟡 **Gold:** Root goal node > - 🟢 **Green:** Nodes on the optimal solution graph > - 🔴 **Pink:** AND node (not in solution) > - ⚪ **Grey:** Tip nodes (not expanded) --- ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (AO* Search Engine) > [!NOTE] > The following block diagram represents the **functional architecture of the AO\* search algorithm** as used in an Expert System's inference engine. ```mermaid graph LR KM[Knowledge Base
Rules and Facts] UI[User Interface
Query or Goal Input] INF[Inference Engine] AOSTAR[AO Search Core] EXP[Node Expansion Module] HEST[Heuristic Estimator h n] PROP[Cost Propagation Module] SG[Solution Graph Builder] EX[Explanation Module] UI --> INF KM --> INF INF --> AOSTAR AOSTAR --> EXP EXP --> HEST HEST --> PROP PROP --> SG SG --> EX EX --> UI style INF fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style AOSTAR fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style SG fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Architecture Flow:** > 1. User submits a goal via the **UI** > 2. The **Inference Engine** loads rules from the **Knowledge Base** > 3. The **AO\* Search Core** iteratively expands tip nodes > 4. The **Heuristic Estimator** assigns $h(n)$ values > 5. The **Cost Propagation Module** updates $f(n)$ upward (sum for AND, min for OR) > 6. The **Solution Graph Builder** constructs the optimal proof tree > 7. The **Explanation Module** returns a human-readable trace --- ### 4.3 Goal Decomposition Hierarchy (Backward Chaining) ```mermaid graph TD ROOT[Top Level Goal: Diagnose Disease] OR1[OR: Hypothesis A] OR2[OR: Hypothesis B] AND1[AND: Evidence Set 1] AND2[AND: Evidence Set 2] LEAF1[Leaf: Symptom X Present] LEAF2[Leaf: Lab Test Y Positive] LEAF3[Leaf: Symptom Z Present] LEAF4[Leaf: Scan Result W Normal] ROOT --> OR1 ROOT --> OR2 OR1 --> AND1 OR1 --> AND2 AND1 --> LEAF1 AND1 --> LEAF2 AND2 --> LEAF3 AND2 --> LEAF4 style ROOT fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style OR1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style OR2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style AND1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style AND2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style LEAF1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style LEAF2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style LEAF3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style LEAF4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** --- #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define an AND-OR tree. Differentiate between AND nodes and OR nodes with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An AND-OR tree is a graph structure used in expert systems to represent the decomposition of a problem into sub-problems, where the solution requires either choosing one alternative (OR) or satisfying multiple sub-goals simultaneously (AND). | **AND Node** | **OR Node** | |---|---| | Conjunctive — all successors must be solved | Disjunctive — any one successor may solve it | | Cost = Sum of successors | Cost = Min of successors | | Example: "Confirm dengue" needs fever AND low platelet AND positive NS1 | Example: "Confirm dengue" can be done via NS1 test OR ELISA test | *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for AND/OR distinction, 1 mark for example)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is a "goal" in an expert system? Explain goal-driven reasoning with a diagram.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **goal** is a desired state or proposition that the expert system attempts to prove. In **goal-driven (backward-chaining) reasoning**, the system starts with the goal and recursively decomposes it into sub-goals until it reaches facts in the knowledge base. - **Top-level goal** → broken into **sub-goals** → broken into **leaf goals** matching facts. - The chain of reasoning is recorded as a **proof tree**. ```mermaid graph TD G[GOAL: Car will not start] G1[SUB-GOAL 1: Check battery] G2[SUB-GOAL 2: Check fuel] F1[FACT: Battery voltage = 0V] F2[FACT: Fuel tank empty] G --> G1 G --> G2 G1 --> F1 G2 --> F2 ``` *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for reasoning steps, 1 mark for diagram/suitability)* --- ### **PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)** *(Module Internal Choice Pattern: Two alternatives — Answer ANY ONE)* --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024] **(a)** Explain the structure and components of an AND-OR tree used in expert systems. Describe the role of **AO\*** search in solving problems represented as AND-OR graphs. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Consider the following AND-OR graph: The root node **S** is an OR node with two successors **A** and **B** (edge costs $c(S,A) = 3$, $c(S,B) = 2$). - Node **A** is an AND node with successors **D** and **E** (costs $c(A,D) = 1$, $c(A,E) = 4$). - Node **B** is an OR node with successors **F** and **G** (costs $c(B,F) = 2$, $c(B,G) = 3$). - Heuristic estimates: $h(D) = 5$, $h(E) = 2$, $h(F) = 4$, $h(G) = 6$. Compute the cost of the optimal solution graph and identify all marked successors using the **AO\*** algorithm. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Answer for Q.A:** **(a) AND-OR Tree Structure & AO\* Search (7 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** An AND-OR tree is a graph where the root represents the problem, internal nodes represent sub-problems, and edges capture dependencies. The root is decomposed into sub-goals connected by AND or OR operators. - **Components (2 marks):** 1. *OR nodes* (disjunction) — choose one path 2. *AND nodes* (conjunction) — must satisfy all branches 3. *Tip nodes* — frontier, with heuristic estimates $h(n)$ 4. *Solution graph* — minimal subtree proving root goal is achievable 5. *Edge costs* $c(n, s)$ — cost of transitioning between nodes - **AO\* Algorithm (2 marks):** - AO\* is a best-first search for AND-OR graphs. - It repeatedly expands the most promising tip node. - Costs propagate upward: - OR: $f(n) = \min [c + f(s)]$ - AND: $f(n) = \sum [c + f(s)]$ - Marks successors that lie on the best partial solution. - Terminates when root is marked SOLVED. - **Solvability Rules (1 mark):** - An AND node is solved if **all** successors are solved. - An OR node is solved if **at least one** successor is solved. - **Significance (1 mark):** AO\* enables efficient heuristic search in domains like medical diagnosis, fault detection, and planning where conjunctive and disjunctive reasoning coexist. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Initialize tip nodes (1 mark):** $$f(D) = 5, \quad f(E) = 2, \quad f(F) = 4, \quad f(G) = 6$$ **Step 2 — Compute cost of AND node A (2 marks):** $$f(A) = \left[ c(A, D) + f(D) \right] + \left[ c(A, E) + f(E) \right]$$ $$f(A) = (1 + 5) + (4 + 2) = 6 + 6 = 12$$ **Step 3 — Compute cost of OR node B (1 mark):** $$f(B) = \min \left\{ (2 + 4), \ (3 + 6) \right\} = \min \{ 6, \ 9 \} = 6$$ Marked successor: **F** (cost 6). **Step 4 — Compute cost of root OR node S (1 mark):** $$f(S) = \min \left\{ (3 + 12), \ (2 + 6) \right\} = \min \{ 15, \ 8 \} = 8$$ Marked successor: **B** (cost 8). **Step 5 — Solution Graph (1 mark):** $$\text{Optimal Solution Path} = S \rightarrow B \rightarrow F$$ $$\text{Optimal Solution Cost} = f(S) = 8$$ **Step 6 — Diagram (1 mark):** ```mermaid graph TD S[S: OR Root f=8] A[A: AND f=12] B[B: OR f=6 BEST] D[D: Tip f=5] E[E: Tip f=2] F[F: Tip f=4 SOLVED] G[G: Tip f=6] S ---|"c=3"| A S ---|"c=2 BEST"| B A ---|"c=1"| D A ---|"c=4"| E B ---|"c=2 BEST"| F B ---|"c=3"| G style S fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 ``` --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Alternative] **(a)** Discuss the concept of **goal decomposition** in expert systems. How do AND-OR trees facilitate backward chaining? Include a block diagram. *(7 marks)* **(b)** An expert system must decide whether to grant a loan. The top-level goal **L** is an OR node with two alternatives: **A** (high-income applicant) and **R** (repayment capacity). - **A** is an AND node with sub-goals **S1** (salary > ₹1,00,000) and **S2** (employment tenure > 5 years). - **R** is an AND node with sub-goals **S3** (no existing loans) and **S4** (CIBIL score > 750). - Edge costs (effort/time): $c(L, A) = 5$, $c(L, R) = 3$, $c(A, S1) = 2$, $c(A, S2) = 4$, $c(R, S3) = 1$, $c(R, S4) = 3$. - Heuristic estimates: $h(S1) = 1$, $h(S2) = 2$, $h(S3) = 1$, $h(S4) = 4$. Compute the cost of the optimal solution graph using AO\* propagation. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Answer for Q.B:** **(a) Goal Decomposition & Backward Chaining (7 marks):** - **Goal Decomposition (2 marks):** A complex problem is recursively broken into simpler sub-goals. Each sub-goal is independently verified using rules in the knowledge base. - **Backward Chaining (2 marks):** Starts from the goal and works toward known facts. The inference engine searches for rules whose consequents match the goal (backward) and tries to prove their antecedents. - **Role of AND-OR Trees (2 marks):** - **AND arcs** represent *all* antecedents that must be proved. - **OR arcs** represent *alternative* rules that can prove a goal. - The **solution graph** is the proof tree constructed during backward chaining. - **Block Diagram (1 mark):** [As in Section 4.2] --- **(b) Loan Expert System (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Initialize (1 mark):** $$f(S1) = 1, \quad f(S2) = 2, \quad f(S3) = 1, \quad f(S4) = 4$$ **Step 2 — AND node A (2 marks):** $$f(A) = (c(A, S1) + f(S1)) + (c(A, S2) + f(S2))$$ $$f(A) = (2 + 1) + (4 + 2) = 3 + 6 = 9$$ **Step 3 — AND node R (1 mark):** $$f(R) = (c(R, S3) + f(S3)) + (c(R, S4) + f(S4))$$ $$f(R) = (1 + 1) + (3 + 4) = 2 + 7 = 9$$ **Step 4 — OR node L (root) (1 mark):** $$f(L) = \min \left\{ (5 + 9), \ (3 + 9) \right\} = \min \{ 14, \ 12 \} = 12$$ Marked successor: **R** (cost 12). **Step 5 — Solution Graph (1 mark):** $$\text{Optimal Path} = L \rightarrow R \rightarrow S3 \rightarrow S4$$ $$\text{Optimal Cost} = f(L) = 12$$ **Step 6 — Inference (1 mark):** The expert system should verify **repayment capacity** (R) as the cheaper path — checking CIBIL score and existing loans is faster (cost 12) than verifying both salary AND employment (cost 14). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls ⚠️** > > 1. **Confusing AND/OR cost formulas:** Students frequently sum costs at OR nodes or take the minimum at AND nodes. **This is the #1 mark-loss reason.** Memorize: *AND = sum, OR = min*. > > 2. **Forgetting to add edge costs:** $f(n) = c(n, s) + f(s)$ — the edge cost is **not optional**. > > 3. **Not marking successors correctly:** At an AND node, **ALL** successors are part of the solution; at an OR node, only the **single best** is marked. Losing this loses 1–2 marks. > > 4. **Skipping the admissibility check:** When asked to "verify" or "justify" the heuristic, students forget the condition $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$. > > 5. **Confusing tip-node $f$ with $h$:** Initially $f = h$, but after expansion $f$ becomes the propagated cost. > > 6. **Diagrams without labels or legend:** KTU examiners award 1 mark for a clean, labeled tree diagram. Always annotate edge costs and node types. --- ### 📋 **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: AND-OR Trees and Goals** - ✅ **AND-OR tree =** problem-decomposition structure with conjunctive (AND) and disjunctive (OR) dependencies. - ✅ **OR node cost** = **MIN** of $(c + f)$ over successors; mark **one** best successor. - ✅ **AND node cost** = **SUM** of $(c + f)$ over successors; mark **all** successors. - ✅ **AO\*** = best-first heuristic search for AND-OR graphs; uses $h(n)$ estimates. - ✅ **Admissibility:** $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ — heuristic must not overestimate. - ✅ **Solution graph** = minimal-cost subgraph proving the root goal is *achievable*. - ✅ **Solved node:** A terminal fact node OR an OR-node with one solved successor OR an AND-node with all solved successors. - ✅ **Backward chaining** = goal-driven reasoning; starts at root goal and decomposes into sub-goals. - ✅ **Tip nodes** = frontier of partially expanded tree; their costs are heuristic estimates. - ✅ **Tip-to-root propagation** is the heart of AO\* — always recompute from bottom-up after expansion. - ✅ **Goals** can be **top-level**, **sub-goals**, or **leaf goals** (matching facts in KB). - ✅ **Real-world applications:** MYCIN (medical), fault diagnosis, hierarchical planning, VLSI design rule checking. - ✅ **Edge costs** must always be added: $f(n) = \min / \sum \left[ c(n, s) + f(s) \right]$ — never forget the $c(n, s)$ term. ---

topics.title: Deductive Logic and Syllogisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT522 - EXPERT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Deductive Logic and Syllogisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Deductive Logic — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deductive Logic** is a branch of formal logic in which a conclusion is derived necessarily and with **absolute certainty** from a given set of premises, provided those premises are true. The argument moves from **general premises → specific (guaranteed) conclusion**.

In the context of **Expert Systems (PECMT522)**, deductive logic forms the *inference backbone* of rule-based and knowledge-based systems. The famous **MYCIN** medical expert system and **DENDRAL** (chemical analysis) both rely on deductive chains to reach conclusions that are *guaranteed* to follow from the rule base.

Mathematically, the canonical form of a deductive argument is:

$$\text{If } P_1, P_2, P_3, \ldots, P_n \text{ are all TRUE, then } C \text{ MUST be TRUE.}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllogism** is the *oldest and most classical form* of deductive logic, formalised by **Aristotle (384–322 BCE)** in his work *Organon*. A syllogism is a three-part deductive argument composed of:
> - **Major Premise** (a general statement)
> - **Minor Premise** (a specific statement)
> - **Conclusion** (logically follows from the two)

The universal notation is:
$$\text{Major Premise} \;\land\; \text{Minor Premise} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Conclusion}$$

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Detective in the Library"

> [!TIP]
> Think of deductive logic as a **detective who already has all the clues** and only has to *connect the dots*.
>
> - **Premise 1 (Major):** *All engineers are rational thinkers.* (General truth)
> - **Premise 2 (Minor):** *Alice is an engineer.* (Specific fact)
> - **Conclusion:** *Therefore, Alice is a rational thinker.* (Must follow)
>
> The conclusion is **not discovered** — it is **unavoidable**. This is the heart of deductive logic: certainty, not probability. Compare this to **inductive reasoning** (where we infer *likely* conclusions from patterns, like a detective with *partial* clues guessing the culprit).

### 1.3 Why Deductive Logic Matters in Expert Systems

| Component | Role in Expert System |
|---|---|
| **Knowledge Base** | Stores the *premises* (rules and facts) |
| **Inference Engine** | Performs the *deduction* (forward / backward chaining) |
| **Conclusion** | Output diagnosis, classification, or decision |

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity vs. Soundness** — Two crucial terms:
> - A deductive argument is **Valid** if the conclusion *must* follow from the premises (structure check).
> - A deductive argument is **Sound** if it is **Valid** *and* all its premises are **actually true** (truth check).
> - Expert systems aim for **sound** deductions to ensure trustworthy advice.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of a Categorical Proposition

A categorical proposition is a declarative sentence that relates two categories (terms). It has exactly four standard forms (the **A, E, I, O** forms):

| Form | Name | Structure | Example |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **A** | Universal Affirmative | All S are P | All doctors are graduates. |
| **E** | Universal Negative | No S are P | No reptiles are mammals. |
| **I** | Particular Affirmative | Some S are P | Some engineers are musicians. |
| **O** | Particular Negative | Some S are not P | Some students are not toppers. |

Where:
- **S** = Subject term (appears in conclusion as the subject)
- **P** = Predicate term (appears in conclusion as the predicate)
- **Middle Term (M)** = appears in *both* premises but **never** in the conclusion

### 2.2 The Four Figures of Syllogism

A syllogism's *figure* is determined by the position of the middle term (M) in the two premises:

| Figure | Major Premise | Minor Premise | Mood Example (Barbara) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Figure 1** | M — P | S — M | M→P, S→M, ∴ S→P |
| **Figure 2** | P — M | S — M | P→M, S→M, ∴ S→P |
| **Figure 3** | M — P | M — S | M→P, M→S, ∴ S→P |
| **Figure 4** | P — M | M — S | P→M, M→S, ∴ S→P |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each **Mood** is a triple of A, E, I, O letters (e.g., AAA, EIO). Only **19 valid moods** exist across the four figures — these are called the **Aristotelian Syllogisms**.

### 2.3 The Eight Classical Rules of Syllogism (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners love testing these **8 rules** — committing them to memory guarantees 7+ marks on a typical 14-mark question.

| # | Rule | Plain-English Meaning |
|:---:|---|---|
| **R1** | Must contain exactly **three terms** (S, P, M), each used at least twice. | The *Three-Term Rule* — prevents ambiguity. |
| **R2** | The **middle term** must be **distributed at least once**. | Otherwise, the link between S and P is broken. |
| **R3** | Any term **distributed in the conclusion** must be **distributed in the premise** where it appears. | Prevents *illicit major* or *illicit minor*. |
| **R4** | Must not contain **two negative premises**. | Two negatives cannot link S to P. |
| **R5** | A **negative premise** requires a **negative conclusion**, and vice-versa. | Maintains polarity of negation. |
| **R6** | Cannot have a **particular conclusion** from **two universal premises**. | The "AA→I forbidden" rule (no necessary existence). |
| **R7** | No conclusion can be drawn from **two particular premises**. | II, IO, OI, OO → no valid conclusion. |
| **R8** | The middle term must be **universal**, not particular, when premises are universal. | Strengthens the bridge. |

### 2.4 The Square of Opposition

The Square of Opposition maps the **logical relationships** between the four categorical forms (A, E, I, O):

| Relation | Between | Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Contradictories** | A ↔ O, E ↔ I | Cannot both be true; cannot both be false. |
| **Contraries** | A ↔ E | Both can be false; both cannot be true. |
| **Subcontraries** | I ↔ O | Both can be true; both cannot be false. |
| **Subalternation** | A → I, E → O | Truth of universal implies truth of particular. |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Used In |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| $P \rightarrow Q$ | Implication (If P then Q) | Conditional deductive proofs |
| $P \leftrightarrow Q$ | Biconditional (P iff Q) | Equivalence rules |
| $\lnot P$ | Negation of P | Contradiction, Modus Tollens |
| $\forall x$ | For all x | Universal premise |
| $\exists x$ | There exists x | Particular premise |
| $P \lor Q$ | P OR Q | Disjunction |
| $P \land Q$ | P AND Q | Conjunction in premises |
| $P \Rightarrow Q$ | P entails Q | Entailment symbol (KTU favourite) |
| $\models$ | Logically entails | Validity check |
| $\vdash$ | Syntactic derivation | Proof theory |

### 2.6 The Three Core Deductive Inference Rules (Propositional Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> Beyond syllogisms, deductive logic in expert systems relies on three **tautologically valid** inference rules:

**1. Modus Ponens (Affirming the Antecedent)**
$$P \rightarrow Q, \; P \; \vdash \; Q$$

**2. Modus Tollens (Denying the Consequent)**
$$P \rightarrow Q, \; \lnot Q \; \vdash \; \lnot P$$

**3. Hypothetical Syllogism (Chain Rule)**
$$P \rightarrow Q, \; Q \rightarrow R \; \vdash \; P \rightarrow R$$

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Deductive Logic |
|---|---|
| **Medical Diagnosis Systems** | MYCIN uses deductive rules: *IF fever AND cough THEN possible_flu*. |
| **Fault Diagnosis** | Power-grid expert systems deduce fault location from sensor premises. |
| **Legal AI** | Statutory rule engines deduce applicability of law. |
| **Automated Theorem Proving** | Coq, Isabelle — purely deductive systems. |
| **Configuration Systems** | SAP, airline reservation engines — if A and B, then offer C. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Classic Barbara Syllogism (Figure 1, Mood AAA)

**Given:**
- **Major Premise (P1):** All mammals are warm-blooded animals.
- **Minor Premise (P2):** All dogs are mammals.
- **Conclusion (C):** Therefore, all dogs are warm-blooded animals.

**Step-by-step logical derivation:**

**Step 1 — Identify the three terms:**
- Subject (S) = dogs
- Predicate (P) = warm-blooded animals
- Middle term (M) = mammals

**Step 2 — Translate into formal logical form:**

$$\text{P1: } \forall x\, (M(x) \rightarrow W(x))$$
$$\text{P2: } \forall x\, (D(x) \rightarrow M(x))$$

**Step 3 — Derive the conclusion using universal instantiation and transitivity:**

Let `a` be an arbitrary individual.

From P2: $D(a) \rightarrow M(a)$ (Universal Instantiation on P2)

From P1: $M(a) \rightarrow W(a)$ (Universal Instantiation on P1)

**Step 4 — Apply Hypothetical Syllogism (transitivity of implication):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(a) &\rightarrow M(a) \quad &\text{(from P2)}\\
M(a) &\rightarrow W(a) \quad &\text{(from P1)}\\
\hline
D(a) &\rightarrow W(a) \quad &\text{(Hypothetical Syllogism)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Generalise via Universal Generalisation:**

$$\forall x\, (D(x) \rightarrow W(x))$$

i.e., **All dogs are warm-blooded animals** — Q.E.D. ∎

**Validation against the 8 Rules:**

| Rule | Check | Status |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| R1: Three terms | dogs, mammals, warm-blooded | ✅ |
| R2: Middle term distributed | "All mammals" distributes M | ✅ |
| R3: Distributed terms consistent | D and W not over-distributed | ✅ |
| R4: No two negative premises | Both affirmative | ✅ |
| R5: Premise-conclusion polarity match | Both affirmative | ✅ |
| R6: No particular from universals | All A's, conclusion A | ✅ |
| R7: Not from two particulars | N/A | ✅ |
| R8: Middle term universal | "All mammals" | ✅ |

**Conclusion:** Valid & Sound argument.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Proving Validity via Truth Table (Modus Ponens)

**Argument form to prove:**
$$P \rightarrow Q, \; P \models Q$$

We construct the **truth table** with $2^2 = 4$ rows (since there are 2 atomic propositions):

| $P$ | $Q$ | $P \rightarrow Q$ | Premise 1 true? | Premise 2 ($P$) true? | Conclusion ($Q$) true? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| T | T | T | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| T | F | F | ❌ | ✅ | — |
| F | T | T | ✅ | ❌ | — |
| F | F | T | ✅ | ❌ | — |

**Interpretation:**
- We look only at rows where **both premises are TRUE** (rows 1, 3, 4).
- In **every** such row, the conclusion $Q$ is also TRUE.
- Hence the argument is **logically valid** (tautologically true). ∎

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Detecting a Fallacy (Undistributed Middle)

**Given:**
- **Premise 1:** All cats are mammals.
- **Premise 2:** All dogs are mammals.
- **Conclusion:** Therefore, all cats are dogs. ❌

**Step 1 — Identify terms:** S = cats, P = dogs, M = mammals.

**Step 2 — Test against R2 (Middle term must be distributed):**
- In P1: "All cats are mammals" — M (mammals) is the **predicate** of an A-form proposition.
- In an A-form, **only the subject is distributed**; the predicate is **NOT distributed**.
- So M is **not distributed in P1**.
- In P2: "All dogs are mammals" — similarly, M is the predicate, **not distributed**.

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** M is distributed **ZERO times** across the premises → violates **Rule 2** → **Fallacy of the Undistributed Middle**.

The conclusion is **invalid** (though may coincidentally be true/false in reality — here it is false, but the *form* is the problem).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How to Solve Any KTU Syllogism Question

| Step | Action | Marks Weight (Typical KTU 14-Mark) |
|:---:|---|:---:|
| **1** | Identify the three terms (S, P, M). | 1 mark |
| **2** | Translate each premise into A/E/I/O form. | 2 marks |
| **3** | Identify the Figure (1, 2, 3, or 4). | 1 mark |
| **4** | Identify the Mood (e.g., AII, EIO). | 1 mark |
| **5** | Check all 8 rules systematically. | 6 marks |
| **6** | State the conclusion (or its absence). | 1 mark |
| **7** | Justify by Venn diagram or formal proof. | 2 marks |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — The Full Derivation Chain (Inference Engine Style)

Consider an expert system rule base:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rule 1: } &\text{IF } \text{patient has\_fever}(x) \text{ AND } \text{cough}(x) \text{ THEN } \text{possible\_flu}(x)\\
\text{Rule 2: } &\text{IF } \text{possible\_flu}(x) \text{ THEN } \text{recommend\_rest}(x)\\
\text{Fact 1: } &\text{has\_fever(Alice)}\\
\text{Fact 2: } &\text{cough(Alice)}\\
\hline
\text{Goal: } &\text{recommend\_rest(Alice)} \; \text{?}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation steps (forward chaining):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: } \text{has\_fever(Alice)} \land \text{cough(Alice)} \quad &\text{(Facts 1 and 2)}\\
&\text{Step 2: } \text{possible\_flu(Alice)} \quad &\text{(Modus Ponens on Rule 1)}\\
&\text{Step 3: } \text{recommend\_rest(Alice)} \quad &\text{(Modus Ponens on Rule 2)}\\
\end{aligned}
$$

Conclusion reached after **3 deductive steps**. ∎
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Architecture of a Deductive Inference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Knowledge Base: Premises and Rules] --> B[Inference Engine: Deductive Engine]
    F[Facts and Observations] --> B
    B --> C{Apply Inference Rule}
    C -->|Modus Ponens| D[Derive Intermediate Conclusion]
    C -->|Modus Tollens| E[Derive Negated Premise]
    C -->|Hypothetical Syllogism| G[Chain Implications]
    D --> H[Validate against 8 Rules of Syllogism]
    E --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Is Conclusion Valid?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Output Sound Conclusion]
    I -->|No| K[Flag as Fallacy: Reject]
    J --> L[Expert System Decision: Action or Diagnosis]
    K --> M[Trigger Backward Chaining or Abduction]
```

---

### 4.2 The Square of Opposition — Logical Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[A: Universal Affirmative] ---|Contradictory| O[O: Particular Negative]
    E[E: Universal Negative] ---|Contradictory| I[I: Particular Affirmative]
    A ---|Contrary| E
    I ---|Subcontrary| O
    A -->|Subalternation| I
    E -->|Subalternation| O
```

---

### 4.3 The 3-Step Flow of a Syllogism (Major, Minor, Conclusion)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Major Premise: General Truth about M and P] --> SYL[Syllogistic Reasoning Engine]
    P2[Minor Premise: Specific Truth about S and M] --> SYL
    SYL --> CHECK{Validate against 8 Classical Rules}
    CHECK -->|All Pass| CONCL[Valid Conclusion: Relation between S and P]
    CHECK -->|Any Fail| FALL[Fallacy Detected: Argument Invalid]
```

---

### 4.4 Hierarchy of Deductive Reasoning Levels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1: Atomic Propositions P and Q] --> L2[Level 2: Compound Propositions via Connectives]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: Categorical Forms: A, E, I, O]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4: Syllogisms: 2 Premises to 1 Conclusion]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5: Chains of Syllogisms: Polysyllogisms]
    L5 --> L6[Level 6: Expert System Rule Inference]
```
