topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT522 - EXPERT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classical Probability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Classical Probability** (also called **A Priori Probability** or **Theoretical Probability**) is the branch of probability theory that deals with experiments whose outcomes are **equally likely** and whose sample space is **finite and well-defined**. It was formalized by **Pierre-Simon Laplace** and represents the foundational deterministic counting approach used in expert systems for reasoning under uncertainty when prior knowledge of the distribution is available.

Mathematically, if an experiment has a finite sample space $S$ containing $n$ equally likely elementary outcomes, and an event $A$ consists of $m$ of these outcomes, then:

$$P(A) = \frac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Total number of possible outcomes}} = \frac{m}{n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECMT522, Module 3):** Classical probability forms the deterministic backbone of expert systems. It is the building block on which **Bayesian inference engines**, **certainty factor models**, and **belief networks** (e.g., MYCIN, PROSPECTOR) are constructed. Any KTU question on Module 3 typically starts with classical probability before moving to Bayes' theorem.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Random Experiment** | An experiment whose outcome cannot be predicted with certainty (e.g., tossing a coin). |
| **Sample Space ($S$)** | The set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment. |
| **Event ($A$)** | A subset of the sample space; one or more outcomes of interest. |
| **Equally Likely Outcomes** | Outcomes that have the same chance of occurring (symmetry-based assumption). |
| **Mutually Exclusive Events** | Two events $A$ and $B$ that cannot occur simultaneously ($A \cap B = \emptyset$). |
| **Exhaustive Events** | A set of events whose union equals the entire sample space. |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Lottery Ticket Model:**
> Imagine a transparent box containing **10 lottery tickets** numbered 1 through 10, all mixed uniformly. If the box is "fair" (each ticket equally likely to be drawn), and you buy **one** ticket, your chance of winning is exactly $\frac{1}{10}$. This intuitive "counting favorable over total" logic is the heart of classical probability. An expert system in medical diagnosis uses the same logic: out of 1000 patients with similar symptoms, 700 had disease $X$ — therefore the classical prior probability of disease $X$ given those symptoms is $0.7$.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- The **probability of any event** is always bounded: $\mathbf{0 \leq P(A) \leq 1}$.
- The **sum of probabilities of all elementary outcomes** in $S$ is always $\mathbf{1}$.
- Probability is a **dimensionless quantity** (no units).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Classical Probability Works

The classical approach to probability rests on **three fundamental pillars** (Laplace's Postulates):

1. **Finite Sample Space:** The experiment has a definite, countable number of possible outcomes.
2. **Equally Likely Outcomes:** Each elementary outcome has the same theoretical chance of occurring (this is the *a priori* assumption — based on symmetry).
3. **Combinatorial Countability:** Favorable outcomes can be counted using **permutation** and **combination** formulas.

**The "Why" Behind the Formula:**

- If all $n$ outcomes in $S$ are equally likely, each outcome carries probability $\frac{1}{n}$.
- An event $A$ containing $m$ outcomes is the *union* of $m$ such equally likely elementary outcomes.
- By the **additivity rule for mutually exclusive outcomes**, $P(A) = m \cdot \frac{1}{n} = \frac{m}{n}$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every formula here is a potential 1- to 3-mark KTU question.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Classical Probability | $P(A) = \dfrac{m}{n}$ | $n$ = total outcomes, $m$ = favorable |
| 2 | Certain Event | $P(S) = 1$ | Sample space is certain |
| 3 | Impossible Event | $P(\emptyset) = 0$ | Null set has zero probability |
| 4 | Complement Rule | $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$ | $A^c$ = "not $A$" |
| 5 | Addition Rule (Mutually Exclusive) | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ | Only if $A \cap B = \emptyset$ |
| 6 | General Addition Rule | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ | Always valid |
| 7 | Conditional Probability | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$, $\;P(B) > 0$ | Probability of $A$ given $B$ has occurred |
| 8 | Multiplication Rule | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$ | Chain rule, always valid |
| 9 | Independence Test | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | Required for independence |
| 10 | Permutation (ordered) | ${}^{n}P_{r} = \dfrac{n!}{(n-r)!}$ | Order matters |
| 11 | Combination (unordered) | ${}^{n}C_{r} = \dfrac{n!}{r!\,(n-r)!}$ | Order does not matter |
| 12 | Total Probability Theorem | $P(A) = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)$ | $\{B_i\}$ partitions $S$ |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Expert Systems

In expert systems, classical probability powers several key subsystems:

- **Knowledge Base Initialization:** Prior probabilities of diseases/symptoms are computed using classical counting on training datasets.
- **Rule-Based Inference:** Forward-chaining engines evaluate $P(\text{Hypothesis} \mid \text{Evidence})$ using prior counts.
- **Pattern Recognition Modules:** Naive Bayes classifiers (a direct extension of classical probability) categorize inputs based on conditional probability tables.
- **Uncertainty Propagation:** MYCIN-style expert systems use classical probability to compute certainty factors.

### 2.4 The Three Axioms of Probability (Kolmogorov, 1933)

A function $P$ is a valid probability measure if and only if:

1. **Non-negativity:** $P(A) \geq 0$ for every event $A$.
2. **Normalization:** $P(S) = 1$ for the entire sample space.
3. **Countable Additivity:** If $A_1, A_2, \ldots$ are pairwise mutually exclusive, then
$$P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Classical probability is **a special case** of the Kolmogorov axiomatic system restricted to finite, equally-likely sample spaces.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Classical Probability from First Principles

**Step 1 — Start with the equally likely assumption.**
Let the sample space $S = \{e_1, e_2, e_3, \ldots, e_n\}$ contain $n$ elementary outcomes, each equally likely. By symmetry, the probability assigned to each elementary outcome is some constant $c$.

**Step 2 — Apply Axiom 2 (Normalization).**
Since $S$ is the certain event, the sum of probabilities of all elementary outcomes must equal 1:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(\{e_i\}) = n \cdot c = 1$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the constant $c$.**

$$c = \frac{1}{n}$$

**Step 4 — Express event $A$ as a union of elementary outcomes.**
Let event $A = \{e_{i_1}, e_{i_2}, \ldots, e_{i_m}\}$ contain $m$ elementary outcomes. By countable additivity (Axiom 3):

$$P(A) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} P(\{e_{i_j}\}) = m \cdot \frac{1}{n}$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form.**

$$\boxed{P(A) = \frac{m}{n}}$$

This is the **classical definition of probability**, rigorously derived from the three Kolmogorov axioms.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Coin Tossing (Direct Application)

**Problem:** A fair coin is tossed 3 times. Find the probability of getting exactly 2 heads.

**Step 1 — Identify the sample space.**
Each toss has 2 outcomes ($\{H, T\}$), and there are 3 tosses. Total outcomes:
$$n = 2^3 = 8$$

The full sample space is:
$$S = \{HHH, HHT, HTH, HTT, THH, THT, TTH, TTT\}$$

**Step 2 — Identify favorable outcomes for "exactly 2 heads".**
$$A = \{HHT, HTH, THH\} \implies m = 3$$

**Step 3 — Apply the classical formula.**

$$P(A) = \frac{m}{n} = \frac{3}{8}$$

**Step 4 — Verification using the binomial distribution.**

$$P(\text{exactly } 2H) = {}^{3}C_{2} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{1} = 3 \cdot \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{3}{8} \;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Combinatorial Application (Drawing Cards)

**Problem:** From a well-shuffled standard deck of 52 cards, 5 cards are drawn. Find the probability that exactly 3 are hearts.

**Step 1 — Total number of 5-card hands (unrestricted).**

$$n = {}^{52}C_{5} = \frac{52!}{5! \cdot 47!} = 2\,598\,960$$

**Step 2 — Favorable outcomes: choose 3 hearts from 13, and 2 non-hearts from 39.**

$$m = {}^{13}C_{3} \times {}^{39}C_{2}$$

Compute each term:

$${}^{13}C_{3} = \frac{13!}{3! \cdot 10!} = \frac{13 \times 12 \times 11}{3 \times 2 \times 1} = 286$$

$${}^{39}C_{2} = \frac{39!}{2! \cdot 37!} = \frac{39 \times 38}{2} = 741$$

$$m = 286 \times 741 = 211\,926$$

**Step 3 — Final probability.**

$$P(\text{exactly 3 hearts}) = \frac{211\,926}{2\,598\,960} = \frac{211\,926}{2\,598\,960}$$

**Step 4 — Simplification.**

$$P = \frac{211\,926}{2\,598\,960} = \frac{211\,926 \div 858}{2\,598\,960 \div 858} = \frac{247}{3028} \approx 0.0816$$

$$\boxed{P(\text{exactly 3 hearts}) \approx 0.0816 \;\;(\approx 8.16\%)}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Conditional Probability (Two Dice)

**Problem:** Two fair dice are rolled. Given that the sum is 8, find the probability that one of the dice shows a 3.

**Step 1 — Sample space of two dice.**
Total outcomes: $n = 6 \times 6 = 36$.

**Step 2 — Define the two events.**
- Let $A$ = "the sum is 8".
- Let $B$ = "at least one die shows 3".

Outcomes for $A$ (sum = 8):

$$A = \{(2,6),\ (3,5),\ (4,4),\ (5,3),\ (6,2)\} \implies |A| = 5$$

Outcomes for $B$ (at least one 3):

$$B = \{(3,1),\ (3,2),\ (3,3),\ (3,4),\ (3,5),\ (3,6),\ (1,3),\ (2,3),\ (4,3),\ (5,3),\ (6,3)\} \implies |B| = 11$$

**Step 3 — Intersection $A \cap B$.**
The outcomes where the sum is 8 **and** at least one die is 3:

$$A \cap B = \{(3,5),\ (5,3)\} \implies |A \cap B| = 2$$

**Step 4 — Compute classical probabilities.**

$$P(A) = \frac{5}{36}, \quad P(A \cap B) = \frac{2}{36} = \frac{1}{18}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the conditional probability formula.**

$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)} = \frac{\frac{2}{36}}{\frac{5}{36}} = \frac{2}{5}$$

$$\boxed{P(B \mid A) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Theorem of Total Probability (Three-Drawer Problem)

**Problem:** An office has 3 drawers. Drawer 1 contains 5 red and 5 white pens. Drawer 2 contains 4 red and 6 white. Drawer 3 contains 3 red and 7 white. A drawer is chosen at random and a pen is drawn. Find the probability that the pen is red.

**Step 1 — Define the partitioning events.**
- $B_1$: Drawer 1 is chosen. $P(B_1) = \frac{1}{3}$.
- $B_2$: Drawer 2 is chosen. $P(B_2) = \frac{1}{3}$.
- $B_3$: Drawer 3 is chosen. $P(B_3) = \frac{1}{3}$.

**Step 2 — Conditional probabilities of drawing red.**

$$P(R \mid B_1) = \frac{5}{10} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$P(R \mid B_2) = \frac{4}{10} = \frac{2}{5}$$

$$P(R \mid B_3) = \frac{3}{10}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Total Probability Theorem.**

$$P(R) = P(B_1)\,P(R \mid B_1) + P(B_2)\,P(R \mid B_2) + P(B_3)\,P(R \mid B_3)$$

$$P(R) = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{2}{5} + \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{10}$$

**Step 4 — Common denominator (LCM of 2, 5, 10 = 10).**

$$P(R) = \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{5}{10} + \frac{4}{10} + \frac{3}{10}\right) = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{12}{10} = \frac{12}{30} = \frac{2}{5}$$

$$\boxed{P(R) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4}$$

---

### 3.6 Quick-Reference Summary Table

| Example Type | Key Combinatorial Tool | Final Answer Format |
|---|---|---|
| Coin / Die tossing | Power rule: $n^k$ | Improper fraction or decimal |
| Card drawing | ${}^{n}C_{r}$ combinations | Decimal rounded to 4 places |
| Conditional probability | Ratio of intersections | Simple fraction |
| Total probability | Sum of products of $P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)$ | Reduced fraction |

The following Mermaid tree diagram enumerates the **sample space** of tossing two fair coins sequentially, where H = Heads and T = Tails. Each leaf node is an equally likely elementary outcome with probability $\frac{1}{4}$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define classical probability. State the conditions under which the classical definition of probability is applicable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Classical probability (also called *a priori* probability) is the ratio of the number of favorable outcomes to the total number of equally likely outcomes in the sample space. If an event $A$ has $m$ favorable outcomes out of $n$ total equally likely outcomes, then:

$$P(A) = \frac{m}{n}$$

**Conditions (1.5 marks):** The classical definition is applicable when:
1. The sample space $S$ is **finite**.
2. All elementary outcomes in $S$ are **equally likely** (symmetry-based assumption).
3. The event $A$ is a well-defined subset of $S$.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the three axioms of probability given by Kolmogorov.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Let $S$ be a sample space and $P$ be a probability function. Then $P$ must satisfy:

**Axiom 1 — Non-negativity (1 mark):** For any event $A$,
$$P(A) \geq 0$$

**Axiom 2 — Normalization (1 mark):** The probability of the entire sample space is unity,
$$P(S) = 1$$

**Axiom 3 — Countable Additivity (1 mark):** For any countable collection of pairwise mutually exclusive events $A_1, A_2, A_3, \ldots$,
$$P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$$

These axioms provide the rigorous mathematical foundation upon which classical, empirical, and subjective probability are all built.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Students forget to **enumerate the sample space fully** before applying $P(A) = m/n$. KTU examiners deduct 2–3 marks for vague or incomplete sample space listings.
> - **Common mistake #2:** In conditional probability problems, students **swap the condition** — writing $P(A \mid B)$ when the problem actually asks for $P(B \mid A)$. This alone costs 4–5 marks.
> - **Common mistake #3:** In the Theorem of Total Probability, students **fail to verify that the events form a partition** of the sample space (mutually exclusive + exhaustive). KTU specifically tests this in Module 3.
> - **Common mistake #4:** Mixing up permutations (${}^{n}P_{r}$) with combinations (${}^{n}C_{r}$). If the problem says "in how many ways can a *team* be selected" — order does **not** matter — use ${}^{n}C_{r}$. If the problem says "in how many ways can a *rank* be assigned" — order **matters** — use ${}^{n}P_{r}$.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Define the following terms with a suitable example for each: **(7 marks)**
   (i) Sample space and event
   (ii) Mutually exclusive and exhaustive events
   (iii) Conditional probability and independence of events

**(b)** A bag contains 8 red balls and 4 white balls. Three balls are drawn one by one **without replacement**. Find the probability that: **(7 marks)**
   (i) All three balls are red.
   (ii) Exactly two balls are red.
   (iii) At least one ball is white.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions (7 marks):**

**(i) Sample Space and Event (2.5 marks):**
The **sample space** $S$ is the set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment. An **event** is any subset of the sample space, including the empty set and $S$ itself.

*Example:* When a die is rolled, $S = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$. The event "even number" $A = \{2, 4, 6\}$.

**(ii) Mutually Exclusive and Exhaustive Events (2.5 marks):**
Two events $A$ and $B$ are **mutually exclusive** if they cannot occur simultaneously, i.e., $A \cap B = \emptyset$. A set of events is **exhaustive** if their union equals the entire sample space $S$, i.e., $A_1 \cup A_2 \cup \ldots \cup A_n = S$.

*Example:* In a single die roll, $A$ = "outcome is even" and $B$ = "outcome is odd" are mutually exclusive **and** exhaustive (together with the partition $\{2,4,6\}$ and $\{1,3,5\}$).

**(iii) Conditional Probability and Independence (2 marks):**
The **conditional probability** of $A$ given $B$ (with $P(B) > 0$) is:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

Two events $A$ and $B$ are **independent** if and only if the occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the other, i.e., $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$, equivalently $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$.

*Example:* Tossing two fair coins — the outcome of the first toss does not affect the second, so they are independent.

---

**(b) Probability Calculation (7 marks):**

**Total balls = 12 (8 red, 4 white). Total ways to draw 3 balls without replacement:**

$$n = {}^{12}C_{3} = \frac{12!}{3! \cdot 9!} = \frac{12 \times 11 \times 10}{6} = 220$$

**(i) All three are red (2 marks):**

$$m_1 = {}^{8}C_{3} = \frac{8 \times 7 \times 6}{6} = 56$$

$$P(\text{all 3 red}) = \frac{56}{220} = \frac{14}{55}$$

**(ii) Exactly two red (2 marks):**
Choose 2 red from 8 **and** 1 white from 4:

$$m_2 = {}^{8}C_{2} \times {}^{4}C_{1} = 28 \times 4 = 112$$

$$P(\text{exactly 2 red}) = \frac{112}{220} = \frac{28}{55}$$

**(iii) At least one white (3 marks):**
Use the complement rule: $P(\text{at least 1 white}) = 1 - P(\text{no white}) = 1 - P(\text{all 3 red})$.

$$P(\text{at least 1 white}) = 1 - \frac{14}{55} = \frac{55 - 14}{55} = \frac{41}{55}$$

**Final Answers:**
$$P(\text{all 3 red}) = \frac{14}{55}, \quad P(\text{exactly 2 red}) = \frac{28}{55}, \quad P(\text{at least 1 white}) = \frac{41}{55}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** State and prove the **Theorem of Total Probability**. Under what conditions is it applicable? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a factory, three machines $M_1$, $M_2$, $M_3$ produce 30%, 45%, and 25% of the total output respectively. The defect rates of these machines are 2%, 3%, and 2% respectively. A product is selected at random and found to be defective. **(7 marks)**
   (i) Find the probability that it came from machine $M_1$.
   (ii) Find the probability that it came from machine $M_2$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theorem of Total Probability (7 marks):**

**Statement (2 marks):**
If $\{B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n\}$ is a finite partition of the sample space $S$ (i.e., the $B_i$ are pairwise mutually exclusive, exhaustive, and $P(B_i) > 0$ for all $i$), then for any event $A$ in $S$:

$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)$$

**Proof (3 marks):**
Since $\{B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n\}$ is a partition of $S$, we have:

$$A = A \cap S = A \cap (B_1 \cup B_2 \cup \ldots \cup B_n) = (A \cap B_1) \cup (A \cap B_2) \cup \ldots \cup (A \cap B_n)$$

The sets $A \cap B_1, A \cap B_2, \ldots, A \cap B_n$ are pairwise mutually exclusive (since the $B_i$ are mutually exclusive). Applying Axiom 3 (countable additivity):

$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \cap B_i)$$

Using the multiplication rule, $P(A \cap B_i) = P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)$. Substituting:

$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Conditions (2 marks):** The theorem is applicable when:
1. The events $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n$ are **mutually exclusive** ($B_i \cap B_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$).
2. The events are **exhaustive** ($B_1 \cup B_2 \cup \ldots \cup B_n = S$).
3. Each $P(B_i) > 0$ (to avoid division by zero in conditional probability).

---

**(b) Defective Product Problem (7 marks):**

**Given data (1 mark):**
- $P(M_1) = 0.30$, $P(M_2) = 0.45$, $P(M_3) = 0.25$.
- $P(D \mid M_1) = 0.02$, $P(D \mid M_2) = 0.03$, $P(D \mid M_3) = 0.02$, where $D$ = "defective".

**Step 1 — Total probability of a defective product (1 mark):**

$$P(D) = P(M_1) \cdot P(D \mid M_1) + P(M_2) \cdot P(D \mid M_2) + P(M_3) \cdot P(D \mid M_3)$$

$$P(D) = (0.30)(0.02) + (0.45)(0.03) + (0.25)(0.02)$$

$$P(D) = 0.0060 + 0.0135 + 0.0050 = 0.0245$$

**(i) Probability that defective came from $M_1$ (2.5 marks):**
Using Bayes' theorem:

$$P(M_1 \mid D) = \frac{P(M_1) \cdot P(D \mid M_1)}{P(D)} = \frac{0.30 \times 0.02}{0.0245} = \frac{0.0060}{0.0245} = \frac{60}{245} = \frac{12}{49}$$

$$P(M_1 \mid D) \approx 0.2449$$

**(ii) Probability that defective came from $M_2$ (2.5 marks):**

$$P(M_2 \mid D) = \frac{P(M_2) \cdot P(D \mid M_2)}{P(D)} = \frac{0.45 \times 0.03}{0.0245} = \frac{0.0135}{0.0245} = \frac{135}{245} = \frac{27}{49}$$

$$P(M_2 \mid D) \approx 0.5510$$

**Final Answers:**
$$P(M_1 \mid D) = \frac{12}{49} \approx 0.2449, \quad P(M_2 \mid D) = \frac{27}{49} \approx 0.5510$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $P(M_1 \mid D) + P(M_2 \mid D) + P(M_3 \mid D) = 1$ (within rounding). This confirms the three posterior probabilities form a valid probability distribution.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Classical Probability (Module 3, PECMT522):**

- **Definition:** $P(A) = \dfrac{\text{favorable outcomes}}{\text{total equally likely outcomes}} = \dfrac{m}{n}$. Always verify equally-likely and finite sample space.

- **Range:** $0 \leq P(A) \leq 1$ for all events $A$. $P(S) = 1$, $P(\emptyset) = 0$.

- **Complement Rule:** $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$ — extremely useful for "at least one" type problems.

- **Addition Rule (general):** $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$. Reduces to simple addition when $A, B$ are mutually exclusive.

- **Conditional Probability:** $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$, defined only when $P(B) > 0$.

- **Independence:** $A$ and $B$ are independent iff $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ — equivalently, $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$.

- **Total Probability Theorem:** $P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)$ — requires $\{B_i\}$ to be a partition of $S$ (mutually exclusive + exhaustive).

- **Combinatorics:** ${}^{n}P_{r} = \dfrac{n!}{(n-r)!}$ (ordered) vs. ${}^{n}C_{r} = \dfrac{n!}{r!(n-r)!}$ (unordered). Choose based on whether **order matters**.

- **Kolmogorov's Three Axioms:** Non-negativity, Normalization ($P(S) = 1$), and Countable Additivity — these are the foundation of all probability.

- **Expert System Context:** Classical probability initializes the **knowledge base priors**, supports **certainty factors**, and feeds **Bayesian inference engines** (e.g., Naive Bayes, MYCIN, PROSPECTOR).

- **Common Pitfalls:** Swapping conditionals, forgetting to verify partition conditions, confusing permutations with combinations, omitting the sample space enumeration step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experimental and Subjective Probabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT522 - EXPERT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Experimental and Subjective Probabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Probability in Expert Systems?

In the context of **Expert Systems (PECMT522)**, probability is the mathematical language used to express **uncertainty, belief, and reasoning under incomplete information**. Since expert systems routinely deal with knowledge that is uncertain, approximate, or based on heuristics, the concept of probability becomes the cornerstone for inference engines (e.g., Bayesian Networks, Certainty Factor models, Dempster-Shafer theory).

Within Module 3, probability is broadly classified into three schools of thought:

1. **Classical (A Priori) Probability** – based on equally likely outcomes.
2. **Experimental / Empirical (A Posteriori) Probability** – based on observed data.
3. **Subjective (Personalistic) Probability** – based on expert judgment and belief.

This note focuses on the **latter two**, which are critical for handling real-world, non-idealized expert system problems where data may be scarce, repeated trials impossible, or outcomes not equally likely.

---

### 1.2 Experimental Probability (Empirical / A Posteriori Probability)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> *Experimental Probability* of an event $E$ is defined as the **limiting value of the relative frequency** of occurrence of that event when a large number of independent trials (or experiments) are conducted under identical conditions.

Mathematically:

$$P(E) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{f}{n}$$

where $f$ is the number of times event $E$ occurs in $n$ trials. For a finite sample, the approximation used is:

$$P(E) \approx \frac{f}{n}$$

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a weather analyst in Kerala during monsoon season. You cannot compute the probability of rainfall on a particular day using classical probability (outcomes are not equally likely). Instead, you observe rainfall data for the past **30 years** and count how many days it rained in August. The ratio **rainy days / total days observed** gives you the **experimental probability** of rain. This probability is **objective** because it is derived from actual observed data, not personal opinion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** Experimental probability is **objective**, **repeatable**, and converges to the true probability as the number of trials increases (Law of Large Numbers). It forms the empirical backbone of many **machine learning** training datasets used inside expert systems.

---

### 1.3 Subjective Probability (Personalistic / Bayesian Probability)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> *Subjective Probability* is the **degree of belief** that a rational, informed individual (or domain expert) assigns to the occurrence of an event, based on available evidence, personal experience, intuition, and judgment — **without relying on repeated trials or equally likely outcomes**.

It is often denoted as:

$$P(E \mid \text{evidence})$$

and is the foundation of the **Bayesian interpretation of probability** used heavily in modern expert systems like MYCIN's successors, diagnostic systems, and belief networks.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Consider a senior cardiologist diagnosing a patient. Even before ordering tests, the doctor may say, *"I am 80% confident this is a myocardial infarction based on the chest pain pattern and ECG."* This **80%** is a **subjective probability** — it is not derived from rolling dice or counting past patients in a controlled trial, but from the **expert's personal conviction** grounded in years of training. Two different cardiologists may assign slightly different subjective probabilities, and that is acceptable because the measure quantifies *personal belief*, not a universal truth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** Subjective probability is **personal, context-dependent, and can be updated** as new evidence arrives (this update mechanism is called **Bayesian Updating** — formally studied in Module 3 of your syllabus).

---

### 1.4 Quick Comparison Table

| Aspect | Experimental Probability | Subjective Probability |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Observed data from repeated trials | Expert belief, judgment, intuition |
| **Objectivity** | Objective | Subjective / personal |
| **Repeatability** | Requires repeatable experiment | No experiment needed |
| **Sample Size Dependence** | Strong dependence on $n$ | No sample size needed |
| **Convergence** | Converges to true $P(E)$ as $n \to \infty$ | Can be revised with new evidence |
| **Typical Use in Expert Systems** | Statistical learning modules | Rule-based belief systems, Bayesian networks |
| **Mathematical Form** | $P(E) = f/n$ | $P(E) =$ Degree of belief $\in [0,1]$ |

---

### 1.5 Why Are These Two Important for Expert Systems?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real-world expert systems (medical diagnosis, fault detection, financial forecasting), **classical equally-likely-outcome probability fails** because:
> - Outcomes are rarely equally likely.
> - Experiments cannot be repeated infinitely.
> - Data may be incomplete or unavailable.

Hence expert systems rely on **experimental probability** (when historical data exists) and **subjective probability** (when only an expert's opinion is available). Together, they enable the system to perform **reasoning under uncertainty**, which is the heart of PECMT522.

---

### 1.6 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Probability Range:** $0 \leq P(E) \leq 1$ (where **0** = impossibility, **1** = certainty).
- **Odds Form:** $\text{Odds}(E) = \dfrac{P(E)}{1 - P(E)}$
- **Expected Frequency in $n$ trials:** $\mathbb{E}[f] = n \cdot P(E)$
- **Standard Confidence Threshold in Expert Systems:** $P \geq \mathbf{0.5}$ (commonly used trigger threshold for rule activation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Experimental Probability

The experimental school of probability rests on three pillars:

1. **Relative Frequency Definition** – the relative frequency of an event stabilizes around a fixed value as the number of trials grows.
2. **Law of Large Numbers (LLN)** – mathematically justifies why $f/n \to P(E)$ as $n \to \infty$.
3. **Statistical Regularity** – assumes the underlying process is stationary (same conditions across trials).

#### 2.1.1 Relative Frequency Approximation

For a finite but sufficiently large number of trials:

$$P(E) \approx \frac{f}{n}$$

The **error margin (statistical fluctuation)** is approximated by the standard error:

$$SE = \sqrt{\frac{P(E)\cdot(1-P(E))}{n}}$$

> As $n$ increases, $SE \to 0$, which means our estimate becomes more reliable.

#### 2.1.2 The Law of Large Numbers (LLN)

The **Weak Law of Large Numbers** states:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P\left(\left\vert \frac{f}{n} - P(E) \right\vert < \epsilon \right) = 1, \quad \text{for any } \epsilon > 0$$

In simple words: as the number of trials becomes very large, the experimental probability gets **arbitrarily close** to the true probability with **overwhelming confidence**.

#### 2.1.3 Properties

- $0 \leq P(E) \leq 1$
- $P(\text{certain event}) = 1$
- $P(\text{impossible event}) = 0$
- If $A$ and $B$ are **mutually exclusive**, then $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$.
- The **complement rule**: $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$.

---

### 2.2 Theoretical Foundation of Subjective Probability

Subjective probability belongs to the **Bayesian / Personalistic school** of probability. The axioms it satisfies (Kolmogorov's axioms) are the same as classical probability, but the **assignment** of probability is done by a **rational agent** based on evidence and belief.

#### 2.2.1 Axioms of Subjective Probability (De Finetti's Framework)

A rational agent's subjective beliefs $P$ must satisfy:

1. $0 \leq P(E) \leq 1$ for every event $E$.
2. $P(\text{sure event}) = 1$.
3. $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$.

#### 2.2.2 Subjective Probability and Bayesian Updating

The core mechanism that makes subjective probability powerful in expert systems is **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

where:
- $P(H)$ = **Prior** subjective probability (belief before evidence).
- $P(H \mid E)$ = **Posterior** subjective probability (revised belief after evidence $E$).
- $P(E \mid H)$ = Likelihood of observing $E$ if $H$ is true.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** When an expert says *"Initially, I am 60% sure, but the test came positive, so now I am 90% sure"*, this transition from 60% → 90% is **Bayesian updating** of a subjective probability.

#### 2.2.3 Odds Form (Useful for Expert Systems)

$$\text{Posterior Odds} = \text{Likelihood Ratio} \times \text{Prior Odds}$$

$$\frac{P(H \mid E)}{P(\neg H \mid E)} = \frac{P(E \mid H)}{P(E \mid \neg H)} \cdot \frac{P(H)}{P(\neg H)}$$

This **odds-likelihood form** is computationally efficient and is used in production expert systems.

#### 2.2.4 Properties of Subjective Probability

- Two experts may legitimately assign **different** subjective probabilities to the same event.
- Subjective probability is **coherent** if it obeys the laws of probability (no Dutch book can be made against the bettor).
- Subjective probability can be **elicited** using techniques like:
  * Direct probability assessment
  * Lottery method
  * Betting odds method
  * Confidence-rating scales

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Experimental Probability | Application of Subjective Probability |
|---|---|---|
| **Medical Diagnosis (MYCIN, CADUCEUS)** | Disease prevalence from hospital records | Doctor's belief in diagnosis before tests |
| **Weather Forecasting** | Historical rainfall data analysis | Meteorologist's intuition on cloud patterns |
| **Financial Expert Systems** | Historical stock volatility | Analyst's judgment on market sentiment |
| **Industrial Fault Diagnosis** | Failure rate from sensor logs | Engineer's assessment of unusual noise |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Accident rates from driving data | Designer's belief in edge-case safety |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **all key formulas** for this topic — these are the formulas you are expected to know for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Experimental Probability (Finite) | $P(E) = \dfrac{f}{n}$ | $0 \leq f \leq n$, $n > 0$ |
| 2 | Experimental Probability (Limiting) | $P(E) = \lim\limits_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{f}{n}$ | Theoretical / asymptotic form |
| 3 | Standard Error of Estimate | $SE = \sqrt{\dfrac{P(E)(1-P(E))}{n}}$ | Used to quantify uncertainty |
| 4 | Complement Rule | $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$ | $A^c$ is the complement event |
| 5 | Addition Rule (Mutually Exclusive) | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ | $A \cap B = \emptyset$ |
| 6 | Addition Rule (General) | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ | Any two events |
| 7 | Subjective Probability Range | $0 \leq P_{\text{subj}}(E) \leq 1$ | Bounds for any assignment |
| 8 | Odds Form | $\text{Odds}(E) = \dfrac{P(E)}{1-P(E)}$ | $P(E) \in (0,1)$ |
| 9 | Bayesian Update | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Foundation of subjective revision |
| 10 | Posterior Odds | $\dfrac{P(H \mid E)}{P(\neg H \mid E)} = LR \cdot \dfrac{P(H)}{P(\neg H)}$ | $LR = \dfrac{P(E \mid H)}{P(E \mid \neg H)}$ |
| 11 | Expected Frequency | $\mathbb{E}[f] = n \cdot P(E)$ | Useful in reliability engineering |
| 12 | Law of Large Numbers (Weak) | $\lim\limits_{n \to \infty} P\!\left(\left\vert \tfrac{f}{n} - P(E) \right\vert < \epsilon\right) = 1$ | $\epsilon > 0$ |
| 13 | Variance of Frequency | $\text{Var}(f) = n \cdot P(E) \cdot (1-P(E))$ | For binomial setting |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Relative Frequency to Limiting Probability

We now formally derive the **experimental probability** by passing from finite samples to the asymptotic limit.

**Step 1 — Define the empirical setup.**
Consider an experiment that can be repeated $n$ times under identical conditions. Let $f$ be the number of times the event $E$ occurs. The **empirical relative frequency** of $E$ is:

$$R_n(E) = \frac{f}{n}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that $R_n(E)$ is a random variable.**
For finite $n$, $R_n(E)$ fluctuates from experiment to experiment. Its expected value equals the true probability:

$$\mathbb{E}[R_n(E)] = P(E)$$

This follows from the fact that $f \sim \text{Binomial}(n, P(E))$ and $\mathbb{E}[f] = nP(E)$, hence $\mathbb{E}[R_n] = \mathbb{E}[f]/n = P(E)$.

**Step 3 — Compute the variance of $R_n(E)$.**
Using $\text{Var}(f) = nP(E)(1-P(E))$:

$$\text{Var}(R_n) = \frac{\text{Var}(f)}{n^2} = \frac{nP(E)(1-P(E))}{n^2} = \frac{P(E)(1-P(E))}{n}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Chebyshev's Inequality.**
For any $\epsilon > 0$:

$$P\!\left(\left\vert R_n - P(E) \right\vert \geq \epsilon\right) \leq \frac{\text{Var}(R_n)}{\epsilon^2} = \frac{P(E)(1-P(E))}{n\epsilon^2}$$

**Step 5 — Take the limit as $n \to \infty$.**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P\!\left(\left\vert R_n - P(E) \right\vert \geq \epsilon\right) \leq \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{P(E)(1-P(E))}{n\epsilon^2} = 0$$

**Step 6 — Conclude the (Weak) Law of Large Numbers.**

$$\boxed{\lim_{n \to \infty} P\!\left(\left\vert \frac{f}{n} - P(E) \right\vert < \epsilon\right) = 1}$$

This is the **mathematical justification** for using $\dfrac{f}{n}$ as the experimental probability.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Convergence of Experimental Probability

**Problem:** A coin is suspected to be biased. We toss it 1000 times and record the frequency of "Heads". Show how experimental probability stabilizes as the number of trials increases.

**Step 1 — Define cumulative data.**
Suppose after $n$ tosses, the number of heads observed is $f_n$. We compute $P_n = f_n/n$.

**Step 2 — Generate sample data (simulated sequence).**

| Number of Tosses ($n$) | Heads Observed ($f_n$) | Experimental Probability $P_n = f_n/n$ | Theoretical (fair coin) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 6 | 0.6000 | 0.5000 |
| 50 | 27 | 0.5400 | 0.5000 |
| 100 | 53 | 0.5300 | 0.5000 |
| 250 | 128 | 0.5120 | 0.5000 |
| 500 | 247 | 0.4940 | 0.5000 |
| 1000 | 503 | 0.5030 | 0.5000 |

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
As $n$ increases from 10 to 1000, the experimental probability converges from 0.6000 → 0.5030, getting closer to the true theoretical value 0.5000. This is a **direct demonstration of the Law of Large Numbers** in action.

**Step 4 — Compute the standard error at $n = 1000$.**

$$SE = \sqrt{\frac{P(1-P)}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.5 \times 0.5}{1000}} = \sqrt{0.00025} \approx 0.0158$$

So with 95% confidence, the true $P(\text{Head})$ lies in:

$$0.5030 \pm 1.96 \times 0.0158 = [0.4720,\ 0.5340]$$

This interval contains 0.5, so we cannot reject the hypothesis that the coin is fair.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Subjective Probability & Bayesian Updating

**Problem:** A doctor is 60% sure a patient has Disease $D$ based on symptoms alone. A test for $D$ has the following characteristics:
- $P(\text{Positive} \mid D) = 0.90$ (sensitivity)
- $P(\text{Positive} \mid \neg D) = 0.10$ (false positive rate)

The patient tests positive. Find the doctor's new (posterior) belief that the patient has $D$.

**Step 1 — Identify the prior subjective probability.**

$$P(D) = 0.60, \quad P(\neg D) = 0.40$$

**Step 2 — Identify the likelihoods.**

$$P(E \mid D) = 0.90, \quad P(E \mid \neg D) = 0.10$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total probability of a positive test, $P(E)$.**

$$P(E) = P(E \mid D)P(D) + P(E \mid \neg D)P(\neg D)$$

$$P(E) = (0.90 \times 0.60) + (0.10 \times 0.40) = 0.54 + 0.04 = 0.58$$

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' Theorem to update the subjective belief.**

$$P(D \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid D) \cdot P(D)}{P(E)} = \frac{0.90 \times 0.60}{0.58} = \frac{0.54}{0.58} \approx 0.9310$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
The doctor's subjective belief **revised from 60% to approximately 93.10%** after observing the positive test. This is the **Bayesian update** of a subjective probability — a fundamental operation in expert systems.

**Step 6 — Verification using the odds-likelihood form.**

$$\text{Prior Odds} = \frac{0.60}{0.40} = 1.5$$

$$\text{Likelihood Ratio} = \frac{P(E \mid D)}{P(E \mid \neg D)} = \frac{0.90}{0.10} = 9$$

$$\text{Posterior Odds} = 9 \times 1.5 = 13.5$$

$$P(D \mid E) = \frac{13.5}{1 + 13.5} = \frac{13.5}{14.5} \approx 0.9310 \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Criterion | Experimental Probability | Subjective Probability |
|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical Basis** | Frequentist limit of relative frequency | Bayesian degree of belief |
| **Data Requirement** | Requires large observed sample | No data needed; only expert opinion |
| **Uniqueness** | Unique (same for all observers) | **Not unique** — varies with expert |
| **Update Mechanism** | Re-run experiment with more data | Apply **Bayes' Theorem** |
| **Computational Cost** | Low (simple counting) | Moderate (requires expert elicitation) |
| **Bias** | Sampling bias possible | Cognitive / anchoring bias possible |
| **Use in Expert Systems** | Statistical ML modules | Rule-based inference, BN engines |
| **When to Prefer** | When historical / sensor data is abundant | When data is scarce, ambiguous, or unique |
| **Example** | Defect rate in factory from logs | Engineer's confidence in a novel fault |
| **Limitation** | Needs stationary conditions | Hard to validate / standardize |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Probability in Expert Systems

The following block-level functional architecture flow maps the three schools of probability used in expert systems and shows where **experimental** and **subjective** probability fit in.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PROBABILITY IN EXPERT SYSTEMS"]:::root
    B["CLASSICAL A PRIORI"]:::classical
    C["EXPERIMENTAL A POSTERIORI"]:::experimental
    D["SUBJECTIVE PERSONALISTIC"]:::subjective

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D

    B --> B1["Equally likely outcomes"]
    B --> B2["P(E) = m / n"]
    B --> B3["Coin toss, dice, cards"]

    C --> C1["Observed data from trials"]
    C --> C2["P(E) approx f / n"]
    C --> C3["Converges via LLN"]
    C --> C4["Historical records, sensor logs"]

    D --> D1["Expert belief and judgment"]
    D --> D2["P(E) = Degree of belief"]
    D --> D3["Updated via Bayes Theorem"]
    D --> D4["Doctor diagnosis, fault intuition"]

    C4 --> E["EXPERT SYSTEM INFERENCE"]
    D4 --> E

    E --> F["DECISION SUPPORT OUTPUT"]
    E --> G["PROBABILISTIC REASONING"]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef classical fill:#dbe5f1,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2545
    classDef experimental fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#5a4500
    classDef subjective fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#274e13
```

---

### 4.2 Bayesian Updating Flow for Subjective Probability

The following diagram illustrates how a subjective probability is **dynamically revised** as new evidence enters the expert system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["PRIOR BELIEF P(H)"]:::prior
    E1["EVIDENCE E1 ARRIVES"]:::evidence
    B1["BAYES UPDATE 1"]:::update
    P1["POSTERIOR P(H | E1)"]:::posterior
    E2["EVIDENCE E2 ARRIVES"]:::evidence
    B2["BAYES UPDATE 2"]:::update
    P2["POSTERIOR P(H | E1, E2)"]:::posterior
    DEC["EXPERT SYSTEM DECISION"]:::decision

    P0 --> E1 --> B1 --> P1 --> E2 --> B2 --> P2 --> DEC

    P0 -.- NOTE1["Initial subjective belief from expert"]:::note
    P1 -.- NOTE2["Revised belief after E1"]:::note
    P2 -.- NOTE3["Final belief after E1, E2"]:::note

    classDef prior fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c65911,color:#5a2a0c
    classDef evidence fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#0b2545
    classDef update fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#5a4500
    classDef posterior fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#274e13
    classDef decision fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
    classDef note fill:#f2f2f2,stroke:#7f7f7f,color:#404040,stroke-dasharray:3 3
```

---

### 4.3 Convergence Behaviour of Experimental Probability (LLN)

The following block diagram visualises how the experimental probability estimate **stabilises** as the number of trials $n$ grows.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START EXPERIMENT"]:::start
    B["N TRIALS COMPLETED"]:::step
    C["COMPUTE FN DIVIDED BY N"]:::step
    D["DISTANCE FROM TRUE P GREATER THAN EPSILON"]:::check
    E["P INCREASE N AND REPEAT"]:::loop
    F["EXPERIMENTAL PROBABILITY CONVERGED"]:::end
    G["OUTPUT P EXPERIMENTAL"]:::output

    A --> B --> C --> D
    D -- YES --> E --> B
    D -- NO --> F --> G

    classDef start fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
    classDef step fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#0b2545
    classDef check fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#5a4500
    classDef loop fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c65911,color:#5a2a0c
    classDef end fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#274e13
    classDef output fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.4 Mapping to Expert System Inference Engine

The following architecture shows how both probability types are integrated inside an expert system's reasoning loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    KB["KNOWLEDGE BASE"]:::kb
    HIST["HISTORICAL DATA"]:::data
    EXP["DOMAIN EXPERT"]:::expert

    HIST --> EP["EXPERIMENTAL PROBABILITY MODULE"]
    EXP --> SP["SUBJECTIVE PROBABILITY MODULE"]

    EP --> FUS["FUSION AND BAYESIAN ENGINE"]
    SP --> FUS

    KB --> FUS

    FUS --> INF["INFERENCE ENGINE"]
    INF --> OUT["RECOMMENDATION WITH CONFIDENCE"]
    INF --> EXPL["EXPLANATION FACILITY"]

    classDef kb fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
    classDef data fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#0b2545
    classDef expert fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c65911,color:#5a2a0c
    classDef fuse fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#5a4500
    classDef engine fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#274e13
    classDef out fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Experimental Probability and Subjective Probability. Give one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Experimental Probability | Subjective Probability |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Repeated trials / observed data | Personal belief / expert judgment |
| Objectivity | Objective — independent of the observer | Subjective — varies from person to person |
| Computation | $P(E) = f/n$ | $P(E) =$ Degree of belief |
| Example (1) | A factory finds 12 defective bulbs in a lot of 200, so $P(\text{defect}) = 12/200 = 0.06$ | A senior doctor says, *"I am 75% sure the patient has dengue"* based on symptoms |

(1 mark for table/definition, 1 mark for example of experimental, 1 mark for example of subjective.)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the Law of Large Numbers. How does it justify the use of relative frequency as an estimate of probability?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Weak Law of Large Numbers** states that for any $\epsilon > 0$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P\!\left(\left\vert \frac{f}{n} - P(E) \right\vert < \epsilon\right) = 1$$

This means that as the number of trials $n$ grows, the **relative frequency $f/n$ converges to the true probability $P(E)$** with probability 1. Hence $f/n$ can be used as a reliable estimate of $P(E)$ when $n$ is large. (2 marks for statement + 1 mark for justification of relative frequency.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Define **Experimental Probability** and **Subjective Probability**. Explain with examples how each is applied in an expert system for medical diagnosis. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A diagnostic expert system must decide whether a patient has Disease $X$. An expert initially assigns prior $P(X) = 0.30$. A lab test returns positive with the following characteristics: $P(\text{Positive} \mid X) = 0.85$ and $P(\text{Positive} \mid \neg X) = 0.08$. Using **Bayes' Theorem**, compute the posterior probability $P(X \mid \text{Positive})$. Interpret the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition of Experimental Probability (1.5 marks):**
Experimental probability (also called *empirical* or *a posteriori* probability) of an event $E$ is the **relative frequency** of occurrence of $E$ in a large number of independent, identically conducted trials:

$$P(E) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{f}{n} \approx \frac{f}{n} \quad \text{(for finite } n \text{)}$$

where $f$ is the frequency of $E$ in $n$ trials.

**Definition of Subjective Probability (1.5 marks):**
Subjective probability is the **degree of belief** assigned to an event by a rational individual based on personal experience, evidence, and judgment, without requiring repeated trials:

$$P(E) = \text{Degree of belief that } E \text{ will occur}, \quad P(E) \in [0,1]$$

**Application in Medical Diagnosis Expert Systems (4 marks):**

| Probability Type | Application Example |
|---|---|
| **Experimental** | The expert system analyses **hospital records** of the last 10 years and finds that out of 5000 patients with chest pain, 850 were diagnosed with myocardial infarction. The system uses $P(\text{MI}) \approx 850/5000 = 0.17$ as the **base-rate prevalence**. |
| **Subjective** | A senior cardiologist interacting with the expert system may declare, *"Based on this patient's ST-segment elevation, I am 90% sure of an acute MI"*. The expert system uses this as a **prior belief** in its Bayesian network. |
| **Combined (Bayesian Update)** | The system combines the experimental base-rate (prior) with the doctor's subjective assessment (likelihood from experience) and test results to compute a **posterior probability** that drives the final recommendation. |

This dual use of experimental and subjective probability makes the medical expert system robust when either data alone or expert opinion alone would be insufficient.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify the prior probabilities (1 mark).**

$$P(X) = 0.30, \quad P(\neg X) = 0.70$$

**Step 2 — Identify the likelihoods (1 mark).**

$$P(\text{Positive} \mid X) = 0.85, \quad P(\text{Positive} \mid \neg X) = 0.08$$

**Step 3 — Apply the law of total probability (2 marks).**

$$P(\text{Positive}) = P(\text{Positive} \mid X) \cdot P(X) + P(\text{Positive} \mid \neg X) \cdot P(\neg X)$$

$$P(\text{Positive}) = (0.85 \times 0.30) + (0.08 \times 0.70) = 0.255 + 0.056 = 0.311$$

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' Theorem (2 marks).**

$$P(X \mid \text{Positive}) = \frac{P(\text{Positive} \mid X) \cdot P(X)}{P(\text{Positive})} = \frac{0.85 \times 0.30}{0.311} = \frac{0.255}{0.311} \approx 0.8199$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark).**
After observing a positive test, the expert system's belief in Disease $X$ **increased from 30% to approximately 82%**. The subjective prior has been updated by the experimental evidence (test result), demonstrating the **Bayesian fusion** of experimental and subjective probabilities in an expert system.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **relative frequency definition of probability**. State and prove (using Chebyshev's inequality) the **Weak Law of Large Numbers**. Discuss its significance in expert systems that rely on statistical learning. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing expert system monitors 800 machine cycles. Out of these, 56 cycles trigger a fault alarm. Compute the **experimental probability of a fault** and the **standard error** of the estimate. If a new expert claims a **subjective probability of 0.05** for the same fault (which is lower than the empirical value), discuss why the system should prefer the experimental estimate in this case. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Relative Frequency Definition (2 marks):**
The relative frequency definition states that if an experiment is repeated $n$ times under identical conditions, and the event $E$ occurs $f$ times, then the **probability of $E$** is defined as the **limiting value** of the ratio $f/n$ as $n \to \infty$:

$$P(E) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{f}{n}$$

**Statement of Weak Law of Large Numbers (1 mark):**
For any $\epsilon > 0$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P\!\left(\left\vert \frac{f}{n} - P(E) \right\vert < \epsilon\right) = 1$$

**Proof using Chebyshev's Inequality (3 marks):**

Let $R_n = f/n$ be the empirical relative frequency. Since $f \sim \text{Binomial}(n, P(E))$:

$$\mathbb{E}[R_n] = P(E), \quad \text{Var}(R_n) = \frac{P(E)(1-P(E))}{n}$$

By **Chebyshev's inequality**, for any $\epsilon > 0$:

$$P\!\left(\left\vert R_n - P(E) \right\vert \geq \epsilon\right) \leq \frac{\text{Var}(R_n)}{\epsilon^2} = \frac{P(E)(1-P(E))}{n\epsilon^2}$$

Taking the limit $n \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P\!\left(\left\vert R_n - P(E) \right\vert \geq \epsilon\right) \leq \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{P(E)(1-P(E))}{n\epsilon^2} = 0$$

Hence:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P\!\left(\left\vert R_n - P(E) \right\vert < \epsilon\right) = 1 \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Significance in Expert Systems (1 mark):**
In expert systems using statistical learning, the LLN guarantees that the **empirical error rate converges to the true error rate** as the training data grows. This justifies the use of large training datasets in modules like neural expert systems, case-based reasoners, and statistical diagnostic systems.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute the experimental probability (2 marks).**

$$P_{\text{exp}}(\text{Fault}) = \frac{f}{n} = \frac{56}{800} = 0.07$$

**Step 2 — Compute the standard error (2 marks).**

$$SE = \sqrt{\frac{P(1-P)}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.07 \times 0.93}{800}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0651}{800}} = \sqrt{8.1375 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.00902$$

**Step 3 — 95% Confidence interval (1 mark).**

$$0.07 \pm 1.96 \times 0.00902 = [0.0523,\ 0.0877]$$

**Step 4 — Discussion: why prefer experimental estimate? (2 marks).**
The new expert's subjective probability of **0.05** lies **below the lower bound (0.0523)** of the 95% confidence interval around the experimental value 0.07. This means the expert's belief is statistically inconsistent with the observed data at the 5% significance level. The expert system should prefer the **experimental probability** because:
- It is derived from a **large sample (n = 800)**, making it statistically reliable.
- The subjective estimate falls outside the **confidence interval**, indicating it is not supported by evidence.
- In the absence of strong justification for the expert's deviation, the **Law of Large Numbers** guarantees that the empirical estimate is closer to the true fault probability.

Hence the expert system should use $P(\text{Fault}) \approx 0.07$ as its base rate, possibly logging the expert's deviation for further review.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing experimental and classical probability** — many students write the classical formula $m/n$ even when the question clearly says *experimental*. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Forgetting to state the assumptions** of the Law of Large Numbers (independent trials, identical conditions, finite variance). (Lose 1 mark.)
> 3. **In Bayes' Theorem questions**, students often forget to compute $P(E)$ using the **law of total probability** and directly substitute values — leading to wrong denominators. (Lose 2–3 marks.)
> 4. **Mixing up prior, likelihood, and posterior** in subjective probability questions. Memorize: Prior = before evidence; Posterior = after evidence.
> 5. **Not interpreting the final answer** — KTU examiners award 1 mark for *interpretation* in every Bayes question. Never end with just a numerical value.
> 6. **Using invalid probability values** outside $[0,1]$ in the working — automatic loss of coherence mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Experimental and Subjective Probabilities**

- **Experimental Probability** is computed from **observed frequencies** of repeated trials: $P(E) \approx f/n$.
- The **limiting form** $P(E) = \lim_{n \to \infty} f/n$ is the **frequentist** definition of probability.
- The **Law of Large Numbers (LLN)** guarantees that $f/n \to P(E)$ as $n \to \infty$ — provable via **Chebyshev's inequality**.
- **Subjective Probability** is the **degree of belief** of a rational expert, ranging in $[0,1]$.
- Subjective probability satisfies the **same Kolmogorov axioms** as classical probability (coherence requirement).
- **Bayes' Theorem** is the **update rule** for subjective probability: $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) P(H)}{P(E)}$.
- The **prior** is the belief *before* evidence; the **posterior** is the belief *after* evidence.
- The **odds-likelihood form** of Bayes is computationally efficient for expert systems: Posterior Odds = Likelihood Ratio × Prior Odds.
- **Standard error** of experimental probability: $SE = \sqrt{\dfrac{P(1-P)}{n}}$ — shrinks as $n$ grows.
- **Confidence interval**: $P \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot SE$ (use $z = 1.96$ for 95%).
- In expert systems, **experimental probability** is preferred when **large reliable data** exists; **subjective probability** is preferred when **only expert opinion** is available.
- **Bayesian fusion** combines both: the experimental base-rate (prior) + expert's subjective judgment (likelihood) → updated posterior.
- When subjective and experimental probabilities **conflict**, the system should favour the **experimentally derived estimate** if $n$ is large and data is unbiased.
- Subjective probability **elicitation methods**: direct assessment, lottery method, betting odds, confidence scales.
- **Complement rule**: $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$ — always useful in KTU problems.
- **Expected frequency** in $n$ trials: $\mathbb{E}[f] = nP(E)$ — used in reliability engineering.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compound Probability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT522 - EXPERT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compound Probability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Compound Probability** (also known as *Joint Probability* or *Combined Probability*) is the branch of probability theory that calculates the likelihood of the **simultaneous or sequential occurrence of two or more random events** drawn from a given sample space. Formally, it is the probability of the **intersection** of events (for "AND" combinations) or the **union** of events (for "OR" combinations), and it serves as the mathematical backbone for reasoning under uncertainty in expert systems, knowledge engineering, Bayesian inference engines, and fuzzy rule-based decision architectures.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **Expert Systems**, compound probability underpins the *Certainty Factor model* (Shortliffe & Buchanan), *Bayesian belief networks*, and *probabilistic reasoning modules* that allow expert systems to propagate uncertainty through chains of inference rules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In PECMT522 (Expert Systems), Module 3 focuses on *Reasoning with Uncertainty*. Compound Probability is the **prerequisite mathematical foundation** required to understand Bayesian networks, Dempster-Shafer theory, and probabilistic inference in production systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **medical expert system** trying to diagnose whether a patient has a disease **AND** shows a specific symptom. Two things must both be true for the diagnosis to fire:
- Event A: The patient has the disease.
- Event B: The patient shows the symptom.

The compound probability answers: "What is the chance that **BOTH** happen together?" — this is the **intersection** $P(A \cap B)$.

Alternatively, consider a fire-alarm expert system: it should ring if smoke **OR** heat is detected. The compound probability also answers: "What is the chance that **AT LEAST ONE** happens?" — this is the **union** $P(A \cup B)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "AND" vs "OR" Distinction is Critical in Expert Systems:**
> - **AND** logic → Multiply probabilities (if independent) or use conditional probability (if dependent).
> - **OR** logic → Add probabilities, then subtract the overlap to avoid double counting.

## 1.3 Core Metrics and Standard Constants

- **Sample Space $\Omega$** — The set of *all* possible outcomes. $\lvert \Omega \rvert$ is the total number of equally likely elementary events.
- **Probability of any single event**: $0 \leq P(A) \leq 1$ for any event $A \subseteq \Omega$.
- **Total Probability Axiom**: $P(\Omega) = 1$ and $P(\emptyset) = 0$.
- **Frequency Notation in Expert Systems**: Confidence values, certainty factors, and posterior probabilities are all bounded in the unit interval **[0, 1]**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Compound Probability Rules

Compound probability problems decompose into two primary logical operators, each with its own set of rules based on event relationship.

### A. The "AND" Operator (Intersection / Joint Probability)

**Step 1 — Identify Independence or Dependence.**
- *Independent Events*: The occurrence of one event does not affect the probability of the other (e.g., flipping a coin AND rolling a die).
- *Dependent Events*: The occurrence of one event changes the probability of the other (e.g., drawing two cards from a deck **without replacement**).

**Step 2 — Apply the Multiplication Rule.**

For **independent** events $A$ and $B$:
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

For **dependent** events (general multiplication rule):
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$$

**Step 3 — Generalize to $n$ Events.**

For $n$ independent events:
$$P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \cdots \cap A_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i)$$

For $n$ dependent events (chain rule):
$$P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \cdots \cap A_n) = P(A_1) \cdot P(A_2 \mid A_1) \cdot P(A_3 \mid A_1 \cap A_2) \cdots P(A_n \mid A_1 \cap \cdots \cap A_{n-1})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" Matters in Expert Systems:** The chain rule is the mathematical engine behind forward-chaining Bayesian networks — each rule's antecedent updates the conditional probability of the consequent.

### B. The "OR" Operator (Union Probability)

**Step 1 — Check for Mutual Exclusivity.**

Two events are **mutually exclusive** if they cannot occur simultaneously: $A \cap B = \emptyset$, hence $P(A \cap B) = 0$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Addition Rule.**

For **mutually exclusive** events:
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$$

For **non-mutually exclusive** events (general addition rule):
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the Overlap.**
The term $P(A \cap B)$ is subtracted because the intersection was counted twice (once in $P(A)$ and once in $P(B)$). This is the **Inclusion-Exclusion Principle**.

### C. Conditional Probability (The Foundation of Inference)

The probability of event $A$ **given** that event $B$ has occurred:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad \text{provided } P(B) > 0$$

Rearranging gives the foundational form of **Bayes' Theorem**:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

### D. Total Probability Theorem

If $B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n$ form a partition of the sample space (mutually exclusive and exhaustive):
$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)$$

This is the workhorse for handling multi-source evidence in expert systems.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table** — Memorize this for KTU Board Exams.

| # | Rule Name | Formula | Key Condition |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Independent AND | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | Events independent |
| 2 | Dependent AND | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$ | Events dependent |
| 3 | Chain Rule ($n$ events) | $P(\bigcap A_i) = \prod P(A_i \mid \cap_{j<i} A_j)$ | Sequential conditional dependence |
| 4 | Mutually Exclusive OR | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ | $A \cap B = \emptyset$ |
| 5 | General Addition (OR) | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ | Non-mutually exclusive |
| 6 | Complement Rule | $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$ | — |
| 7 | Conditional Probability | $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B) / P(B)$ | $P(B) > 0$ |
| 8 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(A_i \mid B) = P(B \mid A_i)P(A_i) / P(B)$ | Posterior reasoning |
| 9 | Total Probability | $P(A) = \sum_i P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)$ | Partition $\{B_i\}$ |
| 10 | Three-Event AND | $P(A \cap B \cap C) = P(A)P(B)P(C)$ | All three independent |
| 11 | Three-Event OR | $P(A \cup B \cup C) = \sum P(A) - \sum P(A \cap B) + P(A \cap B \cap C)$ | Inclusion-Exclusion |

**Note:** In every formula above, units are *dimensionless probabilities* bounded in $[0, 1]$.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Expert Systems

| Application Domain | Use of Compound Probability |
|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| **MYCIN (Medical Expert System)** | Combines certainty factors of chained evidence |
| **Bayesian Networks** | Forward propagation uses chain rule of conditional probability |
| **Speech Recognition** | Hidden Markov Models use compound joint state-observation probabilities |
| **Fraud Detection Systems** | Combines independent risk signals via joint probability |
| **Weather Forecasting AI** | Combines independent meteorological event probabilities |
| **Autonomous Vehicle Perception** | Sensor fusion via joint probability of multi-sensor events |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Multiplication Rule for Independent Events

We derive the rule $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ for two independent events $A$ and $B$ in a finite equiprobable sample space $\Omega$.

**Step 1: Define the sample space and event cardinalities.**

Let $\lvert \Omega \vert = N$ be the total number of equally likely elementary outcomes. Let $\lvert A \vert = n_A$ and $\lvert B \vert = n_B$ be the number of favorable outcomes for $A$ and $B$ respectively.

**Step 2: Express individual probabilities using the classical definition.**

By the classical (Laplace) definition of probability:
$$P(A) = \frac{n_A}{N}, \qquad P(B) = \frac{n_B}{N}$$

**Step 3: Compute the cardinality of the intersection.**

If $A$ and $B$ are independent, every outcome in $A$ can be paired with every outcome in $B$, so the intersection forms a Cartesian-product structure:
$$\lvert A \cap B \vert = n_A \cdot n_B$$

**Step 4: Apply the classical definition to the intersection.**

$$P(A \cap B) = \frac{\lvert A \cap B \vert}{\lvert \Omega \vert} = \frac{n_A \cdot n_B}{N}$$

**Step 5: Factor the expression.**

$$\frac{n_A \cdot n_B}{N} = \frac{n_A}{N} \cdot \frac{n_B}{N}$$

**Step 6: Substitute the original probabilities.**

$$\frac{n_A}{N} \cdot \frac{n_B}{N} = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

**Final Result:**
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bayes' Theorem from the Definition of Conditional Probability

**Step 1: Write the conditional probability of $A$ given $B$.**

From the definition:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

**Step 2: Solve the equation for the joint probability $P(A \cap B)$.**

Multiplying both sides by $P(B)$:
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$$

**Step 3: Symmetrically write the conditional probability of $B$ given $A$.**

$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$$

**Step 4: Solve for the same joint probability.**

$$P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 5: Equate the two expressions for $P(A \cap B)$.**

$$P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 6: Isolate the desired posterior $P(A \mid B)$.**

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

**Step 7: Expand the denominator using the Total Probability Theorem (optional).**

If $\{B_i\}$ is a partition of $\Omega$:
$$P(A \mid B_j) = \frac{P(B_j \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_j \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)}$$

**Final Result (Bayes' Theorem):**
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: General Addition Rule (Inclusion-Exclusion)

**Step 1: Partition the union into disjoint pieces.**

The set $A \cup B$ can be partitioned as $A \cup (B \setminus A)$, which are disjoint. Therefore:
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B \setminus A)$$

**Step 2: Express $B \setminus A$ as $B$ minus the overlap.**

The set $B \setminus A$ consists of outcomes in $B$ that are not in $A$. By the complement rule applied to $A$ within $B$:
$$P(B \setminus A) = P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

**Step 3: Substitute back.**

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + \left[ P(B) - P(A \cap B) \right]$$

**Step 4: Simplify.**

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Verification for Mutually Exclusive Case:** If $A \cap B = \emptyset$, then $P(A \cap B) = 0$, and the formula reduces to $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ as required. ✓

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A spam filter in an expert system has the following probabilities:
- $P(\text{contains "FREE"}) = 0.4$
- $P(\text{contains "WIN"}) = 0.3$
- $P(\text{both "FREE" and "WIN"}) = 0.15$

**Find:** (i) $P(\text{contains "FREE" AND "WIN"})$ — verify independence.
(ii) $P(\text{contains "FREE" OR "WIN"})$.
(iii) $P(\text{contains neither})$.

---

### Solution (i): Check for Independence

For independence, we require:
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

Compute the right-hand side:
$$P(A) \cdot P(B) = 0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.12$$

Compare with the given joint probability:
$$P(A \cap B) = 0.15$$

Since $0.15 \neq 0.12$, the events are **NOT independent** — they are positively correlated (this is expected since spam keywords co-occur).

---

### Solution (ii): Probability of "FREE" OR "WIN"

Using the general addition rule:
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

Substitute the values:
$$P(A \cup B) = 0.4 + 0.3 - 0.15 = 0.55$$

Therefore, the probability that an email contains at least one of the keywords is **0.55**.

---

### Solution (iii): Probability of Containing Neither

Apply the complement rule with respect to the union:
$$P((A \cup B)^c) = 1 - P(A \cup B)$$

Substitute the result from part (ii):
$$P((A \cup B)^c) = 1 - 0.55 = 0.45$$

Therefore, the probability that an email contains **neither** keyword is **0.45**.

---

### Verification (Closure Property)

Check that the total probability sums to 1:
$$P(A \cap B) + P(A \cap B^c) + P(A^c \cap B) + P(A^c \cap B^c) = 1$$

Compute each disjoint region:
- $P(A \cap B) = 0.15$
- $P(A \cap B^c) = P(A) - P(A \cap B) = 0.4 - 0.15 = 0.25$
- $P(A^c \cap B) = P(B) - P(A \cap B) = 0.3 - 0.15 = 0.15$
- $P(A^c \cap B^c) = 0.45$ (from part iii)

Total: $0.15 + 0.25 + 0.15 + 0.45 = 1.00$ ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Visual Taxonomy of Compound Probability

The following Mermaid diagram maps the conceptual hierarchy of compound probability rules and their interdependencies in the context of expert system reasoning.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compound Probability] --> B[AND Operator Intersection]
    A --> C[OR Operator Union]
    A --> D[Conditional Probability]
    A --> E[Bayes Theorem]

    B --> B1[Independent Events P A B equals P A times P B]
    B --> B2[Dependent Events P A B equals P A times P B given A]
    B --> B3[Chain Rule n events]

    C --> C1[Mutually Exclusive P A union B equals P A plus P B]
    C --> C2[General Addition P A union B equals P A plus P B minus P A intersection B]
    C --> C3[Inclusion Exclusion Principle]

    D --> D1[P A given B equals P A intersection B over P B]
    D --> D2[Total Probability Theorem]

    E --> E1[Posterior equals Likelihood times Prior over Evidence]
    E --> E2[Naive Bayes Classifier]
    E --> E3[Bayesian Belief Networks]

    B3 --> F[Expert System Inference Engine]
    C3 --> F
    D2 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F

    F --> G[Uncertainty Propagation in Rule Chains]
    F --> H[Certainty Factor Combination MYCIN]
    F --> I[Sensor Fusion in Autonomous Systems]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Compound Probability in an Expert System Inference Cycle

The diagram below illustrates how compound probability rules are orchestrated within a typical expert system's reasoning cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Evidence Acquisition Layer]
        E1[Evidence Event A]
        E2[Evidence Event B]
        E3[Evidence Event C]
    end

    subgraph PROC[Compound Probability Engine]
        P1[Independence Checker]
        P2[Multiplication Rule]
        P3[Addition Rule]
        P4[Conditional Probability Module]
        P5[Bayesian Updater]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Inference Decision Layer]
        O1[Certainty Factor]
        O2[Posterior Belief]
        O3[Triggered Rule Conclusion]
    end

    E1 --> P1
    E2 --> P1
    E3 --> P1
    P1 -->|Independent| P2
    P1 -->|Dependent| P4
    P2 --> P5
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Venn Diagram Representation (Conceptual Block Schematic)

The following schematic abstracts a Venn diagram to show the four disjoint probability regions formed by two events $A$ and $B$ in a sample space $\Omega$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OMEGA[Sample Space Omega Total Probability equals 1]
        subgraph OUTSIDE[Region 4 A complement intersection B complement equals 1 minus P A union B]
            R4[Complement Region]
        end
        subgraph INSIDE[Region 1 plus Region 2 plus Region 3 equals P A union B]
            R1[Region 1 A only equals P A minus P A intersection B]
            R2[Region 2 A intersection B Joint Region]
            R3[Region 3 B only equals P B minus P A intersection B]
        end
    end

    R1 --- R2
    R2 --- R3
    R4 -.->|Disjoint from| INSIDE
```

**Key Insight for KTU Students:** Every compound probability problem reduces to identifying and computing the area (probability mass) within these four mutually exclusive regions.
